To access the Speed Tables tools, click the [Icon: Speed Tables Tools button](images/speed-table-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar.

You will see this toolbar appear on the top left of the plan view window:

[Screenshot: speed-table.png - Speed Table Tools](images/mode-buttons/speed-table.png)

[[Screenshot: speed-table-new.png - New speed table button](images/tool-buttons/speed-table-new.png)](#newtable)
[[Screenshot: speed-table-edit.png - Speed table properties button](images/tool-buttons/speed-table-edit.png)](#tableproperties)
[[Screenshot: speed-table-delete.png - Delete a speed table button](images/tool-buttons/speed-table-delete.png)](#deletetable)
[Icon: toolbar spacer](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-spacer.png)
[Icon: Close toolbar button](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-close.png)

[Screenshot: speed-table-ex1.png - Example of a raised table on a road](images/speed-table-ex1.png)

This example shows a raised table on a road, with start and end ramps of 2m length, and table height of 10cm.

---

### [Icon: New speed table button](images/tool-buttons/speed-table-new.png) New Speed Table

Select this button to add a new speed table onto a road.

You will be prompted to select a road from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest road centreline is highlighted.
Click to select.
The speed table properties window is shown:

[Screenshot: speed-table-properties-dlg.png - Speed table properties window](images/speed-table-properties-dlg.png)

As soon as the properties window appears, you can start picking a start and end chainage for the speedtable. Please note, you do not have to click **Pick** first.

After you have chosen the start and end chainages, if you wish to alter the values, you can simply type in new values or click the appropriate **Pick** button and visually choose a new chainage from the drawing again.

### Start/End Chainage

This is the road centreline chainage of the bottom of the ramp.
You can use the **Pick** button to select the chainage from the drawing.

### Start/End Ramp Length

The road surface linearly rises to meet the specified *table height* over this ramp length.

### Table Height

This is the vertical thickness of the central part of the raised area of the table (the height of the table above the standard road surface).

### Extend into Secondary Channels

If this option is selected the raised table will extend into the secondary channel instead of stopping at the primary channel.

[Screenshot: speed-table-ex2.png - Example of a raised table extending into a secondary channel](images/speed-table-ex2.png)

Example showing extend into secondary channel **enabled**.

  
[Screenshot: speed-table-ex3.png - Example of a raised table stopping at the primary channel](images/speed-table-ex3.png)

Example showing extend into secondary channel **disabled**.

  
[Screenshot: speed-table-ex4.png - Example of a raised table extending into secondary channel in 3D](images/speed-table-ex4.png)

This example shows what the speed table looks like in 3D with extend into secondary channel enabled.

The speed table includes the secondary channel upstand and the ramps also extend into the secondary channel.

---

### [Icon: Speed table properties](images/tool-buttons/speed-table-edit.png) Speed Table Properties

Select this option to view or modify the properties of a Speed Table.

You will be prompted to select an existing speed table. As you move the cursor over the drawing the closest speed table will highlight with a red border.
Click near the table for which you want to see the properties.
The properties window is shown:

[Screenshot: speed-table-properties-dlg.png - Speed Table properties window](images/speed-table-properties-dlg.png)

You can set any of the properties, as explained above for a [new speed table](#newtable).

---

### [Icon: Delete a speed table button](images/tool-buttons/speed-table-delete.png) Delete a Speed Table

Select this option to remove a speed table from the drawing.

You will be prompted to select an existing speed table. As you move the cursor over the drawing the closest speed table will highlight with a red border.
Click near the table you want to delete and it will disappear.

*Note*: If necessary, the *Undo* facility can be used to put the table back.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Drop Kerbs');
show\_index();
//-->

To access the tools for drop-kerbs, driveways and crossing points click the [Icon: Kerb Tools button](images/kerb-tools-button.png) button.

---

### [Icon: New drop kerb button](images/tool-buttons/drop-kerb-new.png) New Drop Kerb

To create a new drop kerb region click the [Icon: New drop kerb button](images/tool-buttons/drop-kerb-new.png) button.

As you move the mouse over the Plan View you will see that the chainage selector is in operation on the channels.
It is awaiting you to pick the start and end chainage positions on a road channel where you want the dropped part
of your dropped kerb item.

When you have selected it's position, the following window is shown:

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-dlg.png - Drop-kerb properties window](images/drop-kerb-dlg.png)

The preview image shows the plan view of the dropped kerb region extending from top of kerb to back of footpath.

The dimensions of the dropped kerb region can be edited directly on the preview.
This includes the length over which the level changes (up or down) and the length for which the changed level extends.
If you lengthen or shorten the dropped kerb region directly on the preview, it's length will be adjusted equally either
side of the middle of the item.

When the option for Full Path Depth is deselected you can enter how far back the grading extends from the channel into the footpath.

### Centreline Chainage

The centreline chainage represents the mid-point along the dropped kerb feature. The dropped kerb extends equally along the kerbline either side of this chainage.
You can respecify the chainage using the **Pick** button if required. This will then allow you to pick the start and end chainages of the drop kerb.

### Kerb Upstand

The height of the kerb upstand determines the amount of drop of the kerb from the original kerb height. The kerb upstand is the height of the dropped kerb above the channel. If zero, then the kerb will be dropped flush with the channel at the edge of the carriageway.
Normally, a small upstand is desirable to prevent water flowing off the carriageway onto the footpath.

### Left and Right side Drop Kerbs

By default the drop kerb feature is added to both sides of the road, as is common for a pedestrian crossing.
However, if you are designing a residential driveway you may only want it on one side of the road. This can be
done by simply unticking **Add to both sides of the road**.

### Full Path Depth

By default the dropped kerb feature grades all the way from the kerb line to the back of the footpath.
However, by deselecting the **Full Path Depth** option you can specify the distance back from the kerb line over which the grading occurs.

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-ex1.png - Example of specifying the path depth for the drop kerb grading](images/drop-kerb-ex1.png)

In this example, the grading occurs over a depth of 1.5m back from the kerb line.

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-3d-ex1.png - Example of full path depth grading](images/drop-kerb-3d-ex1.png)

This example shows the dropped kerb item graded right to the back of the footpath.

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-3d-ex2.png - Example of non-full path depth grading](images/drop-kerb-3d-ex2.png)

This example show the drop kerb grading extending back over about half the footpath width.

### Rectangular Style

By default the drop kerb item is wider at the kerb line than at the back on the footpath, as shown in the above images.

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-ex3.png - Example of rectangular drop kerb](images/drop-kerb-ex3.png)

But, if you select the **Rectangular style** then the width at the back on the footpath will match the full width at the kerb line.

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-3d-ex3.png - 3d example of rectangular drop kerb](images/drop-kerb-3d-ex3.png)

The rectangular style looks like this on the 3D view.

### Perpendicular to Centreline

By default the drop kerb feature extends back perpendicular to the kerb line.

However, sometimes the kerb line does not run parallel to the centreline, for example, where the road is widening, or at a junction kerb return splay. Normally, it is desirable for a pair of crossing points to guide the pedestrian to cross straight over the road (perpendicular to the centreline).

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-3d-ex4.png - A pair of crossing point not perpendicular to centreline](images/drop-kerb-3d-ex4.png)

This example shows a pair of crossing points at a road junction which are **not** perpendicular to the road centreline.
They are aligned relative to the kerb line which is diverging at this point.

[Screenshot: drop-kerb-3d-ex5.png - A pair of crossing points perpendicular to the centreline](images/drop-kerb-3d-ex5.png)

This example shows the same crossing points aligned perpendicular to the road centreline, so as to guide pedestrians straight across the road.

---

### [Icon: Drop kerb properties button](images/tool-buttons/drop-kerb-properties.png) Modify a Drop Kerb

To modify the properties of a drop kerb item click the [Icon: Drop kerb properties button](images/tool-buttons/drop-kerb-properties.png) button.

As you move the cursor over the plan view the nearest drop kerb item will highlight. Click to select.
The same window will be shown as for adding a new drop kerb (as above), but with all the properties filled out.
Make any required changes, as above, and then click the **OK** button.

Whereas when adding a new drop kerb item you can add a pair on both sides of the road at the same time, when modifying you select only one side at a time, allowing each one to have individual properties.

---

### [Icon: Delete a drop kerb button](images/tool-buttons/drop-kerb-delete.png) Delete a Drop Kerb

To delete a drop kerb item click the [Icon: Delete a drop kerb button](images/tool-buttons/drop-kerb-delete.png) button.

As you move the cursor over the plan view the nearest drop kerb item will highlight. Click to delete when the requisite item is highlighted.

*Note*: The **Undo** facility can be used if you change your mind after deleting a drop kerb.

---

//<!--
show\_header('Setting-Out Information');
show\_index();
//-->

The Setting-Out tools are for the easy annotation of road setting-out information.
Setting-out information is usually required on the finished drawings, so that the design can be accurately set-out on-site.

The annotation is dynamic, and as such automatically updates itself when the design items to which it refers are changed. A setting-out table is therefore never out-of-date, or requiring regenerating.

Here is an example of a centreline setting-out table displayed next to the road to which it refers:

[Screenshot: setting-out-ex1.png - Example of Centreline Setting-Out information](images/setting-out-ex1.png)

A setting-out table can be generated from any designed item line, here is an example of a pond setting-out table:

[Screenshot: setting-out-designline-ex.png - Example of Design Line Setting-Out information](images/setting-out-designline-ex.png)

### Back of Footway Levels

Back of Footway levels are automatically annotated where a standalone footpath is junctioned with another footpath/footway. If the terminated footpath is narrower than 2m, a single annotation is shown on the back of footway of the joined onto path at the centre of the junction.
If the terminated footpath is 2m or wider, levels are shown on the back of footway of the joined onto path at the edges of the junction. This is demonstrated in the following example:

[Screenshot: footway-levels-example.png - Footway levels on footway path junction](images/footway-levels-example.png)
https://www.site3d.co.uk/help/forward-vi:
Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Forward Visibility along a Road');
show\_index();
//-->

This option allows you to set the properties of a road's forward visibility splay.

On the [Icon: Road Tools button](images/centreline-tools-button.png) centreline tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Forward Visibility button](images/tool-buttons/forward-visibility.png) forward visibility button.

Forward visibility splays indicate the area of land which must be free of visual obstruction around a bend in the road. This ensures a driver can always see the road ahead to a distance within which they could stop the vehicle (to avoid hitting an unexpected hazard).

The distance a driver must be able to see ahead, along the driving path, is called the *stopping sight distance* and it is usually related to the design speed of the road or corner in question.
To maintain a clear line of sight, anything located within the visibility splay should not obscure the driver's ability to see around the corner.

[Screenshot: forward-visibility-example.png - Forward Visibility button](images/forward-visibility-example.png)

The above example shows a visibility splay drawn around the curve of the road.
Each straight blue line represents the direct line of sight, from a driver's position on the road, to the position ahead measured along the driver's path, by which the vehicle could stop if initially travelling at the design speed for the road.

The red line shows the envelope of all the sight lines, and so represents the region of land which must not obstruct the driver's view.

Site3D will automatically recalculate and update the visibility splays as you make other design changes to the road.

To set the visibility splay properties for a road, click the [Icon: Forward Visibility button](images/tool-buttons/forward-visibility.png) forward visibility button. Then select a road from the drawing. The *Forward Visibility* window is shown:

[Screenshot: forward-visibility-dlg.png - Forward Visibility](images/forward-visibility-dlg.png)

This window contains the configurable values for the road's forward visibility splays.

### Interval

The **interval** is the distance between the start of each sight-line.
Sight-lines (to the *Sight Stopping Distance*) are calculated along the driver's path at points separated by this interval distance.
The outer extent of all the sight-lines describes the forward visibility envelope.
To get a smoother forward visibility envelope, reduce the interval value.

*Note*: an **interval** value must be positive and the value **0.0** will turn off the forward visibility splays for the selected road.

### Offset from Channel

The **offset from channel** is the parallel distance from the channel for the driver's path along which the forward visibility splays are calculated.
Some authorities require the drivers' path to be mid-way across the driving lane.
Other authorities require it to be a set distance from the channel regardless of the lane width.

### Sight Stopping Distance

The **sight stopping distance** is the distance along the designated drivers' path for which there must be a clear line of sight.
The required **sight stopping distance** is usually related to the road's design speed.

*Note*: This is not the line-of-sight distance, but the distance along the driver's path (i.e. the distance over which the driver should be able to brake the vehicle to a halt after seeing a hazard ahead).

### Eye Height

The **eye height** is the vertical distance above road of the driver's expected eye level.

### Target Height

The **target height** is the vertical distance above the road at the target location (at the *Sight Stopping Distance* further along the road).

The height is usually set to either the height of a toddler or to the height of a fallen toddler or other such important object that the driver may need to stop before or avoid.

---

#### See Also:

* [Contents](index.htm)

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Centreline Extras');
show\_index();
//-->

These options are available from the Road menu, in Centreline Extras.

[Screenshot: centreline-extras.png - Centreline Extras Menu](images/centreline-extras.png)

### Level To Surface

This allows you to set the vertical alignment of a centreline to match the current survey surface.

As shown in the menu you can access this command in a plan view by using the shortcut **Shift+L**.

After picking this option, select a centreline from the plan view. You should see the following message:

[Screenshot: level-to-surface-warning.png - Warning that vertical alignment will be lost](images/level-to-surface-warning.png)

This message is bringing to your attention that the Level To Surface command will change the vertical alignment of the selected centreline, which will remove any previous alignment.

Click **Ok** to continue and level the centreline to the survey surface.

If you click **Cancel** the operation will be cancelled and the alignment of the centreline will remain unchanged.

### Level To Plane

This allows you to set the vertical alignment of a centreline such that it sits on a plane that you specify.

After picking this option, select a centreline from the plan view.

[Screenshot: level-to-plane-picking.png - Picking the points that form the plane](images/level-to-plane-picking.png)

The mouse will change to a crosshair selection cursor for you to select three locations in the plan view. These three points will form a triangle which will be be the basis of the planar surface you are aligning to, based on the Z level of the points.

After selecting three points you should see the following message:

[Screenshot: level-to-surface-warning.png - Warning that vertical alignment will be lost](images/level-to-surface-warning.png)

This message is bringing to your attention that the Level To Plane command will change the vertical alignment of the selected centreline, which will remove any previous alignment.

You should then see the following window allowing you to fine tune the points which define the plane you will level the centreline to:

[Screenshot: level-to-plane-dlg.png - Specifying the points and gradient of the plane](images/level-to-plane-dlg.png)

You can tweak the points which define the plane using the vertical bars or fine tune them by manually entering the levels/offsets from the ground surface.

The arrow at the centre of the triangle shows the gradient of the plane.

The **Lock All Three** option allows you to lock the relative levels of the points that define the plane. This means that while this option is on, any changes to the levels will update the other points to maintain the gradient of the plane. Changing the gradient will not be affected by this option.

A **Manual Gradient** can be specified which will scale the gradient whilst keeping the existing gradient direction.

Click **Ok** to level the centreline to the specified plane.

If you click **Cancel** the operation will be cancelled and the alignment of the centreline will remain unchanged.

### Set Start Chainage

This option allows you to modify the chainage that the centreline starts at.

The chainage along the centreline will be recalculated from the new centreline start chainage.

[Screenshot: set-start-chainage.png - Set Start Chainage Window](images/set-start-chainage.png)

You can input the new centreline start chainage here.

Click **Ok** to apply the new centreline start chainage.

### Reverse Centreline Chainage

This option allows you to select a centreline to reverse the chainage direction and have the chainage start from the opposite end of the centreline.

By default the forward chainage direction is based on the order that you create the points when making a new centreline.
Or, when using the **Select Line** option in the right click menu, you choose the forward chainage direction after
selecting a polyline to create a new centreline from. [See the new road help page for more information](centreline-new.htm).

Below is an example of a road which has been added with the chainage going from the right to the left.
This is indicated by the label position ("Road 2") on the right hand side of the image:

[Screenshot: centreline-reverse-chainage-ex1.png - Reverse Chainage Example - Before](images/centreline-reverse-chainage-ex1.png)

After using the reverse centreline chainage command on this centreline the chainage starts at the junction and increases from left to right:

[Screenshot: centreline-reverse-chainage-ex2.png - Reverse Chainage Example - After](images/centreline-reverse-chainage-ex2.png)

The road design and any attached junctions and design items will stay in the same position they were added.
All of the attached design items will have their design chainages updated to match the new centreline chainages.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Centreline Truncate');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility truncates a road from the start or end to a given chainage.

This facility can be used at any time during the design process.
Site3D will work out all the knock-on effects and update your site accordingly.

[Screenshot: centreline-menu-truncate.png - Road menu showing Truncate selection](images/centreline-menu-truncate.png)

To use the centreline truncate facility click the **Road** menu at the top of the screen and select the **Truncate...** option.

[Screenshot: centreline-truncate-chainage-tracker.png - Centreline Chainage To Truncate](images/centreline-truncate-chainage-tracker.png)

The mouse will change to a chainage tracker and highlights the centreline that will be selected when you click.

The following window will appear after you have selected a centreline.

[Screenshot: centreline-truncate.png - Centreline Truncate window](images/centreline-truncate.png)

### Centreline

This lets you select which centreline to do the truncate operation on. By default this will be the centreline that was initially selected.

### Chainage

This lets you specify the chainage that the centreline should be truncated to. By default this will be the chainage that was shown when you selected the centreline.

### Direction

By default, the direction will be selected based on which end of the centreline was closest to the chainage that was chosen.

From the Start will truncate to the chosen chainage distance from the start of the centreline.

From the End will truncate to the chosen chainage distance from the end of the centreline.

//<!--
show\_header('Centreline Parallel');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility moves a road or standalone footpath centreline parallel.

This facility can be used at any time during the design process.
Site3D will work out all the knock-on effects and update your site accordingly.

[Screenshot: centreline-parallel-road-menu.png - Road menu showing Parallel selection](images/centreline-parallel-road-menu.png)

To use the parallel facility click the **Road** menu at the top of the screen.

Select the **Parallel...** option.

Select a road or standalone footpath centreline (they will highlight as you move the mouse near) that you wish to move parallel.

The following window asking for the direction and offset distance will appear.

[Screenshot: centreline-parallel-dlg.png - Centreline Parallel window](images/centreline-parallel-dlg.png)

### Direction

Choose which side of the selected road or standalone footpath the operation should be performed on.

### Distance

Type in a distance with which the road or standalone footpath centreline will move parallel by.

### Create a Copy

By default, with **create a copy** unticked, the operation will move the selected centreline parallel by the chosen distance.

By choosing the **create a copy** option, the selected centreline will be unchanged and a parallel copy will be created at the
specified distance.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Road Interfacing');
show\_index();
//-->

To create interfacing along a road, or to reset the interfacing along the full length of a road, first click the [Icon: Interface Tools button](images/interface-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar and then the [Icon: New Interfacing button](images/tool-buttons/interface-new.png) button on the [Interface Options toolbar](interface.htm).

You will be prompted to select a road from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest road centreline is highlighted.
Click to select.
The interface properties window is shown.

[Screenshot: interface-dlg.png - Interfacing Window](images/interface-dlg.png)

*Note*: If the road already has interfacing then the existing interface properties are shown in the window, ready to be modified.

At its simplest, the interfacing can be specified with just a single straight grade for the batter slope.
This is shown in the example window above. The default slope is set at 1:3, and the checkboxes are selected to mirror the slope up/down as well as left/right.

This simple interfacing can be seen in the following example image:

[Screenshot: interface-ex1.png - Example of interfacing along the edge of a new road](images/interface-ex1.png)

### Mirror Cut/Fill

By deselecting the **Mirror Cut/Fill** checkbox, you can specify a different downward slope to the upward slope.

[Screenshot: interface-mirror-down-ex.png - example with non- mirror up/down](images/interface-mirror-down-ex.png)

The *upward* slope is used when the existing surface is above the designed surface and therefore the slope must extend upward to meet the existing ground.

The *downward* slope is used when the existing surface is below the designed surface and therefore the slope must extend downwards to meet the existing ground.

[Screenshot: interface-ex2.png - Example of interfacing with both cutting and embankment](images/interface-ex2.png)

In this example, the immediate foreground section of the road is in cutting, with the interfacing sloping upwards from the back of footpath.

As the road passes over the ditch in the mid-ground and the existing surface drops away, the interfacing slopes downwards from the back of footpath to form an embankment.

Where the road emerges out of the ditch it goes back into cutting, with the upwards interfacing again being in effect.

### Mirror Right

By deselecting the **Mirror Left/Right** checkbox, you can specify the interfacing slope on the right-hand side of the road to be different to the slope on the left-hand side.

[Screenshot: interface-mirror-right-ex.png - interface example without left/right mirror](images/interface-mirror-right-ex.png)

### Add Steps

Instead of a single straight slope interfacing, you can insert a series of varying grades to make a stepped interface slope.

When you click the **Add Steps** button it will show the step properties:

[Screenshot: interface-dlg-ex1.png - Interface window with steps](images/interface-dlg-ex1.png)

The step has a fixed length, and can be specified as either a width/height pair, or width and slope, or height and slope.

### No Steps

When steps are present, the **No Steps** button is shown. Clicking the **No Steps** button will remove all the steps, leaving just the single straight slope.

### Multiple Steps

Multi-stepped interfacing can be used to make more complex constructions running alongside the road.

[Screenshot: interface-dlg-ex2.png - Interface window with steps](images/interface-dlg-ex2.png)

In this example, a road-side ditch has been constructed by having steps down, across, and back up, before grading back to the existing ground surface.

[Screenshot: interface-step-ex3.png - Multi-stepped interfacing forming a ditch](images/interface-step-ex3.png)

And it looks like this in 3D.

### Insert / Delete Step

When you click the *Add Steps* button, the steps list is shown, with one step added by default.
To add another step, just click in the next row in the list.

To delete a step, click on it in the list, so that the row highlights, and press the **Delete** key on the keyboard.

Additionally, you can right click the mouse over any step on the list to see the follwowing menu:

[Screenshot: interface-steps-menu.png - interface steps menu](images/interface-steps-menu.png)

You can select to insert a new step *before* the selected step, or *after* the selected step, or you can *delete* the selected step.

### Force

Steps with the **Force** option set are formed regardless of whether the existing ground would have intersected with the interfacing before the final grade.

*Note*: When using **Mirror Cut/Fill** the *forced* steps are not inverted, only the final step which grades back to existing surface is inverted. This can be seen in the above example, with the *forced* steps, drawn in black lines, being the same in the upper and lower preview images, whereas the non-*forced* final slope, drawn in green, go in opposite directions.

[Screenshot: interface-step-ex1.jpg - Example of stepped interfacing](images/interface-step-ex1.jpg)

When **Force** is not selected the step will stop if it intersects the ground surface.

[Screenshot: interface-step-ex2.jpg - Example of stepped interfacing](images/interface-step-ex2.jpg)

However, if the **Force** checkbox is selected the step will continue to the specified length regardless of whether it pierces the ground surface on the way.

In this example, the first step has been specified as a 1.5m slope at a grade of 1:6 which does not stop where it passes through the existing ground.
The final (second) step grades up/down to meet the existing ground at a grade of 1:3.

### Interface to which surface?

By default, the interface slopes will be calculated relative to the current survey surface (i.e. whichever sub-model is set as current survey in the [Layers window](layers-dlg.htm)).

However, you can specify the particular interfacing item's slopes to be relative to any of the imported sub-models, by selecting one from the drop-down list.

This is very useful where some design items need batter slopes which interface with the survey and other design items need batter slopes which interface to another surface.

### All Roads / All Remaining Roads

By default the settings specified on the Interface Properties window will apply to the one selected road.

However, if you choose the **All Roads** checkbox option then when you click **OK** all the roads in the design will be set with the interfacing.

Alternatively, if you choose the **All Remaining Roads** option then the interfacing will be applied to all roads in the design which do not yet have any interfacing set, leaving the interfacing unchanged on those roads which already had it.

### Left/Right Sides

By default the interfacing settings apply to both sides of the selected road.

However, you can deselect the **Left Side** or **Right Side** checkbox to apply the interfacing to only the specified side of the road.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Interface Region');
show\_index();
//-->

To change the interfacing along part of a road, first click the [Icon: Interface Tools button](images/interface-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar and then the [Icon: Interface Region button](images/tool-buttons/interface-region.png) button on the [Interface Options toolbar](interface.htm).

You will be prompted to select an existing interface from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest road interface line is highlighted.
Click to select.

*Note*: You are selecting the interfacing from only one side of the road.

The interface properties window is displayed, showing the properties of the selected interfacing.

[Screenshot: interface-region-dlg.png](images/interface-region-dlg.png)

This window operates in exactly the same way as the [Create Interface](interface-new.htm) window for setting the properties of the interfacing for the new region.

There are the following extra settings:

### Chainage Range

Specify the start chainage and the end chainage for the region of interfacing you want to change.
The interfacing properties which you set on the rest of the window will get applied to this chainage range, with the interfacing unchanged on the remainder of the road.

### Merge Length

Usually where there is a change form one interface style to another you will want it to gradually change from one to the other, rather than have an abrupt level/slope change.

You can specify the length over which the gradual change should occur, both for the start of the region and the end of the region.

[Screenshot: interface-region-ex1.png - Varying interfacing along a road](images/interface-region-ex1.png)

This example shows an interface region with a straight 1:3 slope which has been applied within a more complex interfacing with a ditch.

The change from one style to the other occurs over a distance of 10m, in this instance.

Your interfacing constructions are remembered by the software, so that if the road is subsequently redesigned in horizontal or vertical then the interfacing will be automatically regenerated accordingly.

//<!--
show\_header('Delete Interfacing');
show\_index();
//-->

To remove the interfacing from a side of a road, first click the [Icon: Interface Tools button](images/interface-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar and then the [Icon: Delete Interface button](images/tool-buttons/interface-delete.png) button on the [Interface Options toolbar](interface.htm).

You will be prompted to select an existing interface from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest road interface line is highlighted.
Click to select.

The selected interfacing will be removed from the road and the overall design is automatically regenerated accordingly.

(*Note*: The *Undo* option may be used to restore the design, if you need to.)

Whereas the [Create Interfacing](interface-new.htm) facility does both sides of the road by default, the Delete Interfacing function removes from only one side at a time.

//<!--
show\_header('Earthwork Design Tools');

//-->

The surface shape of an earthwork is defined by a collection of 3D design lines.
The outer design line must be a closed line (complete boundary) and may be interfaced to existing ground.
There are a number of facilities to assist with defining the design lines.

Here is an example of a simple pond, defined with four design lines and interfaced to ground at its edges:

[Screenshot: pond-ex1.png - Example of a pond earthwork](images/pond-ex1.png)

Earthworks comprise of a set of 3D design lines which describe the 3D surface.
There is a range of facilities for designing the 3D lines in both horizonal and vertical.

### [Icon: Start new Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/new-line.png) New Earthwork Line

To create a new earthwork design line click the [Icon: Start new Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/new-line.png) button.

The mouse cursor will be shown as a cross when over the drawing, indicating that you should click the start position for the line.

[Screenshot: earthwork-new-ex1.png - Example of New Earthwork line usage](images/earthwork-new-ex1.png)

After clicking the start point, a line will be shown stretching to the mouse cursor position.
Click the next point to place the line, and continue placing further IPs (Intersection Points) to describe the path of the line.

### New Line Right Click Menu Options

[Screenshot: earthwork-line-menu.png - Earthwork right-click menu](images/earthwork-line-menu.png)

#### Stop Inputting New Line

From the right click menu you can select **Finish** to add the new line or **Cancel** to exit the tool without adding the new line.

#### Enter Accurate Coordinates

To place the points accurately, you can use the various *snap* options on the [snap toolbar](snap-tools.htm), and make temporary construction lines using the [construction line toolbar](construction-tools.htm).

Also, you can right-click the mouse to get a menu where you can select **Enter Coordinate** to type the desired coordinate on the keyboard.

[Screenshot: enter-coord-dlg.png - Window for entering a coordinate via the keyboard](images/enter-coord-dlg.png)

When you have finished placing the IP points for your line path, right click the mouse and select **Finish** from the menu.

#### Closed Loop

You will see that the last point joins to the first point, forming a closed loop. Closed loop is the default setting for earthwork design lines.

You can switch between a *closed line* and a *non-closed line* using the right-click menu.

*Note*: In order for the earthwork feature to have its own surface, you must ensure that the outermost design line is a closed loop.

#### Create From Layout

The **Select Line** option from the right click menu allows you to pick a line from an imported drawing. This will then create a new earthwork line with the same horizontal information.

### [Icon: Add Point into an Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/add-point.png) Add a Point into an Earthwork Line

This option is for modifying the path of an Earthworks design line, by adding one or more IPs (Intersection Points).

Select one of the straight sections between two existing IPs (they will highlight as you move the mouse close).
Then position the new IP at the desired location.

### [Icon: Add Arc into an Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/add-arc.png) Add or Change a Curve on an Earthwork Line

This option is for putting the curves on your Earthworks design line.

Click on an IP point, or existing curve, and you'll see the filleting arc passing through the cursor position.
Move the mouse to resize the arc, and click to place it.

[Screenshot: earthwork-arc-ex1.png - Example of adding a curve on an earthwork line](images/earthwork-arc-ex1.png)

### Radius

You can select an exact radius by right-clicking the mouse and selcting **Enter Radius** from the menu.

[Screenshot: earthwork-radius-menu.png - Earthwork Add Arc right-click menu](images/earthwork-radius-menu.png)
[Screenshot: arc-properties-dlg-no-transitions.png - Earthwork Arc Properties Window](images/arc-properties-dlg-no-transitions.png)

You can enter a specific radius, or select the maximum radius which is shown.
The maximum radius will fillet the angle with a curve stretching all the way to the nearer of the neighbouring IP positions.

### [Icon: Move point on an Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) Move a Point on an Earthwork Line

This option is for moving the path of a earthworks design line, by repositioning one or more of the IPs (Intersection Points).

Select an IP on an earthworks design line (they will highlight as you move the mouse near) and move it to where you want.

### [Icon: Delete point from an Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/delete-point.png) Delete a Point from an Earthwork Line

This option is for changing the path of an Earthwork Line by deleting one or more of the Earthwork Line's IPs (Intersection Points).
If you need to delete a whole design line look at the [Delete an Earthwork Line option](#delete_line).

Move the mouse cursor near to the IP which you want to delete (they will highlight as you move the mouse near) and click to delete.

### [Icon: Delete an Earthworks line button](images/tool-buttons/delete-line.png) Delete an Earthwork Line

This option is for deleting an entire design line from the earthwork.

Select the design line you want to delete (the lines will highlight as you move the mouse cursor near to them).
When you click the mouse you will be asked to confirm the deletion.

After the deletion the earthwork will be updated with the remaining design recalculated accordingly.

If you want to reinstate the deleted line, you can click the [Undo](undo.htm) button.

---

### [Icon: Create parallel design line button](images/tool-buttons/earthwork-create-parallel.png) Create a Design Line Parallel to Another

Ponds and Earthworks often require specific grading between design lines. This tool can help to achieve this by creating a parallel design line.

[Screenshot: earthwork-parallel-ex1.png - earthwork parallel example](images/earthwork-parallel-ex1.png)

Starting with at least one design line on the earthworks, select the [Icon: Create parallel design line button](images/tool-buttons/earthwork-create-parallel.png) button.

[Screenshot: earthwork-parallel-ex2.png - earthwork parallel example](images/earthwork-parallel-ex2.png)

Move the mouse to indicate the offset distance for the parallel line.

*Note*: A specific offset value can be entered by right-clicking the mouse.

[Screenshot: earthwork-parallel-ex3.png - earthwork parallel example](images/earthwork-parallel-ex3.png)

A series of parallel design lines can be used to define a set of stepped grades.

### Parallel Right Click Options

When using the parallel tool [Icon: Create parallel design line button](images/tool-buttons/earthwork-create-parallel.png) you can right click to see other options:

[Screenshot: earthwork-parallel-menu.png - earthwork parallel right-click menu](images/earthwork-parallel-menu.png)

If you select **Manual Offset** you can enter a specific distance to offset the new parallel line by. The offset side and distance will default to match the rubberbanded position before right-clicking.

If you select **Offset By Gradient** you can choose a level/gradient as well as the offset distance. Again, the offset side and distance will default to the rubberbanded position.

[Screenshot: offset-from-gradient-dlg.png - earthwork parallel right-click menu](images/offset-from-gradient-dlg.png)

---

The level of the design lines can be set after their creation using the following options:

### [Icon: Set contour level button](images/tool-buttons/set-level.png) Set Level

#### Set Tank Base Level

With editing a tank, this tool is used to set the level of the bottom surface of the tank. It works the same way as described below for earthworks and ponds.

#### Set Level of a Pond or Earthwork design Line

When editing a pond or earthwork, any of the design lines can be set to a specific level, behaving similarly to a contour line.

When you select this option you will be prompted to select a design line from the screen. The design lines highlight as you move the cursor near. Click to select. You can then enter the desired level for the design line:

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-dlg.png - Earthwork design line level dialog](images/earthwork-level-dlg.png)  
[Screenshot: earthwork-level-ex1.png - Earthwork example before setting level](images/earthwork-level-ex1.png)

This image shows an earthwork with two design lines (and interfaced down to ground at the edge).

Both design lines are currently at the same level. We can change the level of the innner line:

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-ex2.png - Earthwork example after setting level](images/earthwork-level-ex2.png)

This image shows the earthwork after dropping the level of the inner design line.

---

### [Icon: Set tank height](images/tool-buttons/tank-set-height.png) Set the height of a tank

This tool is used to set the height of a tank. This defines the level difference between the bottom and top surfaces of a tank and is determines the maximum storage volume of the tank.

Pressing this button will bring up the Set Tank Height window where you can input the desired tank height.

[Screenshot: tank-set-height-dlg.png - Tank set height dialog](images/tank-set-height-dlg.png)
  

---

### [Icon: Level to Plane button](images/tool-buttons/earthwork-level-to-plane.png) Level a Design Line to a Graded Plane

An earthwork design line can be levelled to a plane at a specified gradient. For example, this can be useful for the base of a pond, so that the water drains in a specific direction.

When you select this option you will be prompted to select a design line from the screen. The design lines highlight as you move the cursor near. Click to select. Then you can specify the required slope:

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-plane-ex1.png - Level to Plane example](images/earthwork-level-plane-ex1.png)

First, specify the direction of the slope (the line of maximum gradient).

You do this by clicking two points to define a the line of maximum gradient for the slope.

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-plane-dlg.png - Level to Plane dialog](images/earthwork-level-plane-dlg.png)

This window is then shown to define the slope gradient.

Give the Z-level for the first point.
For the second point you can either specify its Z-level too, or you can give the specific gradient of the slope.

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-plane-ex2.png - Level to Plane 3d View before](images/earthwork-level-plane-ex2.png)

This image shows the earthwork before the slope grading is applied.
It is the inner design line that will be levelled.

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-plane-ex3.png - Level to Plane 3d View after](images/earthwork-level-plane-ex3.png)

This image shows the earthwork after the inner design line has been levelled to a slope of 1:20.

---

### [Icon: Level to Surface button](images/tool-buttons/level-to-surface.png) Level a Design Line to the Ground Surface

This option can be used to level an earthwork design line so that it sits exactly on the current surface.

When you select this option you will be prompted to select a one of the earthwork design lines from the plan view. The design lines highlight as you move the cursor near. Click to select. The selected line will be instantly levelled onto the current survey ground surface.

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-surface-ex1.png - Level to Ground before](images/earthwork-level-surface-ex1.png)

This image shows an earthwork with two design lines. Both have constant levels all the way round, with the inner line being higher than the outer. Both are above the existing ground surface.

[Screenshot: earthwork-level-surface-ex2.png - Level to Ground after](images/earthwork-level-surface-ex2.png)

This image shows the earthwork after the outer line is levelled to the ground surface.

*Note*: The grade between the inner and outer line will vary all the way around the earthwork. If a constant grade is required then the interfacing function should be used.

---

### [Icon: Longsection Button](images/tool-buttons/vertical-alignment.png) Display the longsection of a design line

After pressing the longsection button on the toolbar, a design line can be picked from the earthwork to open a longsection view of the selected line.

---

### [Icon: Interfacing Button](images/tool-buttons/interface-new.png) Add interfacing to the earthwork

This button opens the interfacing options of an earthwork. The earthwork interfacing options are almost the same as the road interfacing options, except that the earthwork interfacing options only apply to the selected earthwork and an earthwork can only interface outward from the outermost earthwork line.

The interfacing window is covered in depth in the [Road Interfacing help page](interface-new.htm).

---

### [Icon: Split Line Button](images/tool-buttons/earthwork-split-line.png) Split a Design Line

Using the Split Line tool you can cut a closed line into an open line.

---

### [Icon: Earthwork Properties Button](images/tool-buttons/properties.png) Set earthwork properties

Here you can set the properties of the selected earthwork, such as the name, display style, and water level or freeboard of a pond.

If the earthwork type is a standard earthworks or a pond, the earthwork type can be swapped between pond and earthwork.

See the [Earthwork Properties page](earthwork-properties.htm) for more information.

---

### [Icon: Earthwork Contour button](images/tool-buttons/contours.png) Display earthwork contours

Here you can set the automatic contouring for the selected earthwork surface.

See [Automatic Surface Contouring](automatic-surface-contouring.htm) for more information.

---

### [Icon: Move Annotation button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-move.png) Move Earthwork Annotation

After creating an earthwork, the annotation is automatically placed on the plan. The annotation is placed in the largest area of the earthwork. This will also be recalculated after any design change. While this often does a good job at placing the text the text may still overlap other design items on the site or texts on imported drawings.

[Screenshot: earthwork-annotation-pos-auto.png - Automatic Annotation Placement example](images/earthwork-annotation-pos-auto.png)

If you click the Move Annotation button you will highlight the nearest earthwork annotation text, even if it is not the currently selected earthwork. If you click with an annotation selected you will see the new textbox location and a line going from the earthwork to the centre of the box:

[Screenshot: earthwork-annotation-pos-select.png - Annotation Selection example](images/earthwork-annotation-pos-select.png)

If you click again the textbox will be manually positioned to the indicated location:

[Screenshot: earthwork-annotation-pos-manual.png - Manual Annotation example](images/earthwork-annotation-pos-manual.png)

To finish right click and select Quit Command from the right-click menu.

[Screenshot: drainage-annotation-pos-right-click.png - Manual Annotation right click menu](images/drainage-annotation-pos-right-click.png)

To revert back to automatic positioning you can use the Move Annotation tool to select an annotation, then right click and select Use Automatic Position, this will revert the selected annotation back to automatic positioning. Alternatively, you can enter the Move Annotation tool and right click without having an annotation selected. Then, from the right click menu, select Use Automatic Position. This will allow you to select multiple annotations in a row to set them back to automic positioning. When you are done you can right click again and untick Use Automatic Position to return to manual text position or select Quit Command.

The current annotations contents are as follows:

* Label
* Cut Volume
* Fill Volume
* Water Volume
* Water Level
* Min Water Level
* Max Water Level

To toggle the contents and change the text size of the annotation search "EarthworkAnnotation" in the [Options](settings-configuration.htm).

---

### [Icon: Move Earthwork button](images/tool-buttons/object-move.png) Move the current earthwork

Using the move tool you can click to select the point to move the earthwork, click again to place the earthwork in a new position. You can alternatively use the tools right click *Enter Values* option to enter a specific translation.

See [Moving an Earthwork](earthwork-move.htm) for more information.

---

### [Icon: Rotate Earthwork button](images/tool-buttons/object-rotate.png) Rotate the current earthwork

Using the rotate tool you can click to select the point to rotate the earthwork around, then select another point to get the base angle for rotation, click again to select a new relative rotation. You can alternatively use the tools right click *Rotate* option to set a rotation origin and specific rotation angle.

See [Rotating an Earthwork](earthwork-rotate.htm) for more information.

---

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Surface Volumes');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility calculates the cut and fill volumes between two surfaces, or the volumes of one surface to a given level.

*Note:* To compare two surfaces you will need to have more than one surface model in the project.
You may have loaded the first surface when you started the project by opening a ground survey (for example, from a DWG or DXF file).
Additional surfaces can be added to the project using the **Import** option on the **File** menu.
Or you may have created the [Final Surface](final-surface.htm) or [Formation Surface](automatic-formation-surface.htm) from your design model.

To access the Surface Volumes window click on the Surface Volumes button [Icon: Surface volumes button](images/tool-buttons/surface-volumes.png) on the [Surfaces and Volumes toolbar [Icon: Surfaces and Volumes Tools button](images/surface-volume-tools-button.png)](surface-volume-tools.htm).

The following window is displayed for selecting the two surfaces, or surface and level, for the volume calculation:

[Screenshot: surface-volume-dlg.png - Surfaces Volumes window](images/surface-volume-dlg.png)

### Proposed and Existing

If comparing two surfaces, select each of the two surfaces from the drop-down list of available surfaces.
The existing surface is initially pre-set with the *current survey* model.

The Proposed surface is displayed on the preview with a red boundary. The Existing surface is displayed with a green boundary.

### Existing and Level

If comparing one surface to a level, select the surface from the **Existing** drop-down list of available surfaces, and select the **Volume to level** option and enter the desired level.

In this case the *cut* volume will be where the surface is above the given level, and the *fill* volume is where the surface is below the given level.

For example, if you are analysing the water volume of a flood plain, enter the flood water level. The *fill* volume will be the water volume over the surface.

[Screenshot: surface-volume-dlg4.png - Surfaces Volumes window](images/surface-volume-dlg4.png)

### Auto Boundary

By default an auto-boundary is calculated which includes the overlapping areas of the two surfaces.

The overlapping region will be shown shaded in either red, green or yellow.

If the proposed surface is contained entirely within the existing surface, it is shaded in red:

[Screenshot: surface-volume-dlg2.png - Surfaces Volumes window](images/surface-volume-dlg2.png)

If the existing surface is contained entirely within the proposed surface, it is shaded in green.

Whereas, if the two surfaces only partially overlap then the common region will be shaded in yellow:

[Screenshot: surface-volume-dlg3.png - Surfaces Volumes window](images/surface-volume-dlg3.png)

The yellow shading alerts you that your proposed design extends outside the existing survey region. The proposed design region that is outside the existing survey will not contribute to the volume calculation. It is easy to see parts that won't be included because they have the red line boundary rather than the yellow.

### Manual Boundary

You may want to specify your own boundary to limit the region for the volume calculation. In this case select the **Manual Boundary** option and click the **Define Boundary** button.

[Screenshot: surface-volume-boundary.png - Volume model boundary options](images/surface-volume-boundary.png)

Clicking the **Define Boundary** button will return you to the main plan-view window to allow you to select or create a boundary line. Click to place points to define your manual boundary. You can also make use of the [snap tools](snap-tools.htm) and [undo](undo.htm) facility.

When you have completed the boundary, right-click and select **Finish** from the menu.

Alternatively, you can pick existing lines in the right-click menu. Use **Select Line** to pick a boundary line from one of the imported drawings. Use **Select Centreline** to use a centreline.

[Screenshot: surface-volume-boundary-menu.png - Manual boundary right click options](images/surface-volume-boundary-menu.png)

When you have finished, the Surface Volumes window will reappear showing the defined boundary shown in blue. The region common to the two surfaces and the boundary will be shaded in yellow:

[Screenshot: surface-volume-dlg5.png - Surfaces Volumes window](images/surface-volume-dlg5.png)

### Surface Properties

To view some properties of each of your two selected surfaces click the [Icon: Surface Properties button](images/surface-properties-button.png) surface properties button.

[Screenshot: surface-volume-properties-dlg.png - Surface Properties window](images/surface-volume-properties-dlg.png)

#### Max/Min Levels

The Surface Properties window shows the maximum and minimum levels of the surface. This can be useful for verifying which is in Cut and which is in Fill.

You can modify the maximum and minimum levels which are used for the surface. Restricting the level range can be used to exclude high or low drawing features from the surface. This may reduce the outer boundary line, but it will not produce *holes* inside the surface.

#### Trim

To give a complete volume comparison between the two surfaces, one surface should be entirely contained within the other, i.e. their boundaries should not overlap.

Accordingly, it can sometimes be useful to adjust the trimming to maximize the boundary of the larger surface (often the existing ground surface), or minimize the boundary of the smaller surface (often the proposed surface).

[Screenshot: surface-trim-on.png - Trimmed outer boundary](images/surface-trim-on.png)

In this example, Surface 1 (red boundary) is shown with default trimming, and there is some overlap with the Surface 2 (green boundary) along the southern edge.

[Screenshot: surface-trim-off.png - Untrimmed outer boundary](images/surface-trim-off.png)

With trimming reduced on Surface 1 there is now no overlap of the two surface boundaries.

### Volumes...

Click the **Volumes...** button to see the Cut and Fill volumes calculation result:

[Screenshot: surface-volume-report-dlg.png - Cut and Fill volumes report](images/surface-volume-report-dlg.png)

The **Cut** volume is where the proposed surface is lower than the existing surface.

The **Fill** volume is where the proposed surface is higher than the existing surface.

The volumes calculated by Site3D are the exact geometrically calculated volumes between the two surfaces. Site3D does not use approximate voluming methods.

### Display as Isopachyte...

To produce the isopachyte depth model of the cut and fill, click the **Display as Isopachyte...** button.

[Screenshot: surface-volume-save-dlg.png - Model name window](images/surface-volume-save-dlg.png)

You can choose a name for the cut and fill model. When you click **OK** the new isopachyte surface will be saved and added to the list of models in your project.

The saved volume model is automatically shaded with cut and fill height bands and the key is added to the drawing.

[Screenshot: surface-volume-model-ex.png - Example volume model with cut-and-fill height band key](images/surface-volume-model-ex.png)

The height band key on the drawing shows the total cut and fill volumes from the calculation, as well as the cut and fill volume in each depth band:

[Screenshot: height-band-key.png - Height band key](images/height-band-key.png)

The title of the model is shown at the top of the height band key. The title can be turned on and off in the [Options](settings-configuration.htm), the setting name is "IsopachyteHeightBandKeyShowTitle".

The key can be moved using the [Setting-Out](setting-out.htm) move tool [Icon: Move setting-out table button](images/tool-buttons/table-move.png) which is found in the Setting-Out tools [Icon: Setting Out Tools button](images/setting-out-tools-button.png).

The volume model is generated with appropriate default height banding options, and if you want to change the height band properties you can do this through the [Model Colours Window](model-colours.htm):

[Screenshot: model-colours-dlg-volume.png - Cut and Fill height band colours](images/model-colours-dlg-volume.png)

Earthwork profiles can be vertically adjusted when looking at the earthwork long section.

[Screenshot: earthwork-section.png - Earthworks simple section](images/earthwork-section.png)

To adjust the earthwork in this way, use the [simple section tools [Icon: Simple Sections button](images/simple-section-button.png)](simple-section.htm) to place a section path across the earthwork in the plan view:

[Screenshot: pond-ex2-sectioned.png - Simple section crossing a pond earthwork](images/pond-ex2-sectioned.png)

Then whilst in the simple section view click the [Icon: Earthworks Tools button](images/earthworks-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar.

This is the Earthworks Toolbar when in the section view:

button and select an earthwork line. The editable earthwork lines will be highlighted by a visual snap point near the cursor position. Click to select one.

*Note*: If you cannot snap to a line on the earthwork section, it is most likely part of the earthwork interfacing. You can alter the interface properties from the [Earthwork tools](earthwork-tools.htm) in the plan view.

[Screenshot: earthwork-section.png - Earthworks simple section](images/earthwork-section.png)

### Clash Detection

Site3D performs automatic clash detection after every change to the drainage network. If two pipes clash, or pass close to each other, a design alert will show on the drawing highlighting where there this occurs. The design alert window has more information, such as which two pipes are crossing, the coordinate of the clash, and clearance distance if not a physical clash.

For more information on clash detection and design alerts, see the **[Design Alerts](design-alerts.htm)** help page.

  

### Back Drops

Generally, the invert levels of conduits will match the invert level of the manhole and Site3D will try to maintain this when using the drainage tools. For example, in the longsection view if you use the move manhole tool, the invert levels of the pipes at that manhole will be updated to match the new invert level of the manhole.

However, it is possible to design a backdrop if there are two or more pipes connected to a manhole. This can be done by using the [Icon: Edit pipe button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-pipe-properties.png) Conduit Properties tools to select the pipe that you want to backfall into the manhole, then setting the soffit or invert levels of a pipe.

[Screenshot: manhole-backdrop.png - Manhole Backdrop](images/manhole-backdrop.png)
  

### [Icon: Start new Run button](images/tool-buttons/new-line.png) New Drainage Run, or add to a Drainage Run

To create a new drainage run click the [Icon: Start new Run button](images/tool-buttons/new-line.png) button.

Click the position for the first manhole. As you move the mouse to place the next manhole, you will see a line stretching to the cursor position, representing the conduit between the two manholes. Click to place the next manhole, and so on.
When you are finished, right-click the mouse and select *Quit Command* from the menu, or press the *Escape* key on the keyboard.

[Screenshot: drainage-new-ex1.png - Adding a new drainage run](images/drainage-new-ex1.png)

If you click on, or very close to, another manhole of the same network type (Storm or Foul), a new manhole is not placed, but instead the new conduit joins into that manhole.
Thus it is easy to join runs to make a drainage network.

Similarly, if you click on, or very close to, another conduit of the same network type (Storm or Foul), a new manhole is inserted into the selected conduit joining to the new conduit you are adding.

*Note*: By default, each new manhole is placed at a depth such that the conduits which join into it have a soffit level of 1.2m below cover level. The pipes join soffit to soffit through the manhole.

### Switching between adding Adoptable and adding Private/Plot drainage

By default, all drainage is added as adoptable. You can switch to adding new drainage as Private/Plot Drainage from the Drainage menu or, if the new drainage tool is active, from the right-click menu.

Clicking the **Private/Plot Drainage** menu option will toggle between adding new manholes as private or as adoptable drainage. A tick is drawn next to the option if you are currently in Private/Plot Drainage mode.

From the right-click menu (while adding new drainage):

[Screenshot: drainage-right-click.png - The right-click menu when adding in new drainage](images/drainage-right-click.png)

You can also change this from the Drainage menu:

[Screenshot: drainage-menu-private.png - The drainage menu with the private drainage option ticked](images/drainage-menu-private.png)

Manholes placed while in Private/Plot Drainage mode will be added using the default manhole properties for private/plot drainage.

This can be toggled from the right click menu when the add new drainage tool is active:

### Pipe Junctions

To create pipe to pipe junctions when creating a new drainage run, tick on the **Drainage -> Private/Plot Drainage** menu item. Then start or end a pipe run on or very near a conduit of the same
network type (Storm or Foul) and it will automatically connect the pipes together with a junction, making it easy to join runs together midway into another conduit.

### [Icon: Add Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/add-point.png) Add Manhole into a Conduit

To add a new manhole into a conduit, click the [Icon: Add Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/add-point.png) button.

Select the conduit into which the manhole is to be added.
When you click the mouse, a new manhole appears at the cursor position, showing how it will join into the network.
Move the mouse to the required position for the new manhole and click to place it.

[Screenshot: manhole-add-ex1.png - Example of adding a manhole](images/manhole-add-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: manhole-add-ex2.png - Example of adding a manhole](images/manhole-add-ex2.png)
[Screenshot: manhole-add-ex3.png - Example of adding a manhole](images/manhole-add-ex3.png)

*Note*: By default, each new manhole is placed at a depth such that the conduits which join into it have a soffit level of 1.2m below cover level. The pipes join soffit to soffit through the manhole.

*Note*: If a manhole is shown in a bright red disc (as for S15 in the above example), it indicates that it will not fully drain (i.e. it is lower than all its joining conduits)

### [Icon: Move Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) Move Manhole

To move a manhole, click the [Icon: Move Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) button.

Select the manhole by clicking near it with the mouse. When selected, the manhole moves with the cursor. Move the cursor to the new position and click to place the manhole.

[Screenshot: manhole-move-ex1.png - Example of moving a manhole](images/manhole-move-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: manhole-move-ex2.png - Example of moving a manhole](images/manhole-move-ex2.png)
[Screenshot: manhole-move-ex3.png - Example of moving a manhole](images/manhole-move-ex3.png)

*Note*: When a manhole is moved, its cover level will change to that of the new position, but its invert level will remain as before. Therefore the depth of the manhole will change. Similarly, the invert level of the joining conduits will remain the same, but their length will alter, and so gradient of the conduits will change accordingly.

*Note*: If the moved manhole was a junction connection, the invert level is also adjusted to keep the junction connection soffit to soffit. Site3D will also restrict the movement to avoid bending the joined-onto pipe.

### [Icon: Delete Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/delete-point.png) Delete Manhole

To delete a manhole, click the [Icon: Delete Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/delete-point.png) button.

Select the manhole by clicking near it with the mouse.
The selected manhole will be removed along with all conduits which joined into it.

*Note*: You can reverse the deletion by using the *Undo* facility straight after.

### [Icon: Delete Conduit button](images/tool-buttons/delete-link.png) Delete Conduit

To delete a conduit, click the [Icon: Delete Conduit button](images/tool-buttons/delete-link.png) button.

Select the conduit by clicking near it with the mouse.
The selected conduit will be removed from the network, breaking the connection between the manholes at either end.

*Note*: You can reverse the deletion by using the *Undo* facility straight after.

---

### [Icon: Manhole Schedule button](images/tool-buttons/table-new.png) Manhole Schedule

To create or move a schedule of the manholes, click the [Icon: Manhole Schedule button](images/tool-buttons/table-new.png) button.

An outline box is shown next to the cursor, indicating the size of the manhole schedule table.
Move the cursor to where you want to place the schedule on the drawing. Click to place it.

[Screenshot: manhole-schedule-ex.png - Manhole Schedule example](images/manhole-schedule-ex.png)

The manhole schedule displays current information about the drainage network.
If you change the drainage network then the manhole schedule will automatically update to show the correct information.

If you want to reposition the schedule on the drawing, click the [Icon: Manhole Schedule button](images/tool-buttons/table-new.png) button, and redo the placement of the schedule box.
The existing schedule will move to the new position.

You can modify the manhole schedule properties by using the [Drainage->Manhole Schedule Properties](drainage-menu.htm) menu option:

[Screenshot: manhole-schedule-properties-dlg.png - Manhole Schedule Properties window](images/manhole-schedule-properties-dlg.png)

### Schedule Properties - Sorting Style

If the Grouped Ascending or Grouped Descending sorting style is set, the manhole schedule will be split into multiple tables with each network group being a separate table.

When Separate Phases is enabled, the manhole schedule will be split into multiple tables with each phase being a separate table.

When Include Private is enabled, the private/plot drainage will be included in separate tables.

### Schedule Properties - Available Columns

You can toggle on and off columns to suit the information you need. Below is information on columns that are toggled off by default.

**Diagram**

Show a small top-view diagram of each manhole, this combines the information from the [Drainage Schedule](drainage.htm#drainage_schedule) into the Manhole Schedule.

[Screenshot: manhole-schedule-properties-diagram.png - Manhole Schedule Properties Diagram Column](images/manhole-schedule-properties-diagram.png)

**Conduit Invert Levels**

Display all the connected conduit invert levels

[Screenshot: manhole-schedule-properties-conduit-invert-levels.png - Manhole Schedule Properties Conduit Invert Levels Column](images/manhole-schedule-properties-conduit-invert-levels.png)

**Base Level Column**

The Base Level column shows the level at the bottom of the catchpit for manholes that have a catchpit depth.

---

### [Icon: Drainage Schedule button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-schedule.png) Drainage Schedule

The **drainage schedule** shows a small top-view diagram of each manhole, indicating the position and size of each incoming connection.

To create or move a drainage schedule , click the [Icon: Drainage Schedule button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-schedule.png) button.

[Screenshot: drainage-schedule-ex1.png - Drainage schedule example](images/drainage-schedule-ex1.png)

The grids of manhole diagrams are paged out to make it easy to print the schedule on standard paper sizes.

[Screenshot: drainage-schedule-ex2.png - Drainage schedule paged example](images/drainage-schedule-ex2.png)

### [Icon: Toggle gullies button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-gully-toggle.png) Toggle Gullies

Road gully positions are calculated automatically, according to the drainage area set in the [road properties](centreline-properties.htm).

This toolbar button toggles the display of the gullies.

*Note*: You can also turn the gullies on/off using the [layers window](layers-dlg.htm).

### [Icon: Move Drainage Annotation button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-move.png) Move Drainage Annotation

After creating a manhole or conduit, the annotation is automatically placed on the plan. The automatic placement of manhole annotations looks at the other annotations in the same drainage network and determines a location that does not overlap other texts. This will also be recalculated after any design change. While this often does a good job at placing the text the text may still overlap other design items on the site or texts on imported drawings.

[Screenshot: drainage-annotation-pos-auto.png - Automatic Manhole Annotation Placement example](images/drainage-annotation-pos-auto.png)

If you click the Move Drainage Annotation button you will highlight the nearest manhole or conduit annotation text. If you click with an annotation selected you will see a box indicating the new textbox location and a line going from the manhole/conduit to the centre of the box:

[Screenshot: drainage-annotation-pos-select.png - Manhole Annotation Selection example](images/drainage-annotation-pos-select.png)

If you click again the textbox will be manually positioned to the indicated location:

[Screenshot: drainage-annotation-pos-manual.png - Manual Manhole Annotation example](images/drainage-annotation-pos-manual.png)

To finish right click and select Quit Command from the right-click menu.

[Screenshot: drainage-annotation-pos-right-click.png - Manual Manhole Annotation right click menu](images/drainage-annotation-pos-right-click.png)

To revert back to automatic positioning you can use the Move Drainage Annotation tool to select an annotation, then right click and select Use Automatic Position, this will revert the selected annotation back to automatic positioning. Alternatively, you can enter the Move Annotation tool and right click without having an annotation selected. Then, from the right click menu, select Use Automatic Position. This will allow you to select multiple annotations in a row to set them back to automatic positioning. When you are done you can right click again and untick Use Automatic Position to return to manual text position or select Quit Command.

### [Icon: Rotate Drainage Annotation button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-rotate.png) Rotate Drainage Annotation

The rotate annotation option works the same way as the move annotation tool but instead allows you to override the default rotation. Again, you can right click and select **Use Automatic Position** to reset the annotation position and rotation if desired.

### [Icon: Delete Network button](images/tool-buttons/delete-network.png) Delete Network

This option will delete the closest drainage network including its drainable areas. If you want to delete a sub-section of the network you can first use the [Delete Manhole](#delete_manhole.htm) tool to separate the section from the rest of the network, and then use this option on the disconnected section.

### [Icon: Drainage properties button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-properties.png) Drainage Properties

This toolbar button brings up the [Drainage Properties](drainage-display-properties.htm) window, here you can specify how the manholes and conduits are annotated.

You should see the following window:

[Screenshot: drainage-properties-dlg.png - Drainage properties window](images/drainage-properties-dlg.png)

For more information see the [Drainage Properties window help page](drainage-display-properties.htm).

### [Icon: Manhole Network Properties button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-manhole-network-properties.png)Manhole Properties Schedule

For more information see the [Manhole Properties Schedule help page](drainage-manhole-properties-schedule.htm).

### [Icon: Conduit Network Properties button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-pipe-network-properties.png)Pipe Properties Schedule

For more information see the [Pipe Properties Schedule help page](drainage-conduit-properties-schedule.htm).

This option generates a longsection view of the drainage network along a run of conduits between two manholes.

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/drainage-button.png) drainage tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Drainage Longsection button](images/tool-buttons/vertical-alignment.png) drainage longsection button.

You then select the two manholes between which the longsection should be generated:

* You will be prompted to select the start manhole position from the drawing.
  As you move the mouse cursor the nearest manhole is highlighted.
  Click to select.
* Then you will be prompted to select the end manhole for the run.
  As you move the mouse cursor the nearest manhole is highlighted.
  Click to select.

Any two manholes can be selected which are connected by the drainage network. The longsection will be generated for the most direct route between the two manholes.

The longsection view window is shown with a fully annotated drainage long-section drawing:

[Screenshot: drainage-longsection-view.png - Drainage Longsection window](images/drainage-longsection-view.png)

### [Icon: Move Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) Move Manhole

To move a manhole, click the [Icon: Move Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) button.

You can select one of the manholes on the longsection drawing and move it up/down. The manhole's joining conduits also move up/down with it.

[Screenshot: drainage-longsection-move-ex1.png - Move Manhole example](images/drainage-longsection-move-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: drainage-longsection-move-ex2.png - Move Manhole example](images/drainage-longsection-move-ex2.png)
[Screenshot: drainage-longsection-move-ex3.png - Move Manhole example](images/drainage-longsection-move-ex3.png)

You can also **right-click** to see a menu for further options, you can select **Enter Level** for exact positioning.

[Screenshot: enter-chainage-level-dlg.png - Enter level window](images/enter-chainage-level-dlg.png)

### [Icon: Set Pipe Run Gradient button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-set-gradient.png) Set Gradient

To set the gradient on a conduit, or a run of conduits, click the [Icon: Set Pipe Run Gradient button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-set-gradient.png) button.

You should now select the two manholes between which the gradient is to be set. They can be adjacent manholes for setting a single conduit gradient, or they can be separated by a run of conduits and manholes.
  
You will be prompted to select the start manhole position from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest manhole is highlighted.
Click to select.
  
Then you will be prompted to select the end manhole for the run.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest manhole is highlighted.
Click to select.

[Screenshot: enter-chainage-gradient-dlg.png - Enter gradient window](images/enter-chainage-gradient-dlg.png)

The following images show the difference after setting the whole run to a gradient of 1:50 (2%).

[Screenshot: drainage-set-gradient-ex1.png - Set gradient example](images/drainage-set-gradient-ex1.png)
  
[Screenshot: drainage-set-gradient-ex2.png - Set gradient example](images/drainage-set-gradient-ex2.png)

*Note*: The gradient is set from the first selected manhole, which will maintain its depth. The depths of all the other manholes in the selected run are adjusted to achieve the specified conduit gradient.
