The *Swept Path Analysis* toolbar has tools for analysing the swept paths of vehicles.

To access these tools click the [Icon: Swept Path Analysis Tools button](images/swept-path-button.png) button.

You will see this toolbar appear on the top left of the plan view window:

[Screenshot: swept-path.png - Swept path analysis toolbar icon](images/mode-buttons/swept-path.png)

[[Screenshot: swept-path-new.png - New swept path button](images/tool-buttons/swept-path-new.png)](#new)
[[Screenshot: swept-path-delete.png - Delete swept path button](images/tool-buttons/swept-path-delete.png)](#delete)
[Icon: toolbar spacer](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-spacer.png)
[Icon: Close toolbar button](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-close.png)

[[Icon: New swept path button](images/tool-buttons/swept-path-new.png)
Create a new vehicle swept path](#new)
  
[[Icon: Delete swept path button](images/tool-buttons/swept-path-delete.png)
Delete a swept path](#delete)

---

### [Icon: New swept path button](images/tool-buttons/swept-path-new.png) New Vehicle Swept Path

Clicking on this button will open the Vehicle Chooser window where you can select a vehicle to create a swept path from.

[Screenshot: swept-path-vehicle-editor-dlg.png - Vehicle Editor Window](images/swept-path-vehicle-editor-dlg.png)

### Select

From the vehicle list at the top left you can click to select one of default vehicle definitions and then use this to create a swept path for by pressing the **Select** button.

After pressing the **Select** button with a vehicle selected from the vehicle list you can then place this in the project. Move the mouse around the project and click to set the initial position of the vehicle.

Then you can set the initial orientation of the vehicle by moving the mouse to the desired rotation and then clicking to set this.

Then you can can set the next possible position of the vehicle, this will be limited to the maximum turning radius of the vehicle. A swept path between the two positions will be calculated as you move the mouse. Clicking will lock this in and allow you to set the next path position.

### Close

Pressing the **Close** button will close this window and exit the New Swept Path tool.

### Edit

Clicking the **Edit** button will enable all of the edit boxes below allowing you to make changes to the selected vehicle's dimensions. Please note that you cannot edit vehicles from the default vehicle packs. These vehicles are indicated with a "\*" at the end of the vehicle name in the selection list at the top left. If you wish to make changes to a vehicle from the default pack, please copy it first and edit the copy.

### Save

The **Save** button is only available when you are editing a vehicle. If you make changes, click the **Save** button to store the changes in the current vehicle. The dimension edits will then grey out indicating you can't make any further changes.

### Copy

Press the **Copy** button to make a duplicate of the currently selected vehicle. It will add the new copy into the selection list with "\_Copy" at the end of the vehicle name.

### Del

Pressing the **Del** button will remove the current selected vehicle from the vehicle selection list. You cannot delete a vehicle from the default vehicle pack.

### New

Click the **New** button and a new vehicle will be added to the list. It will also enable the dimension edit boxes ready for you to start setting up the new vehicle.

### Editing Vehicle Dimensions

When setting a vehicle **Name**, it must be unique to distinguish it from all other vehicles in the list.

You can type in a name for the vehicle's **Catagory**. This name will be used to group vehicles of the same catagory to gether in the selection list above.

The vehicle's **Width** and **Length** specify the overall vehicle bounding box to be tracked.

The **Front Overhang** is the distance measured from the front of the vehicle to the centre of the Front Axel Block.

The **Wheel Base** is the distance measured from the centre of the Front Axel Block to the centre of the Rear Axel Block.

The vehicle's **Turning Radius** is the kerb to kerb turning radius measured in metres. This is the smallest distance from the outside edge of the outer front wheel that the vehicle can make when turning at full lock. There will likely be parts of the vehicle body that overhang slightly beyond this. Typing in a Turning Angle and clicking the **TA** button will use the vehicles current information to calculate a **Turning Radius** from the **TA** value.

**Lock to Lock Time** is the time in seconds it takes to safely turn the steering wheel from its left most position to the right most position.

**Number of Axles** for the front and rear is number of axles in either the front or rear. For example a car typically has one axle at the front and one axle at the rear whereas a lorry might have one axle at the front but two axles at the rear.

If the vehicle has more than one axle at the front or rear, the **Axle Block Length** edit will enable. Please specify the distance from the centre of the first axle in this block to the centre of the last axle in this block.

**Wheels Per Axle** is the number of wheels on each axle in this block. For example, a car will have two wheels on the front axle (one of the left and one on the right) but a lorry might have four wheels on each rear axle (two on the left and two on the right) to help spread weight.

**Full Track Width** is the distance from the outer edge of the left most wheel on the axle and the outer edge of the right most wheel on the same axle. Ticking **Auto** will mean the Full Track Width is set as the vehicle's bounding box width.

### [Icon: Delete swept path button](images/tool-buttons/swept-path-delete.png) Delete Swept Path

After clicking on this button you can then move the mouse around to highlight the closest vehicle swept path that has been generated. Click to delete the highlighted vehicle swept path.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('File Output');
show\_index();
//-->

To output the results from Site3D use the **File->Save As...** menu option.

[Screenshot: file-save-as-menu.png - Save As options on the File menu](images/file-save-as-menu.png)

The menu is categorised into four types of output file, as follows:

## ---- Plan ----

### Site3D   Project File (\*.erd)

This is the file format used natively to store all details and data of the drawing and design model.
When an ERD file is re-opened it will show the project exactly as it was when you saved it.

### AutoCad DXF (\*.dxf)

The DXF file format can be read by most other CAD related software applications.
This is the Autodesk published format for drawing exchange.

When you select this option you will get a [preview window with a few optional settings](file-save-as-dxf.htm).

Site3D will save in the 2004 version of **DXF** unless the particular entities being saved require the 2007 version or the 2010 version.

### AutoCad DWG (\*.dwg)

The DWG file format originates from Autodesk Inc. for their AutoCADTM product line, but has also more recently been adopted by the Open Design Alliance.

To export **DWG** format files it is necessary to have the free DWG/DXF Converter application installed. It can be found on the Open Design Alliance website:

**https://www.opendesign.com/guestfiles/oda\_file\_converter**

If you have not yet installed the converter, you will be prompted to do so when you try to import/export a DWG. It will offer to show you the web-page where the converter can be downloaded for install.

After installing the converter, DWG export and import will operate seamlessly. The converter's icon may display on the Windows taskbar during the DWG import and export operations.

When you select to save as DWG you will get a [preview window with a few optional settings](file-save-as-dxf.htm).

Site3D will save in the 2004 version of **DWG** unless the particular entities being saved require the 2007 version or the 2010 version.

### Google Earth (\*.kml)

You can export the drawing in KML format, so that your design will display in-situ in Google Earth.

After selecting the folder and file name for the KML file, you will be prompted with the following option:

[Screenshot: kml-export-options-dlg.png - KML export options window](images/kml-export-options-dlg.png)

The default is to *drape* the design onto the Google Earth ground surface. The alternative is for Google Earth to show the design items at the 3D levels according to your design. In most cases it is best to drape the model because the Google Earth ground surface is likely to be less accurate than your ground survey model, and as such, much of your 3D design might be just *underneath* the Google Earth ground surface, and hence not visible in Google Earth. Selecting the *drape* option ensures your design will be visible when viewed in Google Earth.

When you open the \*.kml file with Google Earth it will zoom into the location which matches the coordinates of your drawing.
This is very useful for visualising exactly where a survey drawing is situated within the UK.
  
The gloabl position (latitude and longitude) will be correct if you are working to a known coordinate system, for example, UK Ordnance Survey or US State Plane. (See [Project Options](project-options.htm))

### Line To CSV (\*.csv)

This is a facility for exporting design line coordinate data in CSV format, representing one of the designed features (for example, a road centreline, or a channel line). The format is suitable for reading directly into setting-out devices on site.

When this output option is selected you will be prompted to choose a design line from the drawing and then a chainage interval at which points (X,Y,Z) will be written to the CSV file.

### Table to CSV (\*.csv)

This is a facility for exporting a [setting-out table](setting-out.htm) to CSV files (for example, centreline setting-out). The format is suitable for reading directly into the setting-out devices on site.

When this output option is selected you will be prompted to choose a [setting-out table](setting-out.htm) from the drawing, which is to be written to the CSV file. This facility is similar to **Line to CSV** except that it exports additional information from the table, such as tangent points and point type.

### GPS Device (\*.gpx)

This file format is compatible with popular GPS devices, and can also be loaded by Google Earth.
The output GPX file will contain only the designed road lines.

### Longsections in DXF

This option produces a DXF file containing the longsection drawings from all of the roads in the design.
The longsections are displayed one below the other in the DXF drawing.
Each one is drawn up as it would be shown in the [vertical alignment view](centreline-vertical-alignment.htm) for the road.

### Land XML (\*.xml)

This is a facility to export design information to Land XML files. After clicking this option, simply choose the filename for it to be saved as. Currently supported export features: road centrelines, drainage manholes and drainage conduits.

### IFC (\*.ifc)

This option produces an IFC 2x3 file containing 3D design surface information and solid 3D drainage items with attributes ready to import into your **BIM** package of choice.[Click here for further details](file-save-as-ifc.htm).

### 12DA (\*.12da)

This facility is for exporting design alignment information to 12DA format. Currently supported export features: road centrelines.

### Design to OBJ (\*.obj)

This facility is for exporting to OBJ format and is useful for creating virtual environments. Currently supported export features: final surface (roads, footways and earthworks) and house walls and slabs.

[Click here for further details](obj-export.htm).

### 3D PDF (\*.pdf)

This facility is for exporting 3D PDFs of the site. Note 3D PDFs are not widely supported by PDF readers. Currently supported export features: roads and final surface.

### U3D (\*.U3D)

This facility is for exporting to U3D format. Currently supported export features: survey surfaces, final surface (roads, footways and earthworks).

## ---- Drainage ----

### InfoDrainage Project (\*.iddx)

This is the recommended format for transferring drainage networks to and from InfoDrainageTM. This is useful where you want to use InfoDrainage to design your pipe sizes and invert levels, and to simulate the system under various storm conditions.

After clicking this option you will be shown a list of potential drainage networks in your design that you can choose to select to export.

[Screenshot: drainage-network-export-dlg.png - Drainage Networks export window](images/drainage-network-export-dlg.png)

Next to each network will be a status colour. Green means it will export, whereas red means something needs your attention. If the status colour is red please look at the status message below the list of networks. Select a network from the list and the status message will inform you of anything that needs your attention to enable this network to export. For example, each network must have an outfall specified.

Ponds will be included in the InfoDrainage project with the drainage network if relevant. For more information on pond export see the [Pond Export help page](drainage-pond-export.htm).

*NOTE*: You can select multiple networks for export into the same MDX file (for example, both storm and foul) by holding down the CTRL key on the keyboard when selecting an additional network from the list.

### Microdrainage Project (\*.mdx)

This is the recommended format for transferring drainage networks to and from MicroDrainageTM. This is useful where you want to use MicroDrainage to design your pipe sizes and invert levels, and to simulate the system under various storm conditions.

The output options are the same as for the IDDX option. You will be shown a list of drainage networks in your design that you can choose to export.

### Microdrainage Source Control (\*.srcx)

This option allows exporting of ponds in the SRCX format for use in the MicroDrainageTM Source Control Module, or other SRCX compatiple drainage packages. For more information on pond export see the [Pond Export help page](drainage-pond-export.htm).

### Microdrainage Storm (\*.sws)

This option outputs the storm drainage network design in the SWS format for MicroDrainageTM, or other SWS compatiple drainage packages.
This is useful where you want to use Microdrainage to design your pipe sizes and invert levels, and to simulate the system under various storm conditions.

The output options are the same as for the IDDX option, except that only *one* network can be selected for export into the file.

### Microdrainage Foul (\*.fws)

This option outputs the foul drainage network design in the FWS format for MicroDrainageTM, or other FWS compatiple drainage packages..

The output options are the same as for the IDDX option, except that only *one* network can be selected for export into the file.

### Causeway Flow (\*.pfd)

This option produces a Causeway FlowTM file containing the drainage network.

The output options are the same as for the IDDX option. You will be shown a list of drainage networks in your design that you can choose to export.

### EPA SWMM (\*.inp)

This option produces an EPA SWMM input file containing the drainage network.

The output options are the same as for the IDDX option. You will be shown a list of drainage networks in your design that you can choose to export.

### HydroWorks (\*.dsd)

This option outputs the storm drainage network design in the DSD format of the former HydroWorks system by Wallingford Software Ltd.
The more recent InfoWorks system can also import DSD files.

### Manhole Schedule (\*.csv)

This option is available when you have [placed a manhole schedule](drainage.htm#manhole_schedule) on the Site3D design.
The output CSV file will contain the same information as is shown in the manhole schedule on the plan view.
The CSV file can be loaded into any standard spreadsheet application or text editor.

### Pipe Schedule (\*.csv)

This option is available when you have [placed a manhole schedule](drainage.htm#manhole_schedule) on the Site3D design.
The output CSV file will contain the pipe network information (such as pipe numbers, lengths and inverts) of each network that has a valid outfall set. For more information refer to [Save As Pipe Schedule](file-save-as-pipe-schedule.htm).
The CSV file can be loaded into any standard spreadsheet application or text editor.

### Drainable Area Schedule (\*.csv)

This option is available when you have [placed a drainable area schedule](drainage-catchment-areas.htm#new_table) on the Site3D design.
The output CSV file will contain the current information shown in the Drainable Area Schedule (such as Assigned Pipe No., Adoptable IMP, Total Permeable etc.).
The CSV file can be loaded into any standard spreadsheet application or text editor.

### Hec-Ras (\*.geo)

This option produces a Hec-Ras geometry file, using each road centreline as a river path. Each road's cross-sections of the ground surface are included to define the river catchment profiles.

*Note*: In **Hec-Ras** when you import the \*.geo geometry file, you must select to **import it as GIS** data, not \*.g\* geometry data.

### Run Longsections in DXF (\*.dxf)

This option produces a single DXF file containing all the longsection drawings of complete drainage runs.
The longsections are displayed one below the other in the DXF drawing.
Each one is drawn up as it would be shown using the [vertical alignment view](drainage-longsection.htm).

When you select this option you will get a [preview window with a few optional settings](file-save-as-drainage-longsection.htm).

## ---- Surface ----

### Design to Polyface Mesh (\*.dxf)

This option enables you to export any of the design surfaces to as DXF polyface mesh entities.

You are shown a list of all the available design surfaces, and can individually choose which ones to include in the exported file.

[Screenshot: design-surface-export-dlg.png - Design surfaces export window](images/design-surface-export-dlg.png)

### Survey to Polyface Mesh (\*.dxf)

This DXF output file contains the 3D triangulated surface of the survey model represented as a Polyline *mesh*.
You will find this useful if your application requires a pre-triangulated 3D surface, and can import a polyline mesh.

### Survey to 3D Faces (\*.dxf)

This DXF output file contains the 3D triangulated surface of the survey model represented as 3D FACE entities.
You will find this useful if your application requires a pre-triangulated 3D surface, and can import 3D Faces.

### Survey to Microdrainage PWF (\*.pwf)

This file format is suitable for loading into the popular drainage design and simulation application.
Although Microdrainage is capable of importing a DXF itself,
you may prefer to utilise the pre-triangulated surface from Site3D.
Microdrainage can import the pre-triangulated surface data as a PWF file.
It can then obtain accurate ground levels from this surface.

### Survey to 3D Surface (\*.stl)

The STL format is used by some 3D modelling systems and 3D printers.
The output represents the 3D ground surface, extruded to a thickness appropriate to the site size.

### All Surfaces to ASCII XYZ (\*.xyz)

This output option will produce a text file containing the X,Y and Z coordinates of all the 3D items in the survey model.
It contains only points (no lines).

### Surface to Gridded ASCII XYZ (\*.xyz)

This option produces an ascii file of XYZ coordinates at a regular grid spacing, representing the 3D ground model surface.
This type of regular grid data is sometimes referred to as a DEM (Digital Elevation Model) or a DTM (Digital Terrain Model).

When the option is selected you are prompted to choose the desired grid spacing, before specifying the output file.

### Surface to Gridded ARC (\*.asc)

This option is simlar to the Surface to Gridded ASCII XYZ (\*.xyz) but saves with .asc formatting.

### VRML (\*.wml)

This option produces a file containing Virtual Reality Markup Language (VRML) representing the 3D surface model.

### All Surfaces to Land XML (\*.xml)

This option exports all surfaces to a Land XML file.

### Surface to Land XML (\*.xml)

This option exports a single surface to a Land XML file.

When the option is selected you are prompted to choose the desired surface to export, before specifying the output file.

## ---- Substrata ----

### Substrata Mesh (\*.dxf)

When substrata have been used in the project this option is available to output them to a DXF file in the form of Polyline Mesh entities.

### Substrata 3D Faces (\*.dxf)

When substrata have been used in the project this option is available to output them to a DXF file in the form of 3D FACE entities.

//<!--
show\_header('Save As DXF/DWG');
show\_index();
//-->

The **Save As** option for **AutoCAD DXF** or **DWG** is on the **File** menu.
It shows the following window, displaying a preview of what will be saved.

[Screenshot: dxf-export-dlg.png - DXF Export Preview Window](images/dxf-export-dlg.png)

### Select Layers to Save

Down the left side of the window is a list of all the drawing models and layers which are to be included in the export.
Initially the selected models and layers will match those of the working drawing.
You can tick on/off any of the models or their layers, to determine exactly what will be exported.

*NOTE*: Layers which are *off* will *not* be included in the output (this contrasts with AutoCAD, which includes *off* layers in its DXF/DWG files).

Underneath the list of layers there are some buttons:

### Reset to Current Layers

Clicking this button will reset the list of models and layers to match those of the working drawing.
If you have ticked/unticked layers and the preview is not looking like you want it, you can use this button to start again.

### Select All

This button is a one-click for ticking *everything* in the list of models and layers.

### Clear All

This button is a one-click for unticking everything in the list of models and layers.
You will then need to tick something in the list of layers in order to get any output.

### Select Region...

Clicking this button will cause the window will disappear and you can then use the cursor to click and drag a rectangle region over the plan view.
When you are happy with the region, click the right mouse button to get a menu.
Choose either *Export Selection* or *Export all Outside selection*.
The resulting exported drawing will be clipped to either inside, or outside, the region as appropriate.
Any drawing items which cross the region edge are included in their entirity (The drawing is not trimmed, but rather the region indicates which items to include).

After selecting either *Export Selection* or *Export all Outside selection* you will be asked for the export file location and name (in the same way as if you had clicked the **Export** button on the export window).

### Export Flattened to Level / Export as 3D

*NOTE*: This option only affects the design model, and not any of the imported ground or layout models.

You can choose whether the design is exported as a 2D drawing or a 3D drawing.
The default option is to export as 2D, flattening the design to a specified level.
This allows the design-line curves to be output as proper arcs but not retain their vertical alignment.
This is necessary because AutoCAD DXF/DWG does not permit 3D levels on ARC entities.

The alternative is *Export as 3D*, which retains the vertical alignment levels on the design-lines,
but outputs the curves as a series of 3D points on polyline entities (with no ARC entities or polyline bulges).

### Export

Click the **Export** button when you are happy with the preview of the drawing.
You will be shown the standard window for choosing the file location and name for the DXF/DWG file.

### DWG Converter

To export and import **DWG** format files it is necessary to have the free DWG/DXF Converter application installed.

If you have not yet installed the converter, you will be prompted to do so when you try to import/export a DWG. It will offer to show you the web-page where the converter can be downloaded for install.

After installing the converter, DWG export and import will operate seamlessly. The converter's window may display during the DWG import and export operations.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Push to AutoCAD');
show\_index();
//-->

The **Push to AutoCAD** option is used to transfer the working design drawing directly into an open AutoCAD window,
without the saving and loading of DXF files.

This function can be useful if you use AutoCAD to prepare the final presentation of your drawings.
Another usage is for when you need to save a DWG file (without first saving a DXF file). You can use AutoCAD to save the DWG file.

The **Push to AutoCAD** option is on the **File** menu.
It shows the following window, displaying a preview of what will be transferred.

[Screenshot: push-to-autocad-dlg.png - Push to AutoCAD Preview Window](images/push-to-autocad-dlg.png)

### Layers to Save

Down the left side of the window is a list of all the drawing models and layers which are to be included in the export.
By default the selected layers to be exported will be based off your current layer states for the project.
You can tick on/off any of the models or their layers, to determine exactly what will be exported.

GeoReferenced images can also be exported to AutoCAD with the image correctly skewed and positioned to match the GeoReferencing.

*NOTE*: Layers which are *off* will *not* be included in the output (this contrasts with AutoCAD, which includes *off* layers in its DXF files).

Underneath the list of layers there are some buttons:

#### Select All

This button is a one-click for ticking *everything* in the list of models and layers.

#### Clear All

This button is a one-click for unticking everything in the list of models and layers.
You will then need to tick something in the list of layers in order to get any output.

#### Reset to Current Layers

This button will reset the selected layers back to the initial state. Layers will be ticked based on your current layer states for the project.

#### Select Region

This button will allow you to select a region in the plan view. The window will disappear allowing you to click and drag the mouse to select a rectangular region.

[Screenshot: select-region.png - Select Region](images/select-region.png)

You can then choose to export the either the area within the drawn rectangle or the area outside of the rectangle from the right click menu.

[Screenshot: select-region-menu.png - Select Region Right Click Menu](images/select-region-menu.png)

### Export as 2D / Export as 3D

By default *Export Flattened to Level* is ticked. This will export the design items in 2D at the specified level.
You can input a specific level or leave it as the default value of 0.0.
This allows the design-line curves to be output as proper arcs but not retain their vertical alignment.
This is necessary because AutoCAD does not permit 3D levels on ARC entities.

The alternative is *Export as 3D*, which retains the vertical alignment levels on the design-lines,
but outputs the curves as a series of 3D points on polyline entities (with no ARC entities or polyline bulges).

### Push

Click the **Push** button when you are happy with the preview.
The drawing will then be transferred directly into the open AutoCAD.
A progress bar will show as the drawing entities are being pushed into the AutoCAD drawing, so that you know when it is complete.

//<!--
show\_header('Boundary Longsection');

//-->

This option generates a longsection view of the boundary network along a run of boundaries between two nodes.

On the [Icon: Plot Boundary Tools button](images/plot-boundary-tools-button.png) Boundary tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Boundary Longsection button](images/tool-buttons/vertical-alignment.png) Boundary longsection button.

You then select the two boundary annotations between which the longsection should be generated:

* You will be prompted to select the start node annotation from the drawing.
  As you move the mouse cursor the nearest node annotation is highlighted.
  Click to select.
* Then you will be prompted to select the end node annotation for the run.
  As you move the mouse cursor the nearest node is highlighted.
  Click to select.

Start Node Selection:

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-creation-ex1.png - Plot Boundary Longsection Select Start Node](images/plot-boundary-longsection-creation-ex1.png)

End Node Selection:

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-creation-ex2.png - Plot Boundary Longsection Select End Node](images/plot-boundary-longsection-creation-ex2.png)

Any two node annotation can be selected which are connected together on the same side of the boundary. The longsection will be generated for the most direct route between the two nodes.

The longsection view window is shown with a fully annotated boundary long-section drawing:

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-view.png - Plot Boundary Longsection window](images/plot-boundary-longsection-view.png)

The longsection view illustrates the levels of the boundary run on both sides. The sides are labeled "LEFT" and "RIGHT". These labels are relative to the direction of the run: the left levels are on the left when moving from the first node to the last node. The solid line represents the boundary side you selected during creation, while the dashed line indicates the opposite side.

The toolbar on the longsection window has the following buttons:

[[Icon: Edit level button](images/tool-buttons/set-level.png)
Edit Level](#edit_level)
  
[[Icon: Set gradient button](images/tool-buttons/plot-gradient.png)
Set the Gradient along a Run](#set_gradient)
  
[Icon: Close Toolbar button](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-close.png)
Click this button to close the toolbar

---

### [Icon: Edit level button](images/tool-buttons/set-level.png) Edit Level

To change a level at a node, click the [Icon: Edit level button](images/tool-buttons/set-level.png) button.

You can select one of the nodes on the longsection drawing and move it up/down. The node's joining links also move up/down with it.

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-level-ex1.png - Edit level example](images/plot-boundary-longsection-level-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-level-ex2.png - Edit level example](images/plot-boundary-longsection-level-ex2.png)
[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-level-ex3.png - Move node example](images/plot-boundary-longsection-level-ex3.png)

You can also **right-click** to see a menu for further options

### Edit Level Right Click Menu Options

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-level-menu.png - Edit level right-click menu](images/plot-boundary-longsection-level-menu.png)

#### Set All Levels At Node

This setting will set all levels at the selected node to the proposed level.

#### Select Only Left/Right

From the right click menu you can select **Select Only Left** to prevent the selection of right hand levels or you can select **Select Only Right** to prevent the selection of left hand levels.
This is useful when the left and right levels are the same at a given node.

Once an initial node has been selected the right-click menu changes.

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-level-menu-selected.png - Edit selected nodes level right-click menu](images/plot-boundary-longsection-level-menu-selected.png)

#### Enter Level

To set exact positioning you can use the **Enter Level option**.

[Screenshot: enter-chainage-level-dlg.png - Enter level window](images/enter-chainage-level-dlg.png)

---

### [Icon: Set gradient button](images/tool-buttons/plot-gradient.png) Set Gradient

To set the gradient on a link, or a run of links, click the [Icon: Set gradient button](images/tool-buttons/plot-gradient.png) button.

You should now select the two nodes between which the gradient is to be set. They can be adjacent nodes for setting a single link gradient, or they can be separated by a run of links and nodes.
  
You will be prompted to select the start node position from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest node is highlighted.
Click to select.
  
Then you will be prompted to select the end node.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest node is highlighted.
Click to select.

[Screenshot: enter-chainage-gradient-dlg.png - Enter gradient window](images/enter-chainage-gradient-dlg.png)

The following images show the difference after setting the left side of the whole run to a gradient of 1:50 (2%).

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-gradient-ex1.png - Set gradient example](images/plot-boundary-longsection-gradient-ex1.png)
  
[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-gradient-ex2.png - Set gradient example](images/plot-boundary-longsection-gradient-ex2.png)

*Note*: The gradient is set from the first selected node, which will maintain its depth. The depths of all the other nodes in the selected run are adjusted to achieve the specified link gradient.

### Gradient Right Click Menu Options

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-longsection-gradient-menu.png - Gradient right-click menu](images/plot-boundary-longsection-gradient-menu.png)

#### Straight Grade

From the right click menu you can select **Straight Grade** to level the nodes between the start and end to match the straight gradient.

#### Select Only Left/Right

From the right click menu you can select **Select Only Left** to prevent the selection of right hand levels or you can select **Select Only Right** to prevent the selection of left hand levels.
This is useful when the left and right levels are the same at a given node.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Print / Plot');
show\_index();
//-->

The Printing/Plotting facility is accessed is from the **Print/Plot...** option on the **File** menu, or with the **Plot** [Icon: Print/Plot button](images/tool-buttons/print.png) toolbar button.

This feature prints the *selected view* to your printing device. So, if you want to print a *plan* drawing, click in the Plan View before selecting the printing facility. To print a long-section, select the Longsection View. Similarly, you can print the 3D View and the Cross-Section View.

The Plot Setup window shows a preview of exactly what you'll get on the printer.

*Note*: You can pan and zoom the preview image with the mouse, to position the drawing on the paper.

[Screenshot: plot-dialog.png - Printing/Plotting window](images/plot-dialog.png)

### Printer, Paper Size and Orientation

Choose the printer from the drop-down list of available printers, then choose the desired paper size for the drawing.

*Note*: If you have a PDF printer installed (for example, CutePDF Writer) you can produce PDF files of your drawings by selecting it as the printer.

Choose the desired paper orientation by clicking the *Portriat* or *Landcape* symbol above the preview. The preview will update to show what would be printed on the paper.

### Printing Extents

By default the print preview shows the same drawing region that is on the *Current View*.

Selecting *Full Extents* previews the whole drawing to its full extents.

*Note*: You can still reposition the preview using the normal Pan and Zoom mouse operations.

### Scale

By default *Fit To Page* is active and the initial scale is shown so that the region in the *current view* fits on the paper.

You can deselect *Fit To Page* and choose a pre-set scale from the drop down menu or type in a specific scale. The preview window shows what you'll get on the paper.

*Note*: Zooming with the mouse in the preview will change the selected scale setting.

### Colour

There are three output colour options:

* **Colour** : Print in the same colours as displayed on the *current view*, excepting the background colour, which will be white.
* **Greyscale** : All colours will be rendered as appropriate intensity grey shades. The background is white.
* **Black and White** : All colours are rendered as black. The background is white.

Click the appropriate button and the preview window will update.

You can also choose whether the road surfaces and survey surfaces should be shown by selecting the **Show Road Surface** and **Show Survey Surface** options.

### Copies

Alter the **Number of Copies** to choose how many copies are to be printed/plotted.

### Orientation

These two buttons allow you to choose **Portrait** or **Landscape** paper orientation. The preview will update as soon as you click on the desired paper orientation.

## Advanced

The **Advanced** button shows a window with some printing options that are generally not required to be changed.

[Screenshot: plot-advanced-dlg.png - Advanced Printing/Plotting window](images/plot-advanced-dlg.png)

### Strip Size

When sending plot files to the printer using OpenGL, the image is processed in strips. This setting allows you to choose the amount of data used for each strip. If your printer is struggling to receive each strip you can try to reduce the strip size. Default: 32768KB (32MB).

### Line Width

A standard line width is used for the printed drawing. If your plots are coming out too light or too dark, you can try increasing or decreasing the line width. Default: 0.27mm

### Anti-Alias

By default, the printed lines are not smoothed, so as to keep the output crisp and clean. However, you can enable line edge smoothing by selecting **Anti-Alias**.

### Force OpenGL

By default drawings are sent to the printer using vector geometry to give crisp clean lines, unless the particular view contains features which require more advanced graphical rendering, in which case OpenGL is used automatically.

For example, the 3D View requires OpenGL processing, but the Plan View can usually be sent to the printer with vector graphics.

In general, printing a line drawing with vector geometry is quicker and produces the best quality for the lines.

Here you have the choice to force OpenGL graphical rendering even for line drawings. Default: Off.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('The Undo Facility');
show\_index();
//-->

The software incorporates a powerful facility to allow you to undo any operation, putting your project back into the state it was before the operation.

To undo your last operation click the [Icon: Undo button](images/undo-button.png)
button on the toolbar, or press **Ctrl Z** on the keyboard.

[Screenshot: undo-menu.png - Undo menu](images/undo-menu.png)

You can also access the **Undo** facility from the **Edit** menu.

This gives the added benefit that is shows specifically which operation would be undone.

The undo operation may be performed multiple times to revoke many operations.
You can undo everything right back to when you opened the project in the current session.

*Note*: The *undo* works on the last operation performed in the active project, and as such, may apply to an operation performed on any of the project's windows.
For example, if you display a road longsection, perform *two* operations on it, and then click undo *three* times, you will undo the two longsection operations and whatever operation you did on the other window before you displayed the longsection.

The spot annotation tools allow you to place, edit, or delete a spot level or spot gradient annotation on your site.

To access the spot annotation toolbar click on the Spot Annotation Tools button from the [Design Toolbar](design-toolbar.htm).

You will see the following toolbar appear at the top left of the plan view window:

[Screenshot: spot-annotation.png - Spot Annotation Tools](images/mode-buttons/spot-annotation.png)

[[Screenshot: spot-annotation-new-level.png - New spot level annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-new-level.png)](#new_level_annotation)
[[Screenshot: spot-annotation-new-gradient.png - New spot gradient annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-new-gradient.png)](#new_gradient_annotation)
[[Screenshot: water-flow-path.png - New water flow path button](images/tool-buttons/water-flow-path.png)](#new_water_path)
[[Screenshot: move-point.png - Move spot annotation button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png)](#move_annotation)
[[Screenshot: annotation-move.png - Move annotation text button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-move.png)](#move_annotation_text)
[[Screenshot: annotation-rotate.png - Rotate annotation text button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-rotate.png)](#rotate_annotation_text)
[[Screenshot: spot-annotation-edit.png - Edit spot annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-edit.png)](#edit_annotation)
[[Screenshot: spot-annotation-delete.png - Delete spot annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-delete.png)](#delete_annotation)
[Icon: toolbar spacer](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-spacer.png)
[Icon: Close toolbar button](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-close.png)

### [Icon: New spot level annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-new-level.png) New Spot Level Annotation

This tool will allow you to place multiple spot level annotations on your site quickly.

When you initially click the button you should see a guide that follows the cursor, showing how the spot annotation will appear with the default properties.
The guide shows a small box at the cursor position, which highlights the point which will be annotated. Connected to that is a larger box, showing the area which will contain the text annotation:

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-add.png - Initial Spot Annotation Guide](images/spot-annotation-add.png)

After placing this initial spot level, the Spot Level Annotation Properties window will pop up.
For more information on the properties dialog see the **[Edit Spot Annotation](#edit_annotation)** help section further down the page.

You should see the window below:

[Screenshot: spot-gradient-properties.png - Spot Annotation Properties Window](images/spot-gradient-properties.png)

Clicking the **OK** button will finalize the spot level.

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-level-ex1.png - Spot Level Annotation Example](images/spot-annotation-level-ex1.png)

This image shows an example spot level.

After placing the initial spot annotation you will then be able to click to place successive spot levels with the same properties without having to specify the properties of each individual spot annotation.

To stop placing additional spot annotations you can exit the tool by clicking on the tool icon again, or by simply selecting another tool.

You can also exit the tool by clicking the right mouse button and selecting **Quit Command** from the right click menu:
[Screenshot: quit-command.png - Spot Annotation Right Click Menu](images/quit-command.png)

In the properties there is an option to create an annotation grid, for more information on this see the [Annotation Grid](#spot_annotation_grid) section below. The above description of placing successive spot annotations does not apply to grids, you will exit out of the tool after placing a grid.

  

### [Icon: New Spot Gradient Annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-new-gradient.png) New Spot Gradient Annotation

This tool allows you to place a gradient marker on your site, showing the gradient at the selected point.

This tool behaves exactly as the **[New Spot Level](#new_level_annotation)** tool. For more in-depth help, please refer to the **New Spot Level** section above.

After clicking on the tool icon from the spot annotation toolbar you will see a placement outline that follows the cursor

As with the **New Spot Level** tool, after you click to place the initial spot gradient you will see the Spot Gradient Annotation Properties dialog.

Clicking the **OK** button will finalize the spot gradient.

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-gradient-ex1.png - Spot Gradient Annotation Example](images/spot-annotation-gradient-ex1.png)

This image shows an example spot gradient.

As with the **New Spot Level** tool, after placing the initial spot annotation you will then be able to click to place successive spot levels with the same properties without having to specify the properties of each individual spot annotation.

You can stop placing additional spot gradients in the same way as the **New Spot Level** tool.
Simply exit the tool by clicking on the tool icon again, switch to another tool, or select **Quit Command** from the right click menu.

### Annotate perpendicular or parallel gradient

On the right click menu there is an option to select a design line to place the spot gradient annotation perpendicularly to, or to place the spot gradient parallel to.

When picking one of these options you can then select a design line to remember. After selecting a line the tool switches back to placing spot gradient annotations.
All successive spot gradient annotations that are placed will then then annotate the gradient direction aligned to the selected design line.
Each gradient direction will be parallel/perpendicular to the closest point on the selected line.

[Screenshot: spot-gradient-parallel-perpendicular.png - Spot Gradient Parallel and Perpendicular option](images/spot-gradient-parallel-perpendicular.png)
  

### Annotation Grid

After placing a spot annotation and ticking the **Grid Entire Surface** checkbox, a regular grid of spot annotations will be applied to the surface at the specified spacing.
The point that is initially selected to place the annotation grid will be used to align the grid.

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-grid-ex.png - Spot Annotation Grid Example](images/spot-annotation-grid-ex.png)

This image shows an example spot level grid applied to a cut and fill surface with a spacing of 5m.

An annotation grid is a single object that the spot annotation tools interact with it as a singular entity.
Meaning that every annotation in the grid shares the same text properties.

When the spot annotation tools are used on an annotation grid, the annotation nearest the cursor will behave as though you were editing a single spot annotation.
The change will then be reflected across the grid. This behaviour is shown below:

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-grid-edit-ex1.png - Spot Annotation Grid Editing Example 1](images/spot-annotation-grid-edit-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: spot-annotation-grid-edit-ex2.png - Spot Annotation Grid Editing Example 2](images/spot-annotation-grid-edit-ex2.png)
[Screenshot: spot-annotation-grid-edit-ex3.png - Spot Annotation Grid Editing Example 3](images/spot-annotation-grid-edit-ex3.png)

### Water flow from point

With this tool you can check the steepest downhill path to a low point on a surface from any given point on the surface. This gives an indication of where water would flow to and pool on a surface.

*Note*: The path produced does *not* take water inertia, friction, or volume into account, so would not overtop small obstacles, for example.

After selecting this tool the select surface window will appear where you can select which surface you want to check the water flow path on.
After making your selection you can then move the mouse over the surface to see the flow path from the cursor:

[Screenshot: water-flow-path-ex1.png - Example showing the water flow path from the cursor](images/water-flow-path-ex1.png)

While the tool is active, you can right click and pick "Select Surface" to change which surface is being used.

Clicking with the mouse will add the currently shown path to the model. The green arrow indicates the start point and flow direction, the circle at the end indicates where water would pool:

[Screenshot: water-flow-path-ex2.png - Water flow path example](images/water-flow-path-ex2.png)

Below is an example of this path in the 3D view:

[Screenshot: water-flow-path-ex3.png - Water flow path example](images/water-flow-path-ex3.png)

### [Icon: Move spot annotation button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) Move Spot Annotation

This tool allows you to move a spot annotation to a new position. This will recalculate the level/gradient based on the new point whilst keeping the same text properties.

First select the spot annotation that you want to modify, the spot annotation closest to the cursor will be highlighted showing which annotation will be selected when you click.
After selecting a spot annotation there will be a guide under the cursor showing the how the spot annotation will be changed:

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-move.png - Spot Annotation Move Example](images/spot-annotation-move.png)

If you decide at this point that you do not want to move the spot annotation, you can exit the tool by clicking on the tool icon again, switching to another tool, or selecting **Quit Command** from the right click menu.

Otherwise, choose the new position, and click to move the spot annotation to the new position.

  

### [Icon: Move annotation text button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-move.png) Move Annotation Text

This tool allows you to move the text position of a spot annotation, without affecting the annotated point or other text properties.

First select the spot annotation that you want to modify, the spot annotation closest to the cursor will be highlighted showing which annotation will be selected when you click.

After selecting a spot annotation there will be a guide under the cursor showing the how the spot annotation will be changed.

Note that the position of the level/gradient that is annotated will not be changed.

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-text-move.png - Spot Annotation Text Move Example](images/spot-annotation-text-move.png)

If you decide at this point that you do not want to move the annotation text, you can exit the tool by clicking on the tool icon again, switching to another tool, or selecting **Quit Command** from the right click menu.

Otherwise, choose the new text position, and click to move the text to the new position.

  

### [Icon: Rotate annotation text button](images/tool-buttons/annotation-rotate.png) Rotate Annotation Text

This tool allows you to rotate the text of a spot annotation, without affecting the annotated point or other text properties.

[Screenshot: spot-annotation-text-rotate.png - Spot Annotation Text Rotate Example](images/spot-annotation-text-rotate.png)

If you decide at this point that you do not want to rotate the annotation text, you can exit the tool by clicking on the tool icon again, switching to another tool, or selecting **Quit Command** from the right click menu.

Otherwise, choose the new text orientation, and click to set the text rotation to the new angle.

  

### [Icon: Edit spot annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-edit.png) Edit Spot Annotation

This tool can be used to alter the properties of a previously placed spot annotation.

Clicking on a spot annotation will bring up the spot annotation properties window shown below:

[Screenshot: spot-gradient-properties.png - Spot Annotation Properties Window](images/spot-gradient-properties.png)

#### Point on Surface

Here you fine tune the position of the spot annotation.

Clicking on the **Move** button will allow you to visually select a new position from the plan view, showing an outline of the annotation with the updated properties that have been input.

You can toggle the **Annotate coordinates** option to annotate the Easting and Northing of the point.

#### Surface to Annotate

This is the surface that you will be annotating levels on.

If you select the **Proposed Design** radio button the spot annotation will annotate levels on the proposed design surface and will be drawn in **red**.

If you select the **Current Survey** radio button the spot annotation will annotate levels on the current survey surface and will be drawn in **green**.

If you select the **SubModel** radio button the drop down menu will become activated, and you can then specify which surface you are annotating levels on and the spot level annotation will be drawn in the layer colour of the selected sub-model.

#### Annotation Properties

You can adjust the position of the text relative to the spot level insertion point.

Clicking on the **Move** button will allow you to visually select a new text position from the plan view, showing an outline of the annotation with the updated properties that have been input.

You can also adjust the size of the text with the **Text Height** input and the angle of the text with the **Text Angle** input.

Clicking on the **Rotate** button will allow you to visually select a new text angle from the plan view, showing an outline of the annotation with the updated properties that have been input.

For spot gradient annotations you have the option of enabling **Align to gradient direction**. With this checkbox ticked the annotation will align to the gradient arrow. With this alignment, the text offset specifies the relative offset from the gradient arrow, with the X offset specifying distance in the arrow direction and the Y offset specifying perpendicular distance from the arrow.

[Screenshot: spot-gradient-aligned-text-ex1.png - Text aligned to gradient arrow](images/spot-gradient-aligned-text-ex1.png)
This example shows the **Align to gradient direction** option enabled.

[Screenshot: spot-gradient-aligned-text-ex2.png - Text aligned to gradient arrow](images/spot-gradient-aligned-text-ex2.png)
The text position is kept aligned to the gradient arrow if the gradient direction changes. For example, if the surface is modified or if a new custom gradient direction is specified. This is useful for final surface annotations as the text will stay aligned if the surface gradient changes when the final surface is regenerated.

#### Gradient Properties

For spot gradient annotations, the default setting is to annotate the maximum downhill gradient at the chosen point. This is the **Max gradient direction** option.

Optionally, you can toggle to **Custom gradient direction**, this will allow you to specify the direction of the gradient that you want to annotate. For example, you might want to annotate the gradient perpendicular to a road channel line. After selecting this option you can then specify the angle by typing into the text box or click the **Rotate** button to the right to pick the angle from the drawing. The angle is specified in degrees with 0 degrees pointing east, increasing in the counter-clockwise direction, with 90 degrees pointing north.

  

### [Icon: Delete spot annotation button](images/tool-buttons/spot-annotation-delete.png) Delete Spot Annotation

With this tool you can remove spot annotations from the project.

When the tool is active the nearest spot annotation will be highlighted, click to delete the currently highlighted spot annotation. If the spot annotation you click is part of a spot annotation grid the entire grid will be removed.

If you decide that you want to stop deleting spot annotations, you can exit the tool by clicking on the tool icon again, switching to another tool, or selecting **Quit Command** from the right click menu.

Some useful information tools are available by clicking the **Additional Tools** [Icon: Additional Tools Button](images/tools-button.png) toolbar button. The following toolbar is shown:

[Screenshot: toolbox.png - Additional Tools](images/mode-buttons/toolbox.png)

[[Screenshot: measure-line.png - Measure line button](images/tool-buttons/measure-line.png)](#length)
[[Screenshot: measure-angle.png - Measure angle button](images/tool-buttons/measure-angle.png)](#angle)
[[Screenshot: measure-radius.png - Measure radius button](images/tool-buttons/measure-radius.png)](#radius)
[[Screenshot: measure-level.png - Measure level button](images/tool-buttons/measure-level.png)](#level)
[[Screenshot: measure-grade.png - Measure grade button](images/tool-buttons/measure-grade.png)](#grade)
[[Screenshot: measure-area.png - Measure area button](images/tool-buttons/measure-area.png)](#area)
[[Screenshot: entity-properties.png - Entity properties button](images/tool-buttons/entity-properties.png)](#entity_properties)
[Icon: toolbar spacer](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-spacer.png)
[Icon: Close toolbar button](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-close.png)

### [Icon: Measure line button](images/tool-buttons/measure-line.png) Measure Line/Path

This is useful for quickly measuring the length or size of a drawing feature.

Click on the drawing to choose the start location for the measurement. As you move the mouse, a line will stretch from the first point to the mouse position. The current line length is shown next to the mouse:

[Screenshot: measure-line-ex1.png - Measure Line 1 point - mouse Example](images/measure-line-ex1.png)

*NOTE*: The [snap](snap-tools.htm) options may be used to latch onto visible features on the drawing.

Click again to choose the second point for the measurement. The following window is shown, indicating the plan length between the two points, as well as the total trip length:

[Screenshot: measure-line-ex2.png - Measure Line 2 point - mouse Example](images/measure-line-ex2.png)

Click **OK** to dismiss the window and continue clicking points to measure a combined path length. Right click and select **Quit Command** to finish.

### [Icon: Measure angle button](images/tool-buttons/measure-angle.png) Measure Angle

The **Measure Angle** facility will return the subtended angle between three points.

Click to specify the starting location. Click a second time to specify the mid point and click a third time to specify the end point.

*NOTE*: The [snap](snap-tools.htm) options may be used to latch onto visible features on the drawing.

[Screenshot: measure-angle-ex1.png - Measure angle three points - mouse Example](images/measure-angle-ex1.png)

After choosing the third point a window is shown indicating the subtended angle between the three points.

[Screenshot: measure-angle-ex2.png - Measure angle window - mouse Example](images/measure-angle-ex2.png)

### [Icon: Measure radius button](images/tool-buttons/measure-radius.png) Check Arc Radius

The **Check Arc Radius** tool shows the radius of the nearest arc.

When the **Check Arc Radius** tool is active, the nearest arc is highlighted and the radius is displayed slightly offset from the arc. A line is also drawn from the nearest point on the arc to the centre of the circle that the arc would form.

[Screenshot: measure-radius-ex1.png - Check arc radius example](images/measure-radius-ex1.png)

Clicking the mouse when an arc is highlighted will bring up a window showing the radius and length of the selected arc.

[Screenshot: measure-radius-ex2.png - Check arc radius information window example](images/measure-radius-ex2.png)

### [Icon: Measure level button](images/tool-buttons/measure-level.png) Measure Surface Level

With the **Measure Surface Level** tool you first select a surface you want to check the levels of.
Then the level of the surface point directly underneath the mouse position will be shown next to the cursor.

[Screenshot: measure-level-ex1.png - Measure surface level example](images/measure-level-ex1.png)

From the right click options menu, the Select Surface option allows you to change to a different surface.

Also on the right click menu, the Select Secondary Surface option allows you to check and compare the levels of two surfaces.
After selecting the second surface, the cursor annotation shows the level of both surfaces as well as the difference in level.

[Screenshot: measure-level-ex2.png - Measure surface levels example](images/measure-level-ex2.png)

In the longsection view this tool will automatically annotate the level difference between the proposed design levels and the existing ground surface.

[Screenshot: measure-level-longsec-ex.png - Measure surface levels in longsection example](images/measure-level-longsec-ex.png)

### [Icon: Measure grade button](images/tool-buttons/measure-grade.png) Measure Grade

Clinking the **Measure Grade** tool will bring up a surface selection window to pick which surface you want to interrogate the grade of.

After selecting a surface, the surface gradient of the point directly underneath the mouse position will be annotated with an arrow indicating the downhill direction.

[Screenshot: measure-grade-ex1.png - Measure gradient single point Example](images/measure-grade-ex1.png)

You can also measure the gradient between any two points on the current survey.

After you have clicked one point, a line will stretch from that point to the mouse cursor position. The gradient annotation is then the gradient from the first point to the point under the mouse position.

[Screenshot: measure-grade-ex2.png - Measure gradient single point Example](images/measure-grade-ex2.png)

The gradient is calculated by using difference in level on the selected surface, between the first and second coordinates, and the plan distance between the same two points.

Clicking a second time will temporarily lock the line and gradient annotation allowing you to keep it on screen.

The tool automatically resets, waiting for you to click again. To finish, press the **Escape** key, or right-click and select **Quit Command** from the menu.

### [Icon: Measure area button](images/tool-buttons/measure-area.png) Measure Area

With the **Measure Area** tool you can draw a closed boundary line to see the 2D area inside the enclosing boundary.

As you draw, the current area will be shown next to the cursor. Click to add the point under the cursor to the area boundary.

[Screenshot: measure-area-ex1.png - Measure surface level example](images/measure-area-ex1.png)

On the right click menu you can select Finish Area to stop adding points to the current area boundary.
Only the points that you have clicked to add will be included in the finished area, the point directly under the cursor is not included unless you click to add this point.

The finished areas are shown as shaded regions with the area shown at the centre of the enclosing boundary. These areas kept as long as the command is active.

[Screenshot: measure-area-ex2.png - Measure surface levels example](images/measure-area-ex2.png)

### [Icon: Entity properties button](images/tool-buttons/entity-properties.png) Entity Properties

Use this tool to see the properties of any item on the drawing.

After starting the tool, select an item from the view. They will highlight as you move the mouse around. When you select a highlighted item, the following window is shown, displaying relevant property information:

[Screenshot: entity-properties-dlg.png - Entity Properties Window](images/entity-properties-dlg.png)

Click **OK** to dismiss the window. Then select another item, or right click and choose **Quit Command** to finish.

Construction lines can be placed relative to existing items on the drawing to determine precise coordinates when placing design items, especially when used in combination with the [Snap Tools](snap-tools.htm).

[Icon: Construction Line Toolbar](images/construction-toolbar.png)

The Construction Line toolbar is available when a command is active (for example, the [New Centreline command](centreline-new.htm)). When no command is active the construction toolbar buttons are inactive.

Construction lines are temporary and are cleaned off the drawing when no command toolbar is active.

### [Icon: Construction Line through 2 points button](images/construct-2point-button.png) Construction Line Through 2 Points

Click two different points on the plan view and a construction line is drawn passing through them both.
It is usual to use on the [Snap Options](snap-tools.htm) when selecting the two points, as you will normally want the construction line placed relative to other features on the drawing.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-ex1.png - Example of 2 point construction line](images/construct-2point-ex1.png)

Hover the cursor near the first point, using with [Snap to Point](snap-tools.htm) option also active.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-ex2.png - Example of 2 point construction line](images/construct-2point-ex2.png)

Click and the construction line appears and follows the cursor until you click the second point.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-ex3.png - Example of 2 point construction line](images/construct-2point-ex3.png)

Hover mouse near the required second point with the [Snap to Point](snap-tools.htm) option also active.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-ex4.png - Example of 2 point construction line](images/construct-2point-ex4.png)

After clicking the second point the construction line is shown in blue, passing through the two chosen points, and extending off the view in both directions.

#### Longsection Gradient

The 2 point construction line can also be used in the longsection view. This can be easily accessed from the construction line tools on the right hand side of the longsection view.

After selecting the 2 point construction line, you can right click in the longsection and select the **Enter Gradient** option to bring up a gradient input window. After setting the gradient and clicking OK, the construction line will be locked to that gradient.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-longsec-ex1.png - Example of gradient-locked construction line](images/construct-2point-longsec-ex1.png)

The construction line will follow the mouse cursor whilst staying locked to the entered gradient.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-longsec-ex2.png - Example of gradient-locked construction line](images/construct-2point-longsec-ex2.png)

You can also use the snaps to help position the desired line.

[Screenshot: construct-2point-longsec-ex3.png - Example of gradient-locked construction line](images/construct-2point-longsec-ex3.png)

Click to add the shown construction line.

If you click to set the first point before using the right click option to set the gradient, the construction line will added immediately after entering the gradient. This will create a construction line which passes through that first point at the specified gradient.

### [Icon: Construction Line at parallel offset button](images/construct-parallel-button.png) Construction Line at Parallel Offset

A construction line can be placed parallel to an existing line, offset at a specific distance.

[Screenshot: construct-parallel-ex1.png - Example of parallel construction line](images/construct-parallel-ex1.png)

When you enable the [Icon: Construction Line at parallel offset button](images/construct-parallel-button.png) Parallel Construction Line option, a prompt for the **Offset** is shown at the bottom left corner of the plan view window.

[Screenshot: construct-parallel-ex2.png - Example of parallel construction line](images/construct-parallel-ex2.png)

Enter your required offset for the parallel line. If you have used the option previously, the last offset will be shown by default.

[Screenshot: construct-parallel-ex3.png - Example of parallel construction line](images/construct-parallel-ex3.png)

Hover the cursor near a line. The line will highlight to indicate that it would be selected when you click the mouse.

[Screenshot: construct-parallel-ex4.png - Example of parallel construction line](images/construct-parallel-ex4.png)

Click the mouse and the construction line is indicated.

[Screenshot: construct-parallel-ex5.png - Example of parallel construction line](images/construct-parallel-ex5.png)

The construction line is offered on whichever side of the selected line you place the cursor.

[Screenshot: construct-parallel-ex6.png - Example of parallel construction line](images/construct-parallel-ex6.png)

After clicking the mouse the construction line is shown in blue.

### [Icon: Construction Line extend button](images/construct-extend-button.png) Extended Construction Line

It is often useful to extend an existing line on the drawing, for example, to show where it intersects with another feature.

[Screenshot: construct-extend-ex1.png - Example of Extended Construction Line](images/construct-extend-ex1.png)

Hover the cursor near a line. The line will highlight to indicate that it would be selected when you click the mouse.

[Screenshot: construct-extend-ex2.png - Example of Extended Construction Line](images/construct-extend-ex2.png)

After clicking the mouse a construction line is shown in blue over the top of the selected line.
The construction line extends beyond both edges of the view.

### [Icon: Construction Line tangent button](images/construct-tangent-button.png) Tangent Construction Line

There are three types of tangential construction line: a tangent line from a point to an arc, a tangent line touching two arcs, or a tangent arc of known radius toughing two arcs.

[Screenshot: construct-tangent-menu.png - Tangent Construction right-click menu](images/construct-tangent-menu.png)

You can choose which type of tangent you want to place from the right-click menu.

#### Arc to Point Tangent

First select an existing arc or circle on the drawing. They will highlight as you move the cursor near. Click to select.

Next select a point. You can use the [Snap Options](snap-tools.htm) to latch to an existing point. As you move the cursor the proposed construction line will move around the arc, extending to the cursor position. Click to place the construction line.

*Note*: There are two possible construction lines from a point to a circle. It will be the one towards the point where you clicked to select the arc.

#### Arc to Arc Tangent Line

First select an existing arc or circle on the drawing. They will highlight as you move the cursor near. Click to select.

Then select another arc or circle on the drawing. As you move the cursor, the proposed construction line will show. Click to place the construction line.

*Note*: There are four possible construction lines between two circles. The one offered will be the line which is closest to the cursor position.

#### Arc to Arc Tangent Radius

First select an existing arc or circle on the drawing. They will highlight as you move the cursor near. Click to select.

Then select another arc or circle on the drawing.

Then select which tangent solution you require. The choices will appear as you move the cursor in between the two selected arcs. Click to select.

*Note*: There are eight possible construction arcs between two circles. The one offered will be the arc which is closest to the cursor position.

### [Icon: Construction Circle button](images/construct-circle-button.png) Construction Circle

This option allows you to place a construction circle on the drawing. There are two ways to make the circle:

[Screenshot: construct-circle-menu.png - Construction Circle right-click menu](images/construct-circle-menu.png)

You can choose which way to make the circle from the right-click menu.

#### Centre and Radius

First select the centre point for the circle. You may want to use one of the [Snap Options](snap-tools.htm) to latch to an existing feature.

After selecting the centre point, the circle will stretch to pass through the cursor position. As you move the cursor, the offered circle will increase or decrease in radius accordingly.
Click to place the construction circle. Or, to enter a specific radius, you can right-click and pick the "Enter Radius" option from the menu.

#### Three Points

Simply select three points in succession (you may want to use the [Snap Options](snap-tools.htm) to latch to existing features) and a construction circle will be placed which passes through all three points.

#### Two Items and Mouse

You will be prompted to select two items (either straight lines or arcs).

After selecting the two items, the circle will stretch to pass through the cursor position. As you move the cursor, the offered circle will move accordingly accordingly, so as to be tangential to the two items and pass through the mouse position. Click to place the construction circle.

#### Two Items and Radius

You will be prompted to select two items (either straight lines or arcs).
After selecting the two items, you will be prompted to enter the required radius.

[Screenshot: enter-radius-dlg.png - Enter radius window](images/enter-radius-dlg.png)

After entering the radius, the tangent circle will be shown near to the cursor position (if any tangent solution exists for the given items and radius).

There may be multiple tangent circles that touch the two items with the given radius. As you move the cursor around, the nearest solution is displayed. Click to place the construction circle.

#### Three Items

You will be prompted to select two items (either straight lines or arcs).
While selecting the third item, the tangent circle will be shown near to the cursor position (if any tangent solution exists for the given three items). Click to place the construction circle.

### [Icon: Delete Construction Lines button](images/construct-delete.png) Delete Construction Lines

This option allows you to delete construction lines that you have placed. After selecting this tool, click a construction line to delete it.

[Screenshot: construct-delete-menu.png - Delete Construction Lines right-click menu](images/construct-delete-menu.png)

The Delete All option on the right-click menu will delete every placed construction line.
