//<!--
show\_header('Mitre Corner on Channel/Footpath');
show\_index();
//-->

The mitre corner tool is available when editing the horizontal design of a channel or footway. From the [[Icon: Channel Tools button](images/channel-tools-button.png)Channel Tools](channel-tools.htm) click on the [[Screenshot: channel-horizontals.png - Edit channel horizontal design button](images/tool-buttons/channel-horizontals.png)Edit Channel Horizontal Design](channel-tools.htm) button. Then select a channel or back-of-footway line to modify.

From the channel edit toolbar, press the [Icon: Mitre Corner button](images/tool-buttons/channel-mitre.png) Mitre Corner button.

The following window is shown:

[Screenshot: channel-mitre-dlg.png - Channel mitre window](images/channel-mitre-dlg.png)

As you move the mouse over the Plan View you will see that the chainage selector is in operation on your chosen design line. It is awaiting you to pick the chainage position to indicate the start direction of the mitre.

After you have picked the start chainage, it will be awaiting for you to pick the chainage position to indicate the end direction of the mitre.

### Start and End Chainage

The **mitre** corner is formed by intersecting the direction/tangent of the line at the **Start Chainage** with the direction/tangent of the line at the **End Chainage**.

The following images illustrates how this can be used to easily square off a corner:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex1.png - Road requiring mitred corner](images/channel-mitre-ex1.png) | This image shows a road with standard parallel channels. The layout indicates that the outer channels of these bends should be squared off. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex2.png - Start and End of Mitre](images/channel-mitre-ex2.png) | With the mitre corner tool, pick a chainage point along the straights before and after the arc to extend the straight lines to the intersection point. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex4.png - Result of mitre](images/channel-mitre-ex4.png) | This image shows the resultant road after the **Apply Changes** button [Icon: Apply Changes button](images/tool-buttons/apply-changes.png) has been clicked on the Channels toolbar. |

### Radius

A radius can be specified to fillet the new corner created by a mitre corner item.

The following example, shows how to use a filleted mitre to widen the inner arc on a tight bend.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre2-ex1.png - Road requiring mitred corner](images/channel-mitre2-ex1.png) | This image shows a road with standard parallel channels. The layout drawing indicates the inner curve of the bend should be widened with a larger radius. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre2-ex2.png - Start and End of Mitre](images/channel-mitre2-ex2.png) | A mitre corner can be used to specify the tangent lines of the new arc, by extending the straight lines before and after the arc tangent points. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre2-ex3.png - Start and End of Mitre](images/channel-mitre2-ex3.png) | You can then enter the desired fillet radius, or use the radius *Pick* button to select the arc from the drawing. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre2-ex5.png - Result of mitre](images/channel-mitre2-ex5.png) | This image shows the resultant road after the **Apply Changes** button [Icon: Apply Changes button](images/tool-buttons/apply-changes.png) has been clicked on the Channels toolbar. |

The design path constructs created using the Channel/Footpath tools are remembered.
If you subsequently make any other change to the designed site that would affect this road, then it will be updated automatically to cope with that change.

### Start and End Offset

A mitre corner item can have a different **Start Offset** and **End Offset**, allowing you to change the channel or footpath width around a corner.

This can be used to create a different footpath width at the end of a road as shown below:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-end-cap0.png - Road requiring mitred corner](images/channel-mitre-end-cap0.png) | This image shows the end cap of a road with a standard parallel footway. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-end-cap1.png - Road requiring mitred corner](images/channel-mitre-end-cap1.png) | Set the **Mitre** start and end chainages to points either side of the corner. Then change the relevant offset to the desired footway width. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-end-cap2.png - Start and End of Mitre](images/channel-mitre-end-cap2.png) | This image shows the resultant footway after the **Apply Changes** button [Icon: Apply Changes button](images/tool-buttons/apply-changes.png) has been clicked on the Channels toolbar. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-end-cap3.png - Result of widened mitre](images/channel-mitre-end-cap3.png) | Apply the same process to the opposite footway to have the footway width applied across the entire endcap. |

Or create a channel widening around a bend in the road:

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex5-1.png - Road requiring mitred corner](images/channel-mitre-ex5-1.png) | This image shows a road with standard parallel channels. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex5-2.png - Start and End of Mitre](images/channel-mitre-ex5-2.png) | Set the **Mitre** start and end chainages either side of a corner. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex5-3.png - Start and End of Mitre](images/channel-mitre-ex5-3.png) | Use the **End Offset** *Pick* button to select the desired new road width on the inner side of the bend. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex5-4.png - Result of mitre](images/channel-mitre-ex5-4.png) | Use the **Radius** *Pick* button to select the desired fillet radius on the inner side of the bend. |
| [Screenshot: channel-mitre-ex5-5.png - Result of widened mitre](images/channel-mitre-ex5-5.png) | This image shows the resultant road after the **Apply Changes** button [Icon: Apply Changes button](images/tool-buttons/apply-changes.png) has been clicked on the Channels toolbar. |

The channel item edit tool is available when editing the horizontal design of a channel or footway. [Screenshot: channel-edit-ex1.png - Channel edit selection example](images/channel-edit-ex1.png)

After clicking on an item a window will pop up allowing you to change the properties of the selected item. This will depend on which item was clicked, see the following help pages for more information:

//<!--
show\_header('Delete a Channel or Footway Item');
show\_index();
//-->

The Delete Item tool is available when editing the horizontal design of a channel or footway. From the [[Icon: Channel Tools button](images/channel-tools-button.png)Channel Tools](channel-tools.htm) click on the [[Screenshot: channel-horizontals.png - Edit channel horizontal design button](images/tool-buttons/channel-horizontals.png)Edit Channel Horizontal Design](channel-tools.htm) button. Then select a channel or back-of-footway line to modify.

From the channel edit toolbar, press the [Icon: Delete Item button](images/tool-buttons/channel-delete.png)Delete Item button.

You can now use the mouse to select a channel/footway item.

*Note:* This tool does not delete amorphous items. Click the [Delete Amorphous button [Icon: Delete Amorphous button](images/tool-buttons/channel-amorphous-delete.png)](channel-amorphous.htm#delete) to delete an amorphous item.

[Screenshot: channel-edit-ex1.png - Channel edit selection example](images/channel-edit-ex1.png)

As you move the mouse you may notice the items drawing in different colours. Green items are [Parallels](channel-parallel.htm), blue items are [Widenings/Narrowings](channel-widen.htm), and yellow items are [Mitre Corners](channel-mitre.htm). The item that the mouse is currently over is indicated with a red/orange highlight.

You can then click the highlighted item to delete it. However, there are some situations where the selected item cannot be deleted:

* A parallel or widening that has a mitre attached to it.
* A parallel that has its offset defined by another item.

  
  

When a mitre is deleted the surrounding items will be extended to the intersection point of the mitre:

[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex1.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex2.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex2.png)

When it is not possible to extend the surrounding items to the intersection point, the mitre is deleted and replaced with a single parallel. For example, when there is no possible intersection within the mitre chainage range, or when the mitre IP is on the opposite side of the centreline:

[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex3.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex3.png)
[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex4.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex4.png)

*Note:* When any new item is added, any existing items that are fully overlapped are lost. Deleting the item that replaced them will *not* restore the original items.

  
  

When any other item is deleted it is replaced with the constant offset from the previous item. For example, deleting a widening that was added to a parallel will result in a single parallel:

[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex5.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex5.png)
[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex6.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex6.png)

The offset which is used in this situation is based on the forward chainage direction of the centreline. This can be toggled from the right click menu to work in the reverse chainage direction:

[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-rclickmenu.png](images/channel-delete-item-rclickmenu.png)

Using the same example as above, except with the "Delete in forward chainage direction" option un-ticked, the next items offset will be used instead:

[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex5.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex5.png)
[Screenshot: channel-delete-item-ex7.png](images/channel-delete-item-ex7.png)
  
  

The right click menu also allows you to toggle whether the command finishes after deleting an item. If you untick "Quit command after deleting item", you will stay in the command and can delete multiple items in a row.

//<!--
show\_header('Amorphous Channel Item');
show\_index();
//-->

Nearly all possible shapes of channel/footpath can be constructed by using the [Widening](channel-widen.htm), [Parallel](channel-parallel.htm), and [Mitre Corner](channel-mitre.htm) design tools. These tools allow you to add design items that are based on centreline chainage and offset from the centreline. So, when the associated centreline is repositioned, designs using these items update with these changes as they keep the same relative position to the centreline. This makes them very flexible and you mayHowever, a limitation to the chainage and offset approach is that the items cannot overlap or reverse the chainage direction. To input designs that cannot be achieved due to this limitation, the amorphous design tool must be used instead.

With the amorphous tool you can draw lines to define portions of the channel. These lines stay locked to the project coordinates that that they were added at, so the standard design items may be preferred for the bulk of the design as they are generally more flexible with changes to a layout.

Below is an example where the channel zig-zags and the corresponding centreline chainage goes back and forth along the channel which has been input using the amorphous design tool:

[Screenshot: amorphous-ex1.png - Example of backwards channel](images/amorphous-ex1.png)

The amorphous design tool is available when editing the horizontal design of a channel or footway. From the [[Icon: Channel Tools button](images/channel-tools-button.png)Channel Tools](channel-tools.htm) click on the [[Screenshot: channel-horizontals.png - Edit channel horizontal design button](images/tool-buttons/channel-horizontals.png)Edit Channel Horizontal Design](channel-tools.htm) button. Then select a channel or back-of-footway line to modify.

From the channel edit toolbar, press the [Icon: Add amorphous item button](images/tool-buttons/channel-amorphous.png)Amorphous button to open up the amorphous design tools. The following toolbar will appear to the right of the channel design toolbar:

[Screenshot: new-line.png - New amorphous button](images/tool-buttons/new-line.png)
[Screenshot: add-point.png - Add amorphous point button](images/tool-buttons/add-point.png)
[Screenshot: add-arc.png - Add amorphous curve button](images/tool-buttons/add-arc.png)
[Screenshot: move-point.png - Move amorphous point button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png)
[Screenshot: delete-point.png - Delete amorphous point button](images/tool-buttons/delete-point.png)
[Icon: toolbar spacer](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-spacer.png)
[Icon: Close toolbar button](images/tool-buttons/toolbar-close.png)

The amorphous design tools work just like [the centreline design tools](centreline-tools.htm).

### Drawing an amorphous design

Pressing the [Icon: New amorphous button](images/tool-buttons/new-line.png) New Amorphous button will allow you to draw the line you want to splice into the channel.

When drawing the amorphous line it will show as a red line. The amorphous line overwrites any underlying design and, if it isn't touching the underlying channel at the start and end points of the line, it will get connected with a perpendicular line to tie it in.

[Screenshot: amorphous-ex2.png - Standard channel design](images/amorphous-ex2.png)
[Screenshot: amorphous-ex3.png - Standard channel design with new amorphous](images/amorphous-ex3.png)
[Screenshot: amorphous-ex4.png - Finished amorphous design](images/amorphous-ex4.png)

### Select Channel Line From Layout

When you are drawing a new amorphous line you can right click to get some additional options. The "Select Line" option on the right click menu will allow you to pick a polyline from a drawing to copy the design from.

[Screenshot: amorphous-select-line-ex1.png - Standard channel design](images/amorphous-select-line-ex1.png)
  
  

### Delete Amorphous Line

From the channel edit toolbar, press the [[Icon: Add amorphous item button](images/tool-buttons/channel-amorphous.png)Delete Amorphous](#delete) button to delete any amorphous lines that have been added to the channel.

If there are any amorphous items on your design the closest amorphous design line will highlight as you move the cursor near it. Click the mouse to delete the highlighted amorphous line.

A **secondary channel** is used to add design features like laybys and parking bays to the the side of a road or footpath. In particular this is used where the grade of the feature is different to that of the main road carriageway (for example, where a parking bay's grade falls to the primary channel) or where the feature has an upstand (small kerb) at the join with the primary channel.
The secondary channel defines the shape of the design feature.

You can add a new secondary channel from the [[Icon: Secondary Channels Tools button](images/secondary-channels-button.png) secondary channels toolbar](secondary-channel-tools.htm).

After pressing the [Icon: New Secondary Channel button](images/tool-buttons/secondary-channel-new.png) Add Secondary Channel button you will see the following message appear on the right of the toolbar indicating that you should select a channel or footway:

[Screenshot: secondary-channel-prompt.png - Channel Select Toolbar](images/secondary-channel-prompt.png)

You can now select which road channel or secondary channel onto which you want to add the new secondary channel.
As you move the mouse over the drawing the channel or secondary channel closest to the cursor will highlight.
Click the mouse to select the desired item.

You will then see this toolbar with the options for adding the new secondary channel.

[Screenshot: secondary-channel.png - Secondary Channel Editing Tools](images/mode-buttons/secondary-channel.png)

//<!--
show\_header('Secondary Channel Properties');

//-->

This option is for modifying the vertical properties of a Secondary Channel.

On the [Icon: Secondary Channels Tools button](images/secondary-channels-button.png) secondary channels toolbar, click the [Icon: Secondary Channel Properties button](images/tool-buttons/secondary-channel-properties.png) Secondary Channel Properties button.

You can now select which secondary channel to query.
As you move the mouse over the drawing the secondary channel closest to the cursor will highlight.
Click the mouse to select the desired item.

The following window is shown:

[Screenshot: secondary-channel-properties-dlg.png - Secondary Channels Properties Window](images/secondary-channel-properties-dlg.png)

### Grade

The secondary channel grade defines the gradient slope across the road, from the primary channel, to the bottom of the kerb. A positive value
(for example, 1:40) means the carriageway slopes down from the primary channel to the secondary channel. A negative value
(for example, 1:-40) mean the carriageway slopes up from the primary channel to the secondary channel.

The default is for the primary channel to be higher than the secondary channel.

### Upstand

The secondary channel upstand defines the height of a small kerb that runs along the primary channel in between the it and the
secondary channel.

  

When you click the OK button, your road will be recalculated to use the new design, and any other aspect of the design (for example a nearby junction) will be automatically recalculated to accommodate the changes.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Longsection Properties');
show\_index();
//-->

The longsection properties window allows you to adjust the contents and display settings of the current longsection view.

### Opening the Longsection Properties

While a longsection view window is active, click on the **Longsection Properties** button ([Icon: Longsection Properties button](images/tool-buttons/longsection-properties.png)) from the view toolbar on the right edge of the window.

Alternatively, you can right-click within the longsection view window and select **Section Properties** from the drop-down menu:
[Screenshot: longsection-menu.png - Longsection right-click menu](images/longsection-menu.png)

You should see the window below:

[Screenshot: longsection-properties-dlg.png - Longsection Properties window](images/longsection-properties-dlg.png)
  
  

### Section Display Items

From this list you can toggle which section items are drawn in the current longsection.

For example, you can toggle the drawing of specific ground sections or the channel lines of a centreline longsection.

The longsection drawing will be updated and show the changes immediately.

  

### Table Annotation Rows

From this list you can toggle which table rows are annotated for the current longsection.

For example, you can toggle the annotation row for existing ground levels.

The longsection table will be updated and show the changes immediately.

  

### Section Options

#### Vertical Exaggeration

Vertical Exaggeration allows you to set the scaling of the longsection along the vertical axis.

#### Datum

The Datum box will be grayed-out by default but will display the datum that was automatically selected by Site3D according to the level range of the section drawing.

If you tick the Manual check-box adjacent to the datum text field, the text field will become active allowing you to enter a specific datum level of your choosing.

  

### Section Extension

Here you can specify additional section length to include in the section, projected backward/forward from the start/end points of the section path.

**Backwards** specifies the projected distance beyond the *start chainage* for which the ground profile and other design items are shown.**Forwards** specifies the projected distance beyond the *end chainage* for which the ground profile and other design items are shown.

  

### Drainage

From the drainage section you can toggle the projected drainage along the length of the centreline as well as the inclusion of drainage rows in the longsection.

Note: Drainage crossing through the section get drawn as pipe intersections instead of projecting along the section. This is toggled separately from the Section Display Items list, **Conduit Intersections**.

#### Surface Drainage, Foul Drainage

These items toggle the inclusion of the drainage network on the longsection. If ticked that drainage network will get projected along the centreline longsection and the associated table rows will be included.

If there are no manholes which can be projected or both the Surface Drainage tickbox and Foul Drainage tickbox are unticked, no projected drainage will be shown and no drainage rows will be included in the table.

#### Private Drainage

This toggles whether manholes that are marked as private will be included.

#### Maximum Manhole Offset

This specifies the maximum distance a manhole can be in order for it to be projected onto the longsection. Specifically, this is the maximum perpendicular distance from any point along the centreline.

For example, with a value of 15m, any manholes that lie within 15m from any point along the road centreline will be included in the longsection drawing. Manholes that are beyond the specified distance will not be included.

#### Show Empty Rows

When only one of the drainage network types is included in the longsection (either only surface drainage or only foul drainage), this option toggles whether to include an empty annotation row where the missing drainage network type would be annotated.

This allows you to have a consistent drainage row layout across centreline longsections, even if one of the drainage types in not present in the longsection view.

//<!--
show\_header('Road Vertical Alignment - Move Point');
show\_index();

This option is for modifying the vertical alignment of a road centreline, by moving one or more vertical IPs (Intersection Points).

On the [Icon: Road Tools button](images/centreline-tools-button.png) centreline tools toolbar in a longsection window, click the [Icon: Centreline Move IP button](images/tool-buttons/move-point.png) move point button.

Select a vertical IP (they highlight as you move the mouse cursor around the longsection view).
Then move the mouse to reposition the point.

[Screenshot: vertical-move-point-example.png - Move vertical IP example](images/vertical-move-point-example.png)

The selected vertical IP may already be filleted with a [vertical curve](longsection-fillet.htm). In this case you will see the curve moving with the mouse too.
Click the left mouse button to place the IP/curve at the new location.

To place the points accurately, you can use the various *snap* options on the [snap toolbar](snap-tools.htm).

Also, you can right-click the mouse to get a menu where you have more advanced options:
can select **Enter Coordinate** to type the desired chainage and level on the keyboard.

[Screenshot: longsection-menu-move-point.png - Right-click menu for moving new IP](images/longsection-menu-move-point.png)

### Enter Chainage/Level

[Screenshot: enter-chainage-level-dlg.png - Window for entering a chainage and level via the keyboard](images/enter-chainage-level-dlg.png)

Select **Enter Chainage/Level** to type the desired chainage and Level on the keyboard for this vertical IP.

### Enter Gradient...

[Screenshot: enter-gradient-dlg.png - Window for entering a gradient via the keyboard](images/enter-gradient-dlg.png)

Select **Enter Gradient** to type the desired gradient on the keyboard for this vertical IP. By default it will allow you to enter a chainage and new gradient on the left side of the new IP, but you can also choose to enter the chainage and right hand gradient or enter the left and right gradients to find the new chainage and level by intersecting the two chosen gradients from the next and previous vertical points.

### Lock Chainage Only

Whilst moving the vertical IP, you can flag the chainage of the selected IP as locked. This allows you to keep the chainage of the vertical IP whilst visually picking the level with the mouse. Clicking this menu item toggles this feature on/off.

### Lock Level Only

Whilst moving the vertical IP, you can flag the level of the selected IP as locked. This allows you to keep the level of the vertical IP whilst visually picking the chainage with the mouse. Clicking this menu item toggles this feature on/off.

### Lock Preceding Gradient

Whilst moving the vertical IP, you can flag the preceding gradient of the selected IP as locked. This allows you to move the vertical IP with the mouse whilst maintaining the gradient preceding the vertical IP. Clicking this menu item toggles this feature on/off.

### Lock Following Gradient

Whilst moving the vertical IP, you can flag the following gradient of the selected IP as locked. This allows you to move the vertical IP with the mouse whilst maintaining the gradient following the vertical IP. Clicking this menu item toggles this feature on/off.

You can change the road centreline vertical path at any time during the design process. Site3D will work out all the knock-on effects and update your site accordingly.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Ramp and Steps Designer');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility allows you to add ramps and steps into a centreline vertical alignment.

The design is based on the UK Building Regulations, Approved Document M - Access to and use of buildings.

To use the ramps and steps designer tool, click the [Icon: Ramp and Steps Designer button](images/tool-buttons/ramp-and-steps-designer.png) button on the centreline toolbar in a longsection view.

After pressing the toolbar button, the following setting-out properties window is shown:

[Screenshot: ramp-and-steps-designer-dlg.png - Ramp and step designer window](images/ramp-and-steps-designer-dlg.png)

### Design Type

Here you can select either a ramp design or a step design to create.

### Design Limits

This defines the limits of the design that will be created.

#### Start Point

**From Chainage**: The start point of the ramp or step design.  
**From Level**: The start level of the ramp or step design.

#### End Point

**To Chainage**: The end point of the ramp or step design.  
**To Level**: The end level of the ramp or step design.

### Landings

This specifies the length of landings between flights of ramps or steps. The default value for steps and ramps is taken from the access requirements specified in the building regulations.

For ramps this defaults to 1.2m. Ramps between 1:12 and 1:15 can only be up to 5m in length before requiring a minimum of 1.2m length level landing, ramps between 1:15 and 1:20 can only be up to 10m in length before requiring a minimum of 1.2m length level landing. For stepped access a flight of steps can rise no more than 1.8m before require a minimum of 0.9m length level landing.

[Screenshot: ramp-and-steps-designer-ex1.png - Ramp Design Preview](images/ramp-and-steps-designer-ex1.png)
[Screenshot: ramp-and-steps-designer-ex2.png - Ramp Design Accepted](images/ramp-and-steps-designer-ex2.png)

### Step Design

Specifies the stepped access design requirements. The default values for the stepped access design are taken from the stepped access requirements specified in the building regulations.

**Minimum Step Going**: This specifies the minimum step horizontal length  
**Minimum Step Rise**: This specifies the minimum step vertical rise  
**Maximum Step Rise**: This specifies the maximum step vertical rise

The stepped design that's created uses the minimum and maximum vertical rise of the steps to calculate the number of steps required.
The step rise of the generated design will be a value between the two that achieves the required overall rise.
The step going specifies the minimum allowable horizontal length for each step, the step going of the generated design will be greater than or equal to this value as the step going will be the path horizontal length evenly distributed across the required number of steps.

[Screenshot: ramp-and-steps-designer-ex3.png - Step Design Preview](images/ramp-and-steps-designer-ex3.png)
[Screenshot: ramp-and-steps-designer-ex4.png - Step Design Accepted](images/ramp-and-steps-designer-ex4.png)

//<!--
show\_header('Junction Visibility Splay');
show\_index();
//-->

This option is for adding or modifying the visibility splay for a junction.

The visibility splay shows the region which should be kept clear of visual obstructions so that a driver waiting at the junction can see a safe distance in both direction along the road to be entered.

To access the tools for modifying junction visibility splays, click the [Icon: Junction Tools button](images/junction-tools-button.png) junction tools button.

On the junction toolbar, click the [Icon: Junction Visibility Splay button](images/tool-buttons/junction-visibility.png) Visibility Splay button and select the junction you want to modify, by clicking near it on the plan view.

The following window is shown:

[Screenshot: junction-visibility-window.png - Junction Visibility Window](images/junction-visibility-window.png)

### Visibility Distances

In this window you can edit the distance back from the junction and the distance along the joined-onto road using the edit boxes on the preview.

The splay lines are shown on the junction for the required distances.

Depending on the shape of the road passing by the junction, the splay is either drawn to a point at the given distance along the road channel, or the the tangent with the road channel line. In certain cases, when both are needed, both are automatically shown.

### Left same as Right

By default the left and right sides are mirrored, but if you wish to have a different visibility distance on the left or right, untick the "Left same as right" tickbox at the top middle of the window. This will allow you to change the left/right independantly of each other.

### Enabled

If you want to remove existing visibility splays, simply untick the "Enabled" tickbox at the top left of the window. The effect is immediately shown on the preview to confirm your choice.

### OK/Cancel

Once you are happy with the values and visibility splays, simply click the "OK" button to accept your changes and open a longsection showing a section along the visibility splay lines:

[Screenshot: junction-visibility-longsec.png - Junction Visibility Section](images/junction-visibility-longsec.png)

If press "Cancel" if you wish to exit without changing anything.

You can change the junction visibility splays any time during the design process.

//<!--
show\_header('Junction Verticals');
show\_index();
//-->

When a junction is created, it is levelled to match the the channel/footway that is being joined onto.
The channel levels are then smoothly transitioned from the level at the point that they join to their specified crossfalls.

By default, junctions are vertically levelled over the horizontal length of the junctioning curve.
If this is less than 3m, then 3m is used instead.

With the junction verticals tool you can manually specify the horizontal length to smooth over.

Click on the **Junction Verticals** tool [Icon: Junction Verticals button](images/tool-buttons/junction-verticals.png).
The tool prompts you to select a junction side, as you move the mouse the the nearest channel line is highlighted.

[Screenshot: junction-verticals-ex.png - Select a junction side to edit](images/junction-verticals-ex.png)

After selecting a channel side to edit you will see the Junction Verticals window with the automatic settings filled in.

[Screenshot: junction-verticals-dlg-auto.png - Junction verticals window automatic settings](images/junction-verticals-dlg-auto.png)

If you untick the "Auto" checkbox you will be able to input the channel distance.

[Screenshot: junction-verticals-dlg-pick.png - Junction verticals window manual settings](images/junction-verticals-dlg-pick.png)

Pressing the "Pick" button will allow you to pick the desired levelling distance from the plan view as shown:

[Screenshot: junction-verticals-pick.png - Select manual levelling length from plan view](images/junction-verticals-pick.png)

After selecting a channel length from the plan view you will be returned to the Junction Verticals window with the selected distance.
Clicking OK will apply the manual levelling distance.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Create Final Surface');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility automatically generates the complete *final surface* for your entire site design.

The *final surface* is comprised of the top of design surfaces of all the design items on the site.

To create the final surface click on the Create Final Surface button [Icon: Create final surface button](images/tool-buttons/final-surface.png) on the [Surfaces and Volumes toolbar [Icon: Surfaces and Volumes Tools button](images/surface-volume-tools-button.png)](surface-volume-tools.htm).

The design features used to generate a final surface are:

* the site boundary,
* roads, footways and paths,
* interfacing slopes,
* building pads,
* external works design lines,
* ponds and earthworks.

[Screenshot: final-surface-ex1.png](images/final-surface-ex1.png)[Screenshot: final-surface-ex2.png](images/final-surface-ex2.png)

### Final Surface

When you select the **Create Final Surface** option, a new submodel will be generated with the name *Final Surface*. You will see the Final Surface model in the [layers](layers-dlg.htm) window along with the other submodels you may have imported into the project.

Each time you select the **Create Final Surface** option, the *Final Surface* model will be updated to reflect the current state of the site design.

The final surface can be used in the [Surface->Volumes...](surface-volume.htm) facility to create accurate cut and fill comparisons against other surfaces.

### Site Boundary

In order to define the extent of the final surface, it is useful to indicate the site boundary.

You can do this by digitising a line around the known site boundary using the [New Centreline tool](centreline-new.htm) and then levelling the centreline to the existing surface (using the **Road->Centreline Extras->Level to Surface...** menu option).

//<!--
show\_header('Interface Delete Region');
show\_index();
//-->

To remove an interfacing region from a side of a road, first click the
[Icon: Interface Tools button](images/interface-tools-button.png)
button on the main toolbar and then the [Icon: Delete Interface Region button](images/tool-buttons/interface-delete-region.png)
button on the [Interface Options toolbar](interface.htm).

You will be prompted to select an existing interface region from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest road interface region is highlighted.
Click to select.

The selected interface region will be removed from the road and the overall design is automatically regenerated accordingly.
The regions either side of the deleted region will automatically stretch to fill the deleted region.

(*Note*: The *Undo* option may be used to restore the design, if you need to.)

Whereas the [Create Interfacing](interface-new.htm) facility does both sides of the road by default,
the Delete Interface Region function removes from only one side at a time.

The earthwork editing facilites are accessed by clicking the [Icon: Earthworks Tools button](images/earthworks-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar. ([See here for more details](earthwork-tools.htm))

To edit the Earthwork properties, click the [Icon: Earthwork Properties button](images/tool-buttons/properties.png) button on the earthwork toolbar.

### [Icon: Earthwork Properties button](images/tool-buttons/properties.png) Earthwork Properties

As soon as you click this button, one of the two properties window shown below will appear:

### Standard Earthwork Properties

[Screenshot: earthworks\_properties\_dlg\_1.png - Earthwork Properties Window](images/earthworks\_properties\_dlg\_1.png)

### Pond/Tank Properties

In addition to the standard earthwork properties, the water level of ponds and tanks can be specified. For tanks, the **Type** dropdown box is disabled.

[Screenshot: earthworks\_properties\_dlg\_2.png - Pond Properties Window](images/earthworks\_properties\_dlg\_2.png)
  
  

### General Information

Name: This is a user definable name for this specific earthwork/pond.
  
Type: Shows the current eathwork type and allows you to change between a standard earthwork or a pond. Note: This is disabled for tanks.

### Volumes/Area

The three windows here show the cut and fill volumes compared to the current survey as well as the horizontal 2D
surface area of this earthwork/pond.

### Display

You can change how this pond/earthwork is drawn simply by clicking the **style** dropdown menu and choosing one of the available options.

* [Screenshot: earthwork-height-colouring.png - Height Colouring](images/earthwork-height-colouring.png)
    
  **Height Colouring**:  
  Surface coloured from Max to Min colour depending on relative height.
* [Screenshot: earthwork-grey-fill.png - Grey Fill](images/earthwork-grey-fill.png)
    
  **Single Colour**:  
  Surface drawn in single flat colour.
* [Screenshot: earthwork-lines-only.png - Height Colouring](images/earthwork-lines-only.png)
    
  **Lines Only**:  
  Surface hidden so only the design lines shown.
* [Screenshot: earthwork-cut-fill.png - Height Colouring](images/earthwork-cut-fill.png)
    
  **Cut/Fill Depth**:  
  Surface coloured in bands according to it's cut/fill depth. The cut and fill colours can be chosen from the properties window along with the thickness of the bands by changing the **band depth** value.

### Water Storage

These settings/values are only accessible for **ponds** and **tanks**.

You can specify a **water level** here for pond entities this will update the water volume and wetted area. The **water volume** shows how much storage this pond would hold at the specified water level. The **wetted area** shows how much surface area is under the specified water line.
If the water level window is empty, this means that either no water level has been entered, or the water level is not within the Z extents of the designed pond.

You can alternatively set a **freeboard** value for pond entities, which is the vertical distance above the water level to the maximum possible water level.

For **tanks**, you can set the porosity ratio. This represents the amount of space available for water storage relative to the total (empty + solid) volume in the tank.

### Infiltration

These settings/values are only accessible for **ponds** and **tanks**.

Specify the infiltration rates in m/s that will infiltrate into the pond/tank structure.

The **Base Rate** is applied to any 3D surface flatter than 1 in 10 that is within the water storage area. The **Side Rate** is applied to any surface area steeper than 1 in 10.

you move the mouse it will show a preview of where the earthwork will move too. Then simply left click
again to pick the destination point for the translation and the entire earthwork will move and any
interfacing will automatically update.

If you want to type in translation values, right click and choose **Enter Values..**.

[Screenshot: translate-dialog.png - Earthwork Translate Window](images/translate-dialog.png)

The above window will show where you can pick X, Y and/or Z translation values. Clicking **OK**
will apply the entered values and the entire earthwork will move and automatically update.

The earthwork editing facilites are accessed by clicking the [Icon: Earthworks Tools button](images/earthworks-tools-button.png) button on the main toolbar. ([See here for more details](earthwork-tools.htm))

To rotate an entire earthworks design, click the [Icon: Rotate entire earthwork button](images/tool-buttons/object-rotate.png) button on the earthwork toolbar.

### [Icon: Moves an entire earthwork](images/tool-buttons/object-rotate.png) Rotate Entire Earthwork

Once in the command, left once on a reference point to indicate the rotation origin (point around which
the entire object will rotate). Move the mouse and choose a reference angle. Now when you move the mouse
it will show a preview of where the earthwork will rotate too. Then simply left click again to pick
the destination point for the rotation and the entire earthwork will move and any interfacing will
automatically update.

If you right click, you can choose the **Rotate** option which will show the window below. This option
is useful if you know an exact angle you wish to rotate by.

[Screenshot: house-insert-rotate-dialog.png - Earthwork rotate window](images/house-insert-rotate-dialog.png)

You can type in (or **pick** with the mouse) a rotation origin (the point around which the earthwork will rotate)
and you can also type in (or **pick** with the mouse) a rotation angle.

//<!--
show\_header('Manhole Properties');
show\_index();
//-->

This option shows the properties of a manhole and allows you to modify them.

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/drainage-button.png) drainage tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Edit Manhole button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-manhole-properties.png) manhole properties button.

You will be prompted to select a manhole from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest manhole is highlighted.
Click to select.
The manhole properties window is shown.

[Screenshot: manhole-properties-dlg.png - Manhole properties window](images/manhole-properties-dlg.png)

*Note*: To edit the properties of many manholes at once, use the [Manhole Properties Schedule](drainage-manhole-properties-schedule.htm).

### Type

By default, each new manhole is added as a chamber. Using this dropdown you can alter the type, the currently supported types are:

* Chamber
* [Headwall](#headwall)
* [Deep Bore Soakaway](#soakaway)

Each type has unique options you can alter in this window.

### Name

By default, each new manhole is allocated a name comprising a prefix of either S for storm sewer networks, or F for foul sewer network, followed by the next sequential number for the network. For example, S1, S2, S3, ... S23, S24, and so on. Private manhole labels auto-name with a prefix of PS and PF instead of S and F, so that the adoptable manhole number sequence is not intermixed with private manhole numbers. Both the adoptable and private/plot prefixes are user-configurable. See the [Configuration Optionsfor more information.](settings-configuration.htm)

You can change the name to anything else, as may be appropriate for your manhole. The name may contain spaces, if desired.
The manhole name is displayed on the plan view, next to the manhole.

*Note*: If you want to renumber the entire drainage network, use the [Manhole Renumbering facility](drainage-renumbering.htm).

### Phase

This shows the current phase number for the manhole.
Initially, there are is only one phase (0) for all new manholes. You can choose to alter the phase number so that one or more manholes are on different phases so that the [manhole schedule](drainage.htm#manhole_schedule) and [drainage schedule](drainage.htm#drainage_schedule) are split out into phases. The phase is a numeric value.

### Cover Level

By default the Cover Level is determined automatically. Where the manhole is situated within the designed surface then that will be used for the cover level, otherwise the existing ground surface level is used.

If you need to fix the cover level at a specific value, you can select the **Manual** option and enter your desired cover level.
When the manual cover level is set, it will not change if the manhole is moved to a different location.

### Depth To Soffit

This shows the depth-to-soffit of the outgoing pipe of the manhole. This value is often important for determining the manhole construction type. Changing this value will alter the invert level of this manhole.

### Invert Level

This displays the current invert level at the base of the manhole. When the manhole is first placed, the invert level is set so that the soffit level of the joining conduits are 1.2m below the design surface at the centre of the manhole (or 0.6m below the existing ground if the manhole is not under the design surface). Altering this value will automatically update the depth to soffit value.

You can enter a different invert level, as required.

*Note*: Changing the manhole invert level will also change the level of the joining conduits.
The conduits join into the base of the manhole, with their soffit levels aligned.

### Catch Pit Depth

By default the manhole's invert level is set to the lowest invert level of its joining conduits.
However, if the manhole requires a catch pit, an additional catch pit depth can be specified.
The manhole will be drawn extending below its joining conduits by the given depth.

### Diameter / Width / Length

This shows the current size for the manhole chamber.
The size is set automatically, based on the size of the conduits joining into it, and the manhole construction type.

If necessary, you can override the automatically determined size by selecting the **Manual** option next to it, and entering your preferred size, and/or change between circular/rectangular types.

### Soakaway

This section defines parameters for deep bore soakaways, selected via the **Type** dropdown.

* **Borehole Diameter**: Outer diameter of the borehole below the chamber.
* **Borehole Invert**: Invert level at the bottom of the borehole.
* **Infiltrating Height**: Height of the perforated section of pipe above the borehole invert where infiltration occurs.
* **Infiltration Rate**: Rate of water infiltration through the perforated section into the surrounding soil.
* **Safety Factor**: Reduction factor applied to infiltration rate to account for potential long-term clogging.
* **Porosity**: Ratio of void space to total volume of the material between the perforated pipe and the boreholes outer diameter.
* **Pipe Diameter**: Diameter of the partially perforated pipe within the borehole.

### Sewers Type

You can choose with design standard you are working to. For example, Sewers For Adoption 6 (SFA 6) or Design Construction Guidance (DGC March 2020).
The Sewers Type determines the choice of Manhole Types.

### Manhole Type

This is a drop-down list of manhole construction types available for the selected *Sewers Type*.
For example, with DCG there are Type A, Type B, Type C, etc.

The manhole construction type will be set automatically to the most appropriate type within the Sewers Type, based in factors such as depth-to-soffit, incoming pipe diameters, benching requirements, rectangular/circular shape, and minimum size requirement.

If necessary, you can override the automatically determined type by selecting the **Manual** option next to it, and choosing your preferred type from the drop-down list.

### Cover Type

This is a drop-down list of manhole cover types available based on the BS EN 124 specification. This set of cover types is based on the covers load ratings ranging from A15, which has a 15kN max load capability, to F900, which has a 900kN max load capability. Below are some suggested use cases:

* A15: Pedestrian only areas
* B125: Driveways and private car parks
* C250: Light traffic areas
* D400: Carriageways and public car parks
* E600: HGV use, loading areas
* F900: Docks and airports

### Colour

By default, manholes will use an automatic colour based on whether they are a surface or foul manhole as well as whether it is flagged as part of the private/plot drainage. Tick the **Manual** checkbox next to the colour picker to have this manhole drawn using the colour shown in the colour picker.

Clicking on the colour picker will bring up a colour chooser window. This allows you to select a bespoke colour for this manhole which will be shown in both the plan and longsection windows.

### Existing

By default the drainage network is considered to be a new designed system.
However, you may want to include some pre-existing manholes from the site.
Accordingly, you can select the **Existing** option to flag the manhole as an existing one.

*Note*: Flagging a manhole as *Existing* will force the **Manual** option for the **Cover Level**, because the cover level for an existing manhole is normally fixed.

### Lateral

A lateral is generally a connection spurred off a main sewer run, e.g. for later connection to a building's private drainage.

If you set the manhole as a **lateral** then it is considered not to be part of the main network and will not appear on the manhole schedules.

A **lateral** will also not be included when the network is exported to other drainage software.

### Private

Setting this flag will indicate whether the manhole is Private or Adoptable. This will affect where it is shown on schedules and how it exports out to other systems like Microdrainage.

### Include in Schedules

Setting this flag will indicate whether the manhole will be appear in the schedules, such as the plan manhole schedule.

### Outfall

Every sewer network must have an outfall where the water discharges.
Accordingly, you must set one of the manholes in a network as the **Outfall**.

If **Outfall** is ticked, you will be able to click the **Surcharged** option next to the outfall tickbox.

### Surcharged

If **Surcharged** is ticked, you can type in an elevation value to indicate how full of water the outfall is. This is a constant surcharged level that will be used for the full length of any storm simulations. The elevation is an absolute level and not a depth. This option is only available when **Outfall** is ticked.

### Auto Conduit Numbering

When the **Outfall** option is ticked on, the **Auto Conduit Numbering** option will be available. When selected, this will automatically generate the run number and conduit index *(e.g 2.001)* for every pipe directly connected to this outfall.

The conduit numbers are important for exporting to other drainage systems and need to be set in the standard dendritic convention. Should you need to change the run number, untick this option then edit the run number for each conduit that requires alteration in the [Conduit Properties](pipe-properties.htm).

*Note*: If *Auto Conduit Numbering* is not selected on the outfall, then none of the pipes will have their numbers updated if you subsequently make changes to the drainage network, and then it may not export well to your drainage package.

### Flow Controls

Here you will see a list of the flow controls that have been added into this manhole.

The **Add...** buttons can be pressed to insert a new flow control into this manhole.
The **Edit...** button can be pressed to edit the properties of a flow control which has already been added to this manhole.
And the **Del...** button can be used to remove a flow control from this manhole.

See the [Manhole Flow Controls](manhole-flow-controls.htm) page for more information.

### Include In Surfaces

By default every auto cover levelled manhole is excluded from the final surface as its cover level is determined by the other design items. Whereas, manual cover levels are included by default, you can toggle the *Final Surface* option to include/exclude any manually set cover levels.

By default every manhole is included in the formation surface. However, you may want to exclude an individual manhole, untick the *Formation Surface* option to exclude it.

### Connection Invert Levels

This is a list of incoming and outgoing pipes. The columns are described below:

* **#**: Index of this pipe in this manhole.
* **Key**: This is a single character that relates the pipe in this list to the manhole preview picture at the top of this window.
* **Type**: This is the type of this pipe.
* **Dimension**: This is the dimension of this pipe.
* **Other End**: This is the name of the manhole at the other of of this pipe.
* **Invert Level**: This is the pipe invert level.
* **Depth to Soffit**: This shows the depth to soffit of this pipe.

The Invert Level and Depth to Soffit of closed pipes can be edited by clicking their respective boxes and entering a new value.

### Headwall

A manhole can be set as a **Headwall** in the **Type** dropdown. It will be shown with a headwall symbol on the drawing.

---

* [Drainage Tools](drainage.htm)
* [Manhole Renumbering](drainage-renumbering.htm)

//<!--
show\_header('Conduit Properties');
show\_index();
//-->

This option shows the properties of a drainage conduit and allows you to modify them.

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/drainage-button.png) drainage tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Edit Conduit button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-pipe-properties.png) conduit properties button.

You will be prompted to select a conduit from the drawing.
As you move the mouse cursor the nearest conduit is highlighted.
Click to select.
The conduit properties window is shown.

[Screenshot: pipe-properties-dlg.png - Conduit Properties window](images/pipe-properties-dlg.png)

### Size and Shape

The number of parallel running pipes that connect the two manholes can be set with the **Number of Links** property.

The **Cross Section Shape** drop-down selection box has the following options:

* **Closed Circular** - Standard pipe with a specified diameter
* **Closed Rectangular** - Rectangular pipe specified by width and height
* **Open Rectangular** - Rectangular pipe with an open top
* **Open Trapezoidal** - A channel with an open top that is specified by a base width, height, and side slope
* **Open Triangular** - A triangular channel with an open top specified by the top width and height
* **Swale** - An open trapezoidal shape swale with an optional underdrain

### Swale

Picking **swale** as the cross section shape will treat this pipe as a swale when exported to other drainage systems and when included in a network for the drainage simulation.

The soffit levels at each end are set to the cover level of the connecting manholes. The invert levels are then based on the height of the swale.

An underdrain can be added by ticking the **Underdrain** tickbox. After enabling this option you can specify the storage thickness and diameter of the underdrain pipe.

The swale can be included to the Final Surface and the Formation Surface by clicking the **Include In Surfaces** tick-boxes. In the case of the Formation Surface, the construction depth will be as for an Earthwork.

[Screenshot: pipe-properties-dlg-swale.png - Conduit Properties window](images/pipe-properties-dlg-swale.png)

#### Size and Shape

* **Base Width**: Width of the base of the swale.
* **Height**: Height of the swale surface, excluding underground storage.
* **Side Slope**: Slope of the surface.
* **Mannings N**: Mannings roughness coefficient for the surface.
* **Overflow Height**: Height above the base at which water flows into the downstream manhole.
* **Soil Thickness**: Thickness of the soil layer between the surface and the infiltration trench/storage.
* **Storage Thickness**: Thickness of the storage/infiltration trench.
* **Storage Porosity Ratio**: The amount of space available for water storage relative to the total (empty + solid) volume in the storage layer.
* **Underdrain**: Whether there is a fully slotted underdrain pipe.
* **Underdrain Diameter**: Diameter of the underdrain pipe.
* **Underdrain Offset**: Vertical offset of the underdrain pipe above the base of the storage layer.

#### Infiltration into Trench

* **Rate**: The infiltration rate at which water is able to move from the surface, through the soil layer, and into the storage layer.
* **Safety Factor**: This safety factor reduces the infiltration rate to account for potential clogging.

#### Trench Infiltration

* **Rate**: The infiltration rate at which water is able to move from the storage layer into the surrounding soil.
* **Safety Factor**: This safety factor reduces the infiltration rate to account for potential clogging.

### Soffit and Invert Levels

The current soffit level and invert level are shown for the manhole at each end of the conduit.
If you change the soffit level then the invert level will automatically update to reflect the conduit diameter.
Similarly, if the invert level is changed then the soffit level will automatically update to maintain the conduit diameter.

Changing either the soffit level or invert level will affect the conduit gradient.

### Colour

By default, the pipe colour is taken from the relevant layer, based on whether they are a surface or foul pipe as well as whether it is flagged as part of the private/plot drainage. Tick the **Manual** checkbox next to the colour box to instead have this pipe drawn using the colour shown in the colour box.

Clicking on the colour box will bring up a colour picker window. This allows you to select a bespoke colour for this pipe which will be shown in both the plan and longsection windows.

### Length and Volume

The pipe length shown is the 2D plan distance from manhole centre to manhole centre.

The pipe volume is calculated from the 3D pipe length between the two manhole's internal walls, taking into account whether each manhole is circular or rectangular.
This gives the effective storage space of the pipe as the volume shown does not overlap with the manhole storage volume.

The pipe volume shown gets updated when the diameter or gradient of the pipe is changed.

### Run Number

By default a pipes pipe number is automatically generated so that they are correctly maintained for export to other drainage design systems like MicroDrainage. In the event that you need to change this default behaviour, changing this number changes the run number for this particular pipe from say **2.001** to **3.001**. Note, this only changes the run number *(the number before the decimal place)* and not the pipe index *(the number after the decimal place)*.

This value can only be modified when the outfall for the selected pipe's network has the **Auto Run Numbering** option turned **off** in the [Manhole Properties](manhole-properties.htm) window.

### Diameter

The current conduit diameter is shown.
A drop-down list if standard conduit diameters is available, from which a different diameter may be selected.

If you want to specify a diameter which is not shown on the drop-down list, then select the *Non-standard diameter* option and enter the required diameter in the box.

If you change the conduit diameter then you'll be asked if you wish to the invert levels or the soffit levels to change to accommodate the change. The invert or soffit levels will then update accordingly.

### Pipe Material

Here you can specify a manual pipe material and bedding type.

### Contributing Area

Specify the contributing drainable area for surface water conduits here, or specify contributing units for foul conduits. If you are editing a surface water conduit, you can enter the impermeable percentage as well.

### Roughness Factor

Specify the roughness factor for this conduit.

### Gradient

The current gradient of the conduit is shown.
If you change the gradient then the invert and soffit levels at one of the manholes will update accordingly.
You can select which of the two manholes will maintain its level and which will update to achieve the required conduit gradient.

### Concrete Protection

If the conduit is shallow under the surface it may require concrete protection above it to prevent damage from potential ground movement. By default, shallower than 1.2m under the designed road surface requires concrete protection, but otherwise under the existing ground surface a depth of 0.9m is considered to require concrete protection.

The software will automatically detect any part of the conduit where the depth would require concrete protection, and this will be shown on long-section views and on the 3d View.

If required, you can set different depths for concrete protection for each conduit.

*Note*: The two depths are indicated on the preview image by a grey line (minimum depth under designed surface) and a green line (minimum depth under existing ground) drawn above the conduit.
This is also how the depths are indicated on a longsection with Drainage Min Cover display set On. See [Longsection View Properties](longsection-properties.htm) for more information on how to toggle Section Display Items.

---

* [Manhole Properties](manhole-properties.htm)

show\_header('Drainage Annotation Options');

To access the options for the drainage annotation, from the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/drainage-button.png) drainage tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Drainage Display Properties button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-properties.png) Drainage Display Properties button.

This window appears:

[Screenshot: drainage-properties-dlg.png - Drainage properties window](images/drainage-properties-dlg.png)

### Adoptable or Private

At the top of the window there are two radio buttons, **Adoptable** and **Private**. The currently selected option will determine whether you are editing the display properties of adoptable networks or the display properties for private networks. Toggling between adoptable and private will store the changes you have made to any properties, so when you toggle back you will see your previous changes.

Clicking OK will update both the adoptable and private properties of the project. Clicking Cancel will discard any changes.

### Manhole Label

This option enables display of the manhole name on the plan-view drawing.

If **On Outgoing Pipe** is ticked then the label will instead be drawn along the outgoing pipe.

[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-1.png - Manhole label](images/manhole-annotation-1.png)
[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-2.png - Manhole label along outgoing pipe](images/manhole-annotation-2.png)

### Manhole Diameter

This option enables display of the manhole diameter on the plan-view drawing.

[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-diameter.png - Manhole Diameter](images/manhole-annotation-diameter.png)

### Manhole Type

This option enables display of the manhole type on the plan-view drawing.

[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-type.png - Manhole Type](images/manhole-annotation-type.png)

### Manhole Cover Level

This option enables display of the manhole cover level on the plan-view drawing.

### Manhole Invert Level

This option enables display of the manhole invert level on the plan-view drawing.

If **On Outgoing Pipe** is ticked then the label will instead be drawn along the outgoing pipe.

[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-4.png - Manhole Invert](images/manhole-annotation-4.png)
[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-5.png - Manhole Invert along outgoing pipe](images/manhole-annotation-5.png)

With **On Outgoing Pipe** enabled for both the manhole label and invert level, the annotations are drawn along opposite sides of the outgoing pipe:

[Screenshot: manhole-annotation-3.png - Manhole Invert and Label along pipe](images/manhole-annotation-3.png)

### Text Height

This text box allows you to specify the size of the manhole annotation text displayed on the plan-view drawing.

### Conduit Dimension

This option enables the display of the conduit internal dimensions on the plan-view drawing. This is positioned above the pipe line, half way along.

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-diameter.png - Pipe diameter example](images/pipe-annotation-diameter.png)

### Conduit Gradient

This option enables the annotation of the conduit gradient on the plan-view drawing. This is positioned above the pipe line, half way along.

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-gradient.png - Pipe gradient example](images/pipe-annotation-gradient.png)

### Conduit Material

This option enables the annotation of the conduit material on the plan-view drawing. This is positioned above the pipe line, half way along.

The material of a conduit can be specified in the [conduit properties](pipe-properties.htm).

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-material.png - Pipe gradient example](images/pipe-annotation-material.png)

### Conduit Pipe Number

This option enables the annotation of the conduit pipe number (for example, 1.001). This is positioned below the pipe line, half way along.

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-pipe-number.png - Pipe number example](images/pipe-annotation-pipe-number.png)

### Conduit Length

This option enables the annotation of the conduit length. This is positioned below the pipe line, half way along.

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-pipe-length.png - Pipe length example](images/pipe-annotation-pipe-length.png)

### Conduit Bedding Type

This option enables the annotation of the conduit bedding type. This is positioned below the pipe line, half way along.

The bedding type of a conduit can be specified in the [conduit properties](pipe-properties.htm).

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-bedding.png - Pipe number example](images/pipe-annotation-bedding.png)

### Conduit Flow Direction

This option enables the direction of flow annotation arrow on the plan-view drawing. This is positioned half way along the pipe line.

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-flow-direction.png - Pipe flow direction](images/pipe-annotation-flow-direction.png)

### All Conduit Annotation

Here is an example showing the order of pipe annotations when all annotations are turned ON at the same time:

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-all.png - Pipe full annotation](images/pipe-annotation-all.png)

### Storm and Foul lateral Annotation

These options enable or disable the above drainage annotations for *lateral* connections (a pipe connected to a manhole which is flagged as a *lateral* - See [Manhole Properties](manhole-properties.htm)).

### Text Height

This text box allows you to specify the size of the conduit annotation text displayed on the plan-view drawing.

### Drainage Clearance

When Draw Drainage Clearance Lines is enabled, guide lines will be drawn at the specified offsets from the outer edge of the drainage.

Note: The checkbox to toggle the drawing of these lines affects both private and adoptable drainage, this is because this option sets the visibility state of the DRAIANGE\_KERB\_CLEARANCE layer.

For example, this allows you to visually verify that the drainage is further than the required minimum offset from the kerb. The default values are based on the Design and Construction Guidance of highway sewers, which states that outer edge of pipes should be at least 1m from the kerbline and manholes 0.5m from the kerbline.

[Screenshot: drainage-kerb-clearance-ex.png - Example showing kerb clearance lines drawn](images/drainage-kerb-clearance-ex.png)

### Draw Full Width Pipes

When Draw Full Width Pipes is ticked the conduits will be drawn on the plan view as two parallel lines at the width of the pipe diameter.

Below is an example of the pipes drawn at full width:

[Screenshot: pipe-annotation-full-width.png - Pipe drawn using pipe diameter](images/pipe-annotation-full-width.png)

The Manhole Properties Schedule window shows an editable list of settings for all the manholes in the selected network.

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/drainage-button.png) drainage tools toolbar, click the [Icon: Manhole Network Properties button](images/tool-buttons/drainage-manhole-network-properties.png) Manhole Network Properties button. Then select the drainage network that you want to see the manhole properties for.

After picking a drainage network you will see the following window appear:

[Screenshot: manhole-properties-schedule-dlg.png - Manhole Properties Schedule Window](images/manhole-properties-schedule-dlg.png)

### Information

Additional information may be shown here if relevant. For example, when importing a drainage network, if there are differences in the imported network compared to the current information this may be brought to your attention in this information box.

### Find on Plan

You can zoom to the location on plan of any item in the list by clicking the find icon [Screenshot: magnifying-glass.png - Magnifying Glass](images/tool-buttons/magnifying-glass.png) at the start of each row.

### Properties List

In the list area you can click on the cells to modify the properties of any manhole in the selected network.

#### Editing a property

Clicking on an cell containing editable text or an editable value will show a textbox that can be type into in order to change the property. When a textbox is shown, pressing enter will save the modified property.

For example, to change the invert level of a manhole, click on the invert level in the table, type in a new level, then press enter to apply the change.

#### Editing multiple manholes

Multiple rows of the list can be selected by first clicking on one of the manholes, this may bring up a text edit but this can be ignored. After selecting a manhole you can then hold shift and click on another manhole to select all the rows between and including the selected manholes:

Alternatively, you can select an initial manhole and then hold control (Ctrl) to add another manhole to the selection one at a time. This way the selection does not have to be sequential.

With multiple rows selected you can then click on one of the properties of a selected row to change it for all the selected manholes.

For example, this will allow you to change the Sewers Specification used to determine the automatic type, for multiple manholes all at once.

### Save to CSV

You can save out the current state of the properties list using the **Save to CSV** button. All columns and rows will be exported out as Comma Separated Values (CSV) that can be viewed in a spreadsheet program of your choice (like Excel).

A Foul Calculation PDF Report can be created in Site3D using the Foul Calculation Report tool.

The Foul Calculation report is generated using the Foul Network and all assigned Units the conduits. Before the report is created, you will need to set one of the Foul manholes as the Outfall and you will also need to assign Contributing Units to the relevant conduits.

### Create Foul Calculation Report

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) drainage auto-sizing-levelling and simulation toolbar,
click the [Icon: Foul Calculation Report button](images/tool-buttons/foul-calcs.png) Foul Calculation Report button.

The results window is shown immediately after clicking on the Foul Calculation Report button:

[Screenshot: foul-calcs-report-example.png - Foul Calculation PDF Report](images/foul-calcs-report-example.png)

At the top of each page of the Foul Calculation results you have the report header with the company and project information. You can set up the information which is shown here in the [Simulation Results Properties window](drainage-simulation-storm-results-report.htm#simulation_results_properties).

On the first page of the results you have the network details which lists the manholes and pipes included in the drainage networks which are included in the Foul Calculations.

Below the Manhole and Pipe schedules you have the Foul Calculation Results which will show the per conduit Accumulated Units, Flow capacity, Velocity at Capacity, Actual Flow (Q), Actual Velocity, Actual Depth and Velocitgy at 1/3rd Q.

### Flow Controls

Manholes may include unrestricted pumps indicated by "(pump)" in the schedules. For pumped conduits, only the Actual Flow (Q) is displayed. This represents the average flow rate needed to transfer accumulated upstream units to the downstream manhole. Other types of flow controls are not currently supported for foul simulation.

### Pipe Number

This is the pipe designation assigned when you have an outfall specified for a connected network.

### Accumulated Units

This shows the combined number of units assigned to this pipe in addition to all the units assigned to all upstream pipes.

### Flow Capacity

This shows the potential maximum flow through a non-surcharged pipe.

### Velocity at Capacity

This shows pipe velocity at Flow Capacity.

### Actual Flow (Q)

This shows the Flow calculated using the Accumulated Units and Design Flow (not peak flow) based on the foul flow volume per unit per day value (4000ltrs by default).

### Actual Velocity

This shows the Calculated velocity in a pipe for the Actual Flow.

### Actual Depth

This shows the Calculated Depth in a pipe for the Actual Flow.

### Velocity at 1/3rd Q

This shows the calculated Velocity at 1/3rd of the pipe's Flow Capacity.

show\_header('Inputting M5-60 and Ratio R values');

//-->

When using the Flood Studies Report (FSR) method to calculate rainfall intensity profiles, you need to two values. These are the rainfall depth of an **M5-60** storm, in mm, and ratio of the M5-60 to the M5-two day storm event (**Ratio R**).

On the [Icon: Drainage Auto Sizing and Levelling Tools button](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) drainage size and level tools click the [Icon: UK Map button](images/tool-buttons/fsr-map.png) button.

## M5-60 and Ratio R UK Map

By default the map will show the site position based on the drawings site extents. You can overide this by clicking on the map. The corresponding M5-60 and Ratio R values are shown below the map.

[Screenshot: m5\_60\_ratio\_r\_dlg.png - M5-60 Ratio R window](images/m5\_60\_ratio\_r\_dlg.png)

## Navigating the Map

You can zoom in and pan the map with the mouse:

* Scroll Wheel: Zoom In and Out
* Middle Mouse Button: Click and hold to drag and move around the map.
* Left Mouse Button: Click once to set/change where you want to retrieve the **M5-60** and **Ratio R** values from.

Clicking the left mouse button to select the desired site location will show a green cross where you clicked for confirmation. The **M5-60** and **Ratio R** values will automatically show in the edit boxes below the map.

  

## Zooming into the Map

To see more map detail, simply zoom in to the map using the scroll wheel and more place names will show on the screen, as indicated in the images below:

[Screenshot: m5\_60\_ratio\_r\_map\_zoom.png - M5-60 Ratio R map zoom](images/m5\_60\_ratio\_r\_map\_zoom.png)

Placenames are colour coded:

* Red: Cities
* Green: Towns
* Purple: Suburban areas
* Light blue: Villages
* Grey: Hamlets

  

## Typing in known values

Alternatively, if you know the values already, you can simply type the **M5-60** and **Ratio R** values directly into the edit boxes below the map.

#### M5-60

The M5-60 value is the expected depth of rainfall, in millimetres (mm), from a storm lasting **60 minutes** with a return period of **5 years**.

#### Ratio R

The dimensionless ratio "r" value is the M5-60 minutes value divided by the M5-2 days value.

Click the **OK** button to confirm the values shown, or press the **Cancel** button to close the window without making any changes.

## Resetting Site Location

If you manually click a new location on plan, the **Reset Position to Site Extent** button will appear at the bottom. If clicked, this will reset the position on the map to the current site position.

//<!--
show\_header('Inputting FEH rainfall data');
show\_index();
//-->

The Flood Estimation Handbook (FEH) rainfall model is a method of modelling rainfall data for drainage simulation and flood analysis.

  

## FEH99

The FEH 1999 model estimates rainfall using a rainfall depth-duration-frequency model.

The DDF model is defined by six parameters (c, d1, d2, d3, e, f) which you can input by clicking on the "FEH99"
button in the [Auto Size and Level Network window](drainage-auto-sizing-levelling.htm) or the
[Storm Simulation window](drainage-simulation.htm).

[Screenshot: feh-1999-dlg.png - FEH 1999 data input window](images/feh-1999-dlg.png)
  
  
  

## FEH13 or FEH22 Data

The FEH 2013 dataset also uses a depth-duration-frequency model similar to the FEH 1999 model but with improvements based on modern data analysis.
FEH 2022 is a further improvement on that with increased temporal resolution and coverage. These datasets are typically given as an XML file.

The FEH22 file format remains the same as the FEH13, in fact both sets of data may be in the same file.
Site3D can load files which contains both FEH13 and FEH22 datasets as well as files which only have either FEH13 or FEH22 data.
In the situation where a file has both FEH13 and FEH22 data, Site3D will prefer the FEH22 data where possible.

The rainfall data is represented as a matrix of depths for standard return periods and durations.
You can load a FEH13 or FEH22 dataset from a file by clicking on the "FEH13 or FEH22"
button in the [Auto Size and Level Network window](drainage-auto-sizing-levelling.htm) or the
[Storm Simulation window](drainage-simulation.htm).

#### Specifying the FEH13 or FEH22 data set

Press the **Browse** button to select the file on your computer, or you can manually type or paste the file path directly into the input box.

[Screenshot: feh-2013-dlg.png - FEH 2013 data input window](images/feh-2013-dlg.png)
