A storm simulation can be run from Site3D using the [storm simulation tool](drainage-simulation.htm)[Icon: Drainage storm simulation button](images/tool-buttons/storm-simulation.png). After you have ran a storm simulation you can then see the simulation report.

### Simulation Results Report

The simulation results window is shown after running a storm simulation on a drainage network.
The results are then stored in the network so you can safely close the results display and return to it later as long as the network remains unmodified.

*Note*: Changes made to the drainage network will invalidate any previously calculated simulation results for the modified network because the simulated network will no longer match the drainage network in the model.
In this situation the drainage simulations should be re-run for the modified network.

To see a previously calculated simulation results report you can press the [Icon: Drainage storm simulation results button](images/tool-buttons/storm-simulation-results.png) Simulation Results Report button on the [Icon: Drainage Auto Sizing and Levelling button](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) Drainage Analysis toolbar and select the network on the Plan View.

### Document Header Details

At the top of each page of the simulation results you have the report header. On the top right it shows the company name, the name of the person who ran the simulation and the date when the results were generated.

In the centre underneath the "Drainage Simulation Results" title it shows the name of the project.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-header.png - Storm Simulation Results](images/storm-simulation-results-header.png)

You can customise the header information and how the simulation results report is displayed using the [Simulation Results Report Properties](#Report_Properties).

### Drainage Network Details

On the first page of the results you have the network details which lists the following items that were included in the simulated network:

* Manholes
* Conduits
* Permeable Paving

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p1.png - Storm Simulation Results Network Details](images/storm-simulation-results-p1.png)

#### Manhole Schedule Columns

* Manhole: Name of the manhole.
* Catchment Area: 2D area of ground where rainfall will flow directly into this manhole.
* Size: Circular diameter or rectangular width and length describing the plan shape of this manhole.
* Type: SFA/DCG type of this manhole.
* CL: Cover Level at the top of the manhole.
* IL: Invert Level at the bottom of the manhole.
* Depth to Soffit: Level difference between the cover level and the soffit of the outgoing conduit of this manhole.
* Easting: X-axis part of the manhole's coordinate.
* Northing: Y-axis part of the manhole's coordinate.

#### Pipe Schedule Columns

* Pipe Number: Name of the conduit.
* US Manhole: Name of the upstream manhole.
* US IL: Invert Level of this conduit at the upstream manhole end.
* DS Manhole: Name of the downstream manhole.
* DS IL: Invert Level of this conduit at the downstream manhole end.
* Shape: Cross sectional shape of this conduit, eg circular, rectangular, trapezoidal, etc.
* Diameter: Cross sectional diameter of this conduit if circular or otherwise width and height.
* Length: 2D length of this conduit measured from the centre of manholes at either end.
* Gradient: Longsectional gradient of this conduit.
* Roughness: Colebrook-White roughness factor of this conduit.
* US Depth to Soffit: Level difference between the upstream manhole's cover level and this conduit's soffit level at the upstream manhole.
* DS Depth to Soffit: Level difference between the downstream manhole's cover level and this conduit's soffit level at the downstream manhole.

#### Permeable Paving Schedule Columns

* Permeable Paving: Name of the permeable paving area.
* Assigned Manhole: Name of the manhole that the outfall connects to.
* Effective Storage Volume: Water storing volume in the Storage layer, taking into account the porosity.
* CL: Cover level of the assigned manhole.
* IL: Level at the base of the Storage layer.
* Storage Infil Rate: Infiltration rate into the ground under the storage layer.
* Safety Factor: Factor applied to the infiltration rate to account for siltation.
* Easting: X-axis part of the annotation position within the area.
* Northing: Y-axis part of the annotation position within the area.

*Note*: If the manhole is assigned to a pond/tank it will instead use the pond/tank invert level, cover level and freeboard settings. Furthermore, if it is assigned to a tank with a freeboard of 0m then it will not report flood risk when the water head is above the top level of the tank, but will instead report surcharged.

### Outfall Details

Below the schedules is outfall details showing which node is the designated outfall and whether it is a **Free Discharge**, **Surcharged**, **Infiltration Pond** or **Infiltration Tank**.

### Flow Controls

After the outfall details are a list of the flow controls included in the network showing the relevant details of each control used.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p2.png - Storm Simulation Results - Flow Controls](images/storm-simulation-results-p2.png)

#### Weirs

* Invert Offset: Height of weir wall above manhole invert level.
* Weir Width: Width of weir over which water can flow.
* Discharge Coeff: Discharge coefficient (unitless).

#### Flap Valves and Orifices

* Invert Offset: Height of orifice invert level above manhole invert level.
* Orifice Diameter: Cross sectional diameter of orifice opening.
* Discharge Coeff: Discharge coefficient (unitless).

#### Vortex Flow Controls

For example, ACO Q-Brake, Stormbrake, Hydro-BrakeÂ® Optimum

* Model Ref: Manufacturers reference ID for the specific device.
* Design Depth: Maximum expected depth of water (head) above the invert of the flow control.
* Design Flow: Desired maximum discharge rate used in the design and specification of this device.
* Invert Offset: Height of vortex flow control's invert level above manhole invert level.
* FF Head: (Flush Flow Head) Head of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control transforms from standard flow to vortex flow.
* FF Flow: (Flush Flow) Flow rate of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control transforms from standard flow to vortex flow.
* KF Head: (Kickback Head) Head of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control produces a steady vortex.
* KF Flow: (Kickback Flow) Flow rate of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control produces a steady vortex.

#### Pumps and Depth/Flows

* Invert Offset: Height of flow control's invert level above manhole invert level.
* Depth and Flow: List of sequential pairs of values that describe the flow rate at a specific depth for this device.

### Pond and Tank information

Any ponds and tanks that are in the network are listed out next with a table for each showing the properties like Invert Levels, Max Depth etc. Underneath this table is a pictorial depth area diagram for the simulated pond structure that also includes the max water level achieved in the simulation annotated with the critical storm for each return period.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p3.png - Storm Simulation Results - Pond](images/storm-simulation-results-p3.png)

#### Pond and Tank Columns

* Pond/Tank Invert: Lowest Z elevation of the pond or tank structure that stores water.
* Max Depth: Difference in level between highest possible water level and invert level.
* Volume to Water Level: Volume of water in the pond/tank at the freeboard water level.
* Water Level: Maximum water level at freeboard.
* Freeboard: Vertical distance above the water level to the maximum possible water level.
* Infil Base: Infiltration Rate on the base of the structure.
* Infil Side: Infiltration Rate on the side of the structure.
* Safety Factor: Safety Factor that is applied to the base and side infiltration rates.

### Draindown Graph

For each of the ponds and tanks you will also get a graph showing the water level over the course of the simulation. The peak is indicated with an annotation for the level and time. From the peak you have the drain down to the end of the simulation which can be used to calculate the half drain down time:

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p4.png - Storm Simulation Results - Drain Down](images/storm-simulation-results-p4.png)

### Simulation Settings

After the pond and tank information is the simulation settings.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p4a.png - Storm Simulation Results - Simulation Settings](images/storm-simulation-results-p4a.png)

* Rainfall type (FSR, FEH) used in the simulation with any relevant settings.
* Rainfall locale (England and Wales, Scotland) used in the simulation.
* Rainfall profiles used (Summer, Winter) and their Cv values used in the simulation.
* Global Time of Entry for drainage features that are set to use the Global value.
* List of Storm Durations simulated.
* List of Storm Return Periods simulated along with their Climate Change values displayed in pairs (Return Period, Climate Change %).

#### Additional Storage / MADD Factor

As a result of discussions with LLFAs (Lead Local Flood Authorities), Site3D does not include a setting for additional storage per hectare or MADD Factor. Accordingly, the drainage simulation is done with 0.0m³/ha additional storage and a MADD Factor of zero.

### Simulated Rainfall Events

The Simulated Rainfall Events table lists out all storms simulated showing which Return Period, Duration and Season information with the calculated Rainfall Average Intensity and also any potential continuity errors.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p5.png - Storm Simulation Results - Rainfall Events](images/storm-simulation-results-p5.png)

### Continuity Errors

Continuity errors in a drainage system simulation are used to assess the accuracy of the model's results and are an indication of flow stability through the network. Continuity Errors are reported as two separate percentage values, each representing the difference between input and output for different system components and should ideally be as close to zero as possible.

* Flow Continuity Error: Reflects the accuracy of simulation through the systemâs manholes, conduits, tanks and ponds.
* Runoff Continuity Error: Reflects the accuracy of the simulation across any modeled drainable areas.

A positive continuity error indicates a higher inflow than outflow i.e. unexpected water loss, whereas a negative value indicates a higher outflow than inflow i.e. unexpected water gain. The equation used for continuity errors is:

Continuity Error = 100 \* (1 - Total Outflow / Total Inflow)

#### Continuity Error Severity Levels

The continuity errors are classified and coloured based on their severity as follows:

* 0-5%: Acceptable level of accuracy (black).
* 5-10%: Less accurate but still within acceptable tolerance (orange).
* >10%: Unreliable for realistic simulations (red).

#### Common Causes of High Continuity Errors

* Short pipe sections.
* Extreme flow control values in manholes.

By reviewing these continuity errors, the overall reliability of the simulation can be determined and potential issues in the system design can be identified. You can further inspect the errors by turning on per item continuity errors in the [Simulation Results Properties window](drainage-simulation-storm-results-report.htm#simulation_results_properties), this adds an additional row to the **Simulated Rainfall Events** table showing item continuity errors for the simulated storm.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-item-continuity.png - Highest Item Continuity Errors](images/storm-simulation-results-item-continuity.png)

### Simulation Results

The final section shows the simulation results for each return period. Each Return period will show a table of the simulation results for Manholes, Conduits and permeable paving.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-results-p6.png - Storm Simulation Results](images/storm-simulation-results-p6.png)

#### Manhole Table Columns

* Manhole: Name of the manhole.
* Critical Storm: The storm which resulted in this manhole having either the most amount of flooding or the highest depth of water for the return period.
* Peak: Time in minutes from the beginning of the storm when the peak flooding or peak water depth occurred for this manhole.
* Level: Z Elevation of water level at the peak minute of the critical storm.
* Depth: Water depth in this manhole at the peak minute of the critical storm.
* Inflow: Rate of water flowing into this manhole at the peak minute of the critical storm.
* Flood: Total volume of water lost to flooding from this manhole in the critical storm. Blank if no flooding.
* Status: Summary the state of the manhole, pond, tank or permeable paving (OK, Surcharged, Flood Risk, Flood, Outfall)

##### Status Description for Manholes

* Outfall: The manhole is the assigned outfall of the system.
* OK: The maximum water level is lower than the outgoing pipe soffit.
* Surcharged (orange): The water level is higher than the outgoing pipe soffit, but lower than the freeboard level.
* Flood Risk (orange): The water level is above the freeboard level of the manhole but below the cover level.
* Flood (red): The water level exceeds the cover level of the manhole resulting in flooding.

#### Conduit Table Columns

* Pipe No.: Name of the conduit
* Critical Storm: Name of storm which resulted in this conduit having the highest flow depth for the return period or the highest flow rate if surcharged.
* Peak: Time in minutes from the beginning of the storm when the peak flow depth was achieved for this conduit.
* US Manhole: Name of upstream manhole.
* DS Manhole: Name of downstream manhole.
* Flow Depth: Depth of flow in this conduit at the peak minute of the critical storm.
* Max Velocity: Highest velocity of water flowing through this conduit for any storm in the return period.
* Max Flow: Highest flow rate of water flowing through this conduit for any storm in the return period.
* Flow/Capacity: Max Flow as a ratio of the maximum capacity when flowing only under gravity. This value can be more than 1 if flowing under pressure in surcharged situations.
* Status: Summary the state of the conduit (OK, Surcharged).

##### Conduit Status Description

* OK: The water level is lower than the conduit soffit.
* Surcharged (orange): The conduit is fully submerged

##### Status Description for Permeable Paving

Below are the status options for permeable paving. Permeable paving is built up of a storage layer, a bedding layer, and a pavement layer.

* OK: The permeable paving water level is within the storage layer.
* Surcharged (orange): The water level is above the storage layer but within the bedding layer.
* Flood Risk (orange): The water level is above the bedding layer but within the pavement layer.
* Flooded (red): The water level is above the cover level of the paving.
* Runoff (red): Water has runoff the surface without entering the paving.

*Note*: Runoff is only reported if water overflows the kerb height without infiltrating into the pavement layer.

### Simulation Results Report Properties

You can access the storm simulation report properties by pressing the [Icon: Drainage storm simulation result properties button](images/tool-buttons/storm-simulation-results-properties.png) button on the [Icon: Drainage Auto Sizing and Levelling](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) Drainage Analysis toolbar. You will see the following window appear:

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-report-properties-dlg.png - Storm Simulation Report Properties Window](images/storm-simulation-report-properties-dlg.png)

#### Report Header Information

Here you can customize the information shown in the header at the top of every page.

#### Network Details

When the Network Details option is ticked you will see the manhole and pipe schedule at the top of the report.

#### Item Continuity Errors

When the Item Continuity Errors option is ticked the highest item continuity errors will be displayed next to each storms total error. See [Continuity Errors](drainage-simulation-storm-results-report.htm#continuity_errors) for more information.

#### Flood Risk Freeboard

If the water level in a manhole goes above this depth below cover level in the drainage simulation, this manhole will be annotated as "Flood Risk" in the simulation results.

The Freeboard of other design items like ponds and tanks is configurable in the Pond and Tank properties windows. The Freeboard for Ponds and Tanks will also be shown per item in this report in the Pond and Tank details.

A storm simulation can be run from Site3D using the [storm simulation tool](drainage-simulation.htm)[Icon: Drainage storm simulation button](images/tool-buttons/storm-simulation.png). After you have run a storm simulation on a network you can press the [Icon: Drainage storm simulation playback button](images/tool-buttons/storm-simulation-playback.png) Simulation Playback button and then select the network to play back a simulation from the selected network.

### Simulation Playback

If the selected drainage network has multiple storm simulations you will be shown a window where you can select the active storm. If there is only one storm simulation on the network this will automatically be chosen.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-select-storm-dlg.png - Storm Select window](images/storm-simulation-select-storm-dlg.png)

After selecting the drainage network and storm, the simulation playback controls will be shown and the drainage in the model will be shown in the simulation colouring.
Pipes and manholes will be drawn in green, gradually become more orange as they fill and are drawn in red if they are completely full or overflowing.
Below is an example part way through the simulation with the other design item layers turned off so you can clearly see the simulation colours:

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-playback-controls.png - Simulation playback controls](images/storm-simulation-playback-controls.png)

#### Active Storm

At the top of the playback controls is a label showing which storm is currently select for simulation playback.

#### Time Bar

Below the active storm label is the time bar, this has a slider bar which you can grab with the mouse and drag to view the simulation at a specific time. The current simulation time and maximum simulation time are shown to the right.

#### Playback Controls

Below the time bar are the playback controls:

* [Icon: Step simulation back button](images/tool-buttons/sim-playback-step-back.png) Go back one simulation step
* [Icon: Play storm simulation button](images/tool-buttons/sim-playback-play.png) Play Simulation
* [Icon: Step simulation forward button](images/tool-buttons/sim-playback-step-forward.png) Go forward one simulation step

After pressing the Play Simulation button the button will change to the Pause Simulation button ([Icon: Pause storm simulation button](images/tool-buttons/sim-playback-pause.png)).
The Step Back/Step Forward buttons will also be deactivated during simulation playback.

#### During Simulation Playback

Below is an example with the standard design item layers turned on, you will notice that the drainage networks which are not part of the simulated network are drawn in grey:

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-playback-ex1.png - Storm simulation playback example](images/storm-simulation-playback-ex1.png)

During the simulation playback, a manhole which is overflowing/flooding will be drawn with a red background as shown below:

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-flooded-manhole.png - Example of a manhole which is flooding during simulation playback](images/storm-simulation-flooded-manhole.png)

If you have a longsection open you will also see the simulation playback in the section view. You will see the water levels for the current simulation time step:

*Note*: You can use the [Icon: Longsection button](images/simple-section-button.png) Longsection button to generate a longsection window during playback. Just select the two manholes either end of the desired drainage longsection.

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-playback-ex2.png - Storm simulation playback in longsection example](images/storm-simulation-playback-ex2.png)

The red line shows the maximum water level for that drainage item, this is the highest level that is reached during the simulation:

[Screenshot: storm-simulation-playback-ex3.png - Storm simulation playback in longsection example](images/storm-simulation-playback-ex3.png)

//<!--
show\_header('Back To Back Drainage Storm Simulation');
show\_index();
//-->

A back to back storm simulation can be run from Site3D using the back to back storm simulation tool. After you have ran a storm simulation you can then see the simulation report and also play the simulation back within Site3D.

### Run a storm simulation

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) drainage auto-sizing-levelling and simulation toolbar,
click the [Icon: Back to back drainage storm simulation button](images/tool-buttons/back-to-back-drainage-simulation.png) Run Back to Back Storm Simulation button.

After clicking on the Run Simulation button you will see the following window appear:

[Screenshot: back-to-back-storm-simulation-dlg.png - Run Back to Back Storm Simulation Window](images/back-to-back-storm-simulation-dlg.png)

#### Storm Type

The area to the right shows the current rainfall parameters being used. You can click on one of the buttons to the left to pick new settings using data from either FSR, FEH99, or FEH13.

#### Critical Storage

You must select a storage item or outfall manhole to act as the critical storage item for the simulation. Site3D will then try to intelligently pick the most critical storm, of any storms previously ran using the standard drainage simulation tool [Icon: Drainage storm simulation button](images/tool-buttons/storm-simulation.png).

*Note*: Storage items that are part of networks with no outfalls will not show in the dropdown.

The network information for the selected storage item will appear below it along with a preview of the network, that will be simulated. A network will show a green status icon if it is valid.
If the network has an orange status it indicates an issue with the networks design which should be resolved, the messages column will explain the issue.

#### Time Between Storms

You can specify the dry period between the end of the critical storm and the start of the follow-on storm using the *Time between storms* option. The default is 1440 mins (24 hours).

#### Critical Storm and Season

The critical storm is pre-selected based on worst level/flooding in the selected storage item during previously ran simulations, you can choose to manually override the season, duration and return period of this storm.

#### Follow-on Storm

The follow on storm is simulated after the critical storm in one back to back simulation.You must manually choose the duration and return period of this storm.

#### Other Settings

All other settings, such as Global Time of Entry, CV values etc. are the same as in [Drainage Simulation Window](drainage-simulation.htm). These all default to match the drainage simulation tools settings.

[Click here for more in-depth information on the Storm Simulation Settings](drainage-simulation.htm).

#### Run Simulation

Press the Run Simulation button to start the requested simulation, after completion a report will appear showing the results for the combined storm.

[Click here for more in-depth information on the Storm Simulation Results Report](drainage-simulation-storm-results-report.htm).

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Manhole Flow Controls');
show\_index();
//-->

Flow controls are applied to the primary downstream conduit of a manhole. All other conduits are not affected by the control.

Multiple flow controls can be added to a manhole, and are added in parallel to one another.

Flow controls can be managed for a specific manhole by using the [Manhole Properties](manhole-properties.htm) tool and selecting a manhole.
The list of flow controls will be shown in the Manhole Properties window.

Alternatively, you can also manage manhole flow controls from the [Drainage menu](drainage-menu.htm) by clicking on the **Add/Edit Flow Control** option.
After selecting the menu option you then select the manhole to manage the flow controls for that manhole.
The Flow Control Manager window will then appear:

[Screenshot: flow-controls-dlg.png - Flow control window](images/flow-controls-dlg.png)

In the Manhole Properties window or Flow Control Manager window you will see a list of the flow controls that have been added into the selected manhole.

Click the **Add...** button and a list of flow controls will appear.
Select the desired type of flow control to add, and the relevant flow control properties window will appear.

Select a flow control from the list and click the **Edit...** button to show the relevant flow control properties window.

Select a flow control from the list and click the **Del...** button to remove the flow control from the manhole.

When you are done making changes to the flow controls for this manhole click OK to close the window and apply the changes.
After the window is closed you will then be able to select another manhole to edit.
To stop selecting manholes you can right click and select **Quit Command** or switch to another tool.

### Weir

A weir control can be specified by width and discharge coefficient:

[Screenshot: flow-controls-weir-properties-dlg.png - Weir control properties](images/flow-controls-weir-properties-dlg.png)

### Orifice

An orifice control can be specified by orifice diameter and discharge coefficient:

[Screenshot: flow-controls-orifice-properties-dlg.png - Orifice control properties](images/flow-controls-orifice-properties-dlg.png)

### Flap Valve

An flap valve (non-return valve) can be specified by diameter and discharge coefficient:

[Screenshot: flow-controls-flap-valve-properties-dlg.png - Flap valve control properties](images/flow-controls-flap-valve-properties-dlg.png)

### Vortex

A generic or manufacturer-specific vortex flow control can be added.

See the following help page for more information about adding or editing a vortex flow control: [Vortex Flow Control Properties](vortex-properties.htm)

### Depth/Flow

A vortex control can also be added by specifying the Depth/Flow Hydrograph.
The manufacturer of a vortex control will usually provide a table of depth to flow data as a csv or spreadsheet file.
This depth/flow data can be copied and then you can press the Paste button in the Depth Flow Properties window to add this in one go.

[Screenshot: flow-controls-depth-flow-properties-dlg.png - Vortex control properties](images/flow-controls-depth-flow-properties-dlg.png)

The Invert Offset is the vertical offset of the flow controls invert level above the manholes invert level. The depth flows are applied above the flow controls invert level.

### Pump

Pumps are used to move water in one direction from a lower manhole to a higher manhole and can be used to simulate rising mains. You can choose between two pump types: Restricted and Unrestricted.

A Restricted Pump requires a Depth/Flow Hydrograph. This graph defines how the pump operates:

* Depth refers to the water level above the manholes invert.
* Flow is the maximum rate at which the pump discharges water downstream.

[Screenshot: flow-controls-pump-restricted.png - Restricted Pump control properties](images/flow-controls-pump-restricted.png)

An Unrestricted Pump does not use a hydrograph. Instead, it automatically pumps water at the same rate as it enters the manhole - the outflow always equals the inflow. This type is mainly used for preliminary surface water design or in foul simulations.

[Screenshot: flow-controls-pump-unrestricted.png - Unrestricted Pump control properties](images/flow-controls-pump-unrestricted.png)

Both pump types require a Start Up Depth and a Shut Off Depth.

* Start Up Depth: The pump turns on when water rises above this depth (measured from the manhole invert).
* Shut Off Depth: The pump turns off when water drops below this depth.

Between these depths, the pump remains in its current state (on or off).

For Restricted Pumps, manufacturers typically provide a table of Depth vs Flow values (e.g., in a CSV or spreadsheet). You can copy this data and use the **Paste** button in the properties window to import it quickly.

//<!--
show\_header('Save As IFC');
show\_index();
//-->

IFC is a popular file format for **BIM** models (**B**uilding **I**nformation **M**odelling).

Site3D produces a high quality 3D IFC model that will display in your BIM viewer (for example, Autodesk's RevitTM). The IFC model looks the same as it does in the Site3D 3D View, and includes attribute information for all the design elements.

The design elements are exported as 3D solid objects in the IFC file, which makes it very useful for checking for clashes with items from other disciplines in your BIM model.

*Note*: The IFC export version is IFC2x3.

To export an IFC BIM model use the **File->Save As->IFC (\*.ifc)** menu option.

You are shown the following window, to choose the design and model elements to include:

[Screenshot: ifc-export-dlg.png - IFC Export Preview Window](images/ifc-export-dlg.png)

### IFC Export List

The window shows a list of all the roads and paths, ponds and drainage networks, and project models, which may be included in the export.

Initially all models and design items will be selected for export. You can select or deselect any of the models or design items to choose exactly what will be exported.

### Base Point - Revit

Specifying the "Base Point" coordinate in the export window sets the exported design's origin/insertion point.
The design will then be exported with coordinates that are relative to the base point coordinate.
This is useful as some IFC viewers, in particular **Revit**, prefer to work with smaller local coordinates.
In this case, you should specify the base point coordinate at the local coordinate system origin point for the IFC model.

As an example, if you intend to combine the export with a **Revit** project, the coordinate you input here should match the coordinate *at* the "Internal Origin Point" in the Revit project.
This is usually the "**Project Base Point**" coordinate (by default the Project Base Point and Internal Origin Point in Revit are coincident and move with each other).

*Note*: Revit does not cope well with standard grid size coordinates that are in the hundreds of thousands (e.g. X=500,000E,Y=300,000N). If you output to Revit with no base point, it will either not display the model, or display it looking visually inaccurate. For Revit, the base point will need to be inside, or very close to, the site.

### Export Styles

By default objects such as pipes are exported to IFC as a cross sectional shape with an extrusion length and direction.
This is the preferred IFC shape representation as it provides a parametric definition for BIM systems.
Some external systems may not be able to handle the extruded shape definitions, and when imported the objects may appear missing because the system can't display them.
If this is the case, untick the **Objects as Extruded Shapes** tickbox and all objects will be exported as a triangulated mesh instead.

//<!--
show\_header('OBJ Export');

//-->

Wavefront OBJ is a popular file format for 3D objects. In civil engineering, it is commonly used for rendering concept and photo-realistic representations and animations of designs.

Site3D produces a high quality 3D OBJ model that can be imported into rendering programs such as Lumion, Blender, Unity and Unreal where you can change lighting conditions, add sky and even apply textures to the different surfaces.

To export the coloured final surface as an OBJ file, use the **File->Save As->Design to OBJ(\*.obj)** menu option.

You are asked to choose the origin point for the exported model:

[Screenshot: obj-export-origin.png - OBJ Export Choose Origin](images/obj-export-origin.png)

Left click on a position within your design that will be the centre of the model when imported into a rendering program.
It is usually recommended to choose an origin that is in the centre of your design so that your model is centred in the view when imported into a rendering program.

*Note: After choosing a filename, Site3D will export both the OBJ file and a MTL materials file of the same filename as the OBJ file, but with '.mtl' extension instead.
The materials file contains default surface colours.*

  

Example rendered export from BlenderTM

[Screenshot: 3d-render-2.jpg - Example 3D Render](images/3d-render-2.jpg)

//<!--
show\_header('Save As Pipe Schedule');
show\_index();
//-->

A schedule of the pipes in the drainage network can be output to a CSV file.

The **Save As** option for **Pipe Schedule** is on the **File** menu.

Here is an example extract of an exported pipe schedule, as viewed in a typical spreadsheet program:

[Screenshot: csv-pipe-schedule-example.png - CSV Pipe Schedule Example Export](images/csv-pipe-schedule-example.png)

### Pipe Schedule (csv)

The CSV export includes the following columns:

* PN: Pipe number
* U/S MH: Upstream Manhole Name
* D/S MH: Downstream Manhole Name
* SHAPE: Pipe Shape
* LEN: 2D pipe length, measured from the inner face of the upstream manhole to the inner face of the downstream manhole, including internal offsets\*
* LEN3D: 3D pipe length, measured the same way as LEN but accounting for elevation\*
* DIMS: Pipe Dimensions
* U/S DEPTH: Depth to invert at the upstream end
* D/S DEPTH: Depth to invert at the downstream end

*NOTE*: A pipe network must have an outfall set in order to be exported. See [Manhole Properties](manhole-properties.htm).

\* Pipe lengths (2D and 3D) are calculated from the internal offset within one manhole to the internal offset within another. The internal offset is the distance the pipe is recessed from the inner face of the manhole. This offset is defined by the setting "DrainagePipeManholeInternalOffset" and can be updated in the [Configuration Options](settings-configuration.htm).  
Open-topped channels (e.g., swales) measure length directly from inner face to inner face, without applying the internal offset.

The **Save As** option for **Run Longsections in DXF** is on the **File** menu.

This will save multiple selected drainage longsections to a single dxf file.

You should see the following window:

[Screenshot: drainage-run-longsection-export.png - Drainage Run Export Window](images/drainage-run-longsection-export.png)

### Drainage Networks to Export

The Drainage Networks section of the window lists all of the drainage networks on the site.
Here you select which networks you want to be included in the export.

The list has columns which contain the network name, network status, number of manholes in the network, and a message displaying information for the network status.

The network status column will show a green block if the network is valid and the *Messages* column will state the outfall manhole.
If the network is not valid the status block will be red and the *Messages* column will contain the reason the network is not valid.

The example image above has Surface Network 1 and Foul Network 1 selected for export, shown by them being highlighted in blue.
You can select a single network by clicking on it with the mouse. If you then hold down *control* and click other networks they will be added to the selection. If you select a single network and then hold down *shift* and click another network, every network between them will also be selected. If you hold down *control* and press the keyboard *A* key, all the networks in the list will be selected.

### Direction

Here you can select whether you want the [drainage longsections](drainage-longsection.htm) to be drawn from the highest point to the lowest point or the lowest to the highest.

There are diagrams next to the options showing how an example longsection will look if you choose that setting.

# Commands

Frequently used commands can be performed using the following keyboard shortcuts:

| Shortcut | Command |
| --- | --- |
| CTRL+C or CTRL+Insert | Copy plan to clipboard |
| CTRL+D | Delete Centreline |
| CTRL+F | Find text in the plan view |
||  |  |
| --- | --- |
| SHIFT+F3 | Find text - Go next |
| CTRL+G | Gouraud shading |
| CTRL+I | Centreline interfacing |
| CTRL+L | Centreline level at chainage |
| SHIFT+L | Centreline level to surface (section view) |
| CTRL+O | Open file |
| CTRL+P | Print plan view |
| R | Recreate final and formation surface |
| CTRL+S | Save file |
| CTRL+V or SHIFT+Insert | Paste plan from clipboard |
| X | Centreline cross section |
| CTRL+Z or ALT+Backspace | Undo |
| F1 | Open help |
| F3 | Snap to point |
| F4 | Snap to intersection |
| F5 | Snap to line |
| F8 | Snap to orthogonal |

# 3D View Movement

Keyboard shortcuts for moving the camera in the 3D view. These shortcuts are especially useful if you are using a laptop without a mouse. For further information on 3D view navigation refer to [3D View Navigation](model-view-toolbar.htm#navigation).

| Shortcut | Movement |
| --- | --- |
| SHIFT+Cursor movement | Rotate the camera |
| Arrow keys | Pan the camera left, right, forward, backward |
| SHIFT+Up and SHIFT+Down arrow keys | Zoom towards and away from the centre position |
| Comma | Rotate camera left |
||  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Full stop | Rotate camera right |
| Backspace | Flip the camera to look from the other side of the centre position |

When a time-limited trial license is present the following window is shown periodically while the software verifies the trial duration:
  
*(Note*: This window is only shown for trial licenses. When a purchased license is present this trial validation window does not appear.)

[Screenshot: license-trial-dlg1.png - Time Limited Trial window](images/license-trial-dlg1.png)

[Screenshot: license-trial-dlg.png - Time Limited Trial window](images/license-trial-dlg.png)

*Note*: To validate a trial license your computer must have access to the internet.
If the software is unable to access the internet the following status will be shown:

[Screenshot: license-trial-dlg2.png - Time Limited Trial window](images/license-trial-dlg2.png)

If you see this when your computer is connected to the internet then it may be that you are using an anti-virus/security/firewall system that routinely blocks internet access to new software. In this case you will need to configure your anti-virus/security/firewall system to permit *out-going* connections for this software. If you are unsure how to do this then you may need to consult with your system administrator. [Minimally, ports 21, 80 and 123 must be permitted for out-going connections]

When initially purchased, the software license comes with one year's support and maintenace included.
After the initial year, you should purchase annual support and maintenance cover from your reseller.

The support and maintenance entitles you to contact the reseller for advice on using the software, and you also get software updates as they become released.

The software automatically determines your current support and maintenace coverage period over the internet.
If your computer is not connected to the internet, or you want to check the coverage period, you can click the *Support Period...* option on the *Help* menu.

[Screenshot: support-key-menu.png - Support Peiod option on the Help menu](images/support-key-menu.png)

The following window is displayed, showing the current status of the support coverage:

[Screenshot: support-key-dlg.png - Support Period window](images/support-key-dlg.png)

### Support Key

The support key will normally have been obtained automatically by the software via the internet.
If your computer is not connected to the internet, then you should request your *Support Key* from your reseller, and it can be provided by phone or email. You can then enter the Support Key into the box on this window.

### Expired Support

If your support period has expired, the software will notify you of this, when you next start it, by showing a small window in the bottom right of the screen:

[Screenshot: support-expired-dlg.png](images/support-expired-dlg.png)

Clicking the button will show the online website page for the software version history.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('3D Controls Window');
show\_index();
//-->

The 3D controls window can be accessed by clicking the "Show 3D Controls" in the [View Menu](view-menu.htm).

In this window you have access to advanced controls for manipulating the 3D view.

[Screenshot: 3dcontrols.png - 3D controls window](images/3dcontrols.png)

#### Rotate

These buttons will rotate the view around the target point clockwise or anti-clockwise.

#### Look

These buttons rotate the target point around the view clockwise or anti-clockwise.

#### Close In

These buttons move the view toward or away from the target point whilst maintaining the same Z level.

#### Zoom

These buttons will move the view toward or away from the target, keeping the target position in the same place. This has the effect of zooming the target in or out.

#### Move

These buttons will move both the view and target position forward, backward, left, or right.

#### Height

Move the camera up or down along the Z axis.

#### Pitch

Rotates the view up or down vertically whilst keeping the camera position the same.

#### Options

The top button will flip the view position with the target position, below that is a button to set the current view position down to ground level.

#### Sensitivity

The sensitivity slider controls how much of an effect all of the above buttons have when pressed, for example when pressing the button to move forward the sensitivity will affect how far the camera moves.

#### Rotate Increment

Here there is a text box where you can input an angle that you want to rotate, in degrees, around the target point. The view will rotate around the target point clockwise or anti-clockwise by the specified amount.

#### Vertical Angle

This section behaves the same way as the "Rotate Increment" section, but on the vertical plane.

  

---

* [View Menu](view-menu.htm)

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Model Scale');
show\_index();
//-->

This option is for scaling imported layouts/surveys or other sub-models in the project.

To get the Scale option click the **Surface** menu and select **Scale**. You will see this window:

[Screenshot: model-scale-dlg.png - Model Scale Window](images/model-scale-dlg.png)

### Model to Scale

This shows the list of available sub-models.
A sub-model is created when you load or import a drawing.
You must select at least one sub-model.

You may select multiple models to scale. To do this, press and hold the *Ctrl* key on the keyboard while clicking each item you wish to select.
You can also de-select items this way by clicking on a selected item whilst holding the *Ctrl* key.
To select all models, simply click the *Select All* button.

### Scale By

Enter the desired scale factors for each of the X, Y and Z axes.

*Note*: By default the Z (vertical) axis is not scaled. You must select the **Scale Z** check-box to enter the Z-axis scale.

*Note*: To flatten a model to 2D, select **Scale Z** and enter 0.0 for the **Z** axis scale.

#### From mm

The *From mm* button can be clicked to pre-set the scale values for the purpose of converting a model from millimetres to metres.

This can be useful after loading a drawing that has been prepared in units of millimetres in another software application (for example, architects often work in millimetres).
Site3D always works in units of metres when metric units are in operation, so this options scales everything down by a factor of one thousand.

#### From Feet

The *From Feet* button can be clicked to pre-set the scale values for the purpose of converting a model from feet to metres.

This can be useful after loading a drawing that has been prepared in units of feet in another software application (for example, engineers in the USA usually work in feet).
Site3D always works in units of metres when metric units are in operation, so this options scales everything down by the feet-to-metres conversion factor.

### Origin of Scale

This is the point around which the selected drawings will be scaled.
You can enter the X, Y and Z values of the coordinate,
or, you can press the 'Pick...' button and choose it from the drawing.
Once chosen, it will return to this window with the X, Y and Z values pre-filled.

To get the Rotate option click the **Surface** menu and select **Rotate**. You will see this window:

[Screenshot: model-rotate-dlg.png - Model Rotate Window](images/model-rotate-dlg.png)

### Model to Rotate

This shows the list of available sub-models and the design model.
A sub-model is created when you load or import a drawing.
You must select at least one model.

You may select multiple models to rotate. To do this, press and hold the *Ctrl* key on the keyboard while clicking each item you wish to select.
You can also de-select items this way by clicking on a selected item whilst holding the *Ctrl* key.
To select all models, simply click the *Select All* button.

### Origin

This is the point around which the selected models will be rotated.
You can enter the X, Y and Z values of the coordinate,
or, you can press the 'Pick...' button and choose it from the drawing.
Once chosen, it will return to this window with the X, Y and Z values pre-filled.

### Angle

Enter the required rotation angle in degrees.

If you are unsure of the exact angle to rotate by, click the 'Pick...' button and select the 'from' and
'to' points. The subtended angle is calculate between these two points and the origin, and the rotate window will return with the angle pre-filled.

*Note*: A positive value means anti-clockwise rotation and a negative value means clockwise rotation.

When you click 'OK' the selected models will be rotated by the specified angle.

This facility allows you to split a road into two at a selected chainage.

This is particularly useful if you need to raise or lower an entire road by a specified amount.

This facility can be used at any time during the design process.
Site3D will work out all the knock-on effects and update your site accordingly.

To use the centreline split facility click the **Road** menu at the top of the screen and select the **Split...** option.

[Screenshot: centreline-menu-split.png - Road menu showing Split selection](images/centreline-menu-split.png)

The mouse will change to a chainage tracker and highlights the centreline that will be selected when you click.

The following window will appear after you have selected a centreline.

[Screenshot: centreline-split.png - Centreline Split window](images/centreline-split.png)

### Centreline

This lets you select which centreline to do the operation on. By default this will be the centreline that was initially selected.

### Chainage

Here you can specify the exact point along the road centreline where the road is split into two.

### Splitting a road

Clicking the **OK** button will apply the split in the road. This will result in two separate roads that can be edited independently, with the same design before and after the split as the previous single road design.

[Screenshot: centreline-split-ex.png - Centreline Split Example](images/centreline-split-ex.png)

//<!--
show\_header('Centreline Translate');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility allows you to precisely translate a road by a specified amount on the X, Y, and Z axes.

This is particularly useful if you need to raise or lower an entire road by a specified amount.

*Note*: If the road it junctioned at either end, the level at the junctioned end will not change because the level is tied into the other road. Only the vertical IP levels will change in this case. If the road has no vertical IPs (i.e. it is a straight grade between two junctions) then its level will not change at all.

This facility can be used at any time during the design process.
Site3D will work out all the knock-on effects and update your site accordingly.

To use the centreline translate facility click the **Road** menu at the top of the screen and select the **Translate...** option.

[Screenshot: centreline-menu-translate.png - Road menu showing Translate selection](images/centreline-menu-translate.png)

The mouse will highlight the centreline that will be selected when you click.

The following window will appear after you have selected a centreline.

[Screenshot: centreline-translate.png - Centreline Translate window](images/centreline-translate.png)

### Centreline

This lets you select which centreline to do the translation operation on. By default this will be the centreline that was initially selected.

### Translation Amounts

Here you can set how much to translate the centreline from its original position.

### Create a Copy

By default, with **create a copy** unticked, the operation will move the selected centreline parallel by the chosen distance.

By choosing the **create a copy** option, the selected centreline will be unchanged and a parallel copy will be created at the
specified distance.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Gully Properties');
show\_index();
//-->

This window has settings for the general properties of for gully placement and connection to the drainage system.

On the [Icon: Drainage button](images/drainage-button.png) **Drainage Tools** toolbar, click the [Icon: Auto Gully Properties button](images/tool-buttons/auto-gully-properties-btn.png) **Auto Gullies properties** button.

The Auto Gullies properties window is shown.

[Screenshot: auto-gully-properties-dlg.png - Auto Gully Properties Window](images/auto-gully-properties-dlg.png)

### Visible

Tick this option to show the gullies in the plan and 3D views, or untick to hide the gullies. This option is the same as toggling the *DRAINAGE\_GULLIES* layer in the **Layers Window**.

### Drainable Area

This shows the default drainable area used for gully spacing.

The gully drainable area can be specified per-road by using the Centreline Properties tool and selecting a road. See the [centreline properties](centreline-properties.htm) help page for more information.

### Minimum Spacing

This is the horizontal distance between consecutive gullies along a channel line and stops gullies being automatically placed too close to another.

### Force Double Gullys At Low Points

Auto gullies will by default put double gullies at low points. Unticking this option will change all low points to a singular gully.

### Connection Angle

This is the subtended angle in degrees between the gully conduit and the sewer pipe it connects to. The default is 45 degrees but if you wanted the gullies to connect at right angles then you would change this angle to 90 degrees. This value is ignored if the gully conduit connects to a manhole.

### Max Conduit Length

Auto gullies will only connect to the sewer network if the point it would connect to is within this distance of the gully. This distance defaults to 15m and it sets the threshold at which it will not look any further to connect.

### Conduit Depth to Soffit

The outgoing conduit from the gully pot is not at the invert of the gully but at a specific level under the road surface to maintain reasonable structural cover above the pipe. The values defaults to 0.6m (600mm) and changing this value will either make the head of the gully conduit shallower or deeper under the road.

### Min Conduit Grade

When a gully connects to the sewer system, the gully conduit will check to see if the gradient of the gully conduit is steep enough so as not to silt up or even flow in the wrong direction. The value defaults to 1 in 100 gradient, but please note the gully conduit will likely be much steeper than this gradient as it simply denotes the slackest gradient that is allowed before the gully will fail to connect to the sewer system.

### Conduit Diameter

This is the diameter in meters of the conduit that connects a gully to the sewer system and defaults to a 0.15m (150mm) diameter pipe.

https://www.site3d.co.uk/help/drainage-r:
Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Manhole Renumbering Tool');
show\_index();
//-->

This facility renames the manholes on the Storm or Foul networks according to a standard numbering convention.

Run 1 is considered to be the longest run from the outfall (longest in terms of distance along the network, rather than number of manholes). The manhole at the head of Run 1 is then named S1 (for storm networks) or F1 (for foul networks).

The next manhole down is named S2 (or F2), then S3, etc., until it comes to a branch off Run 1. This branch is considered to be Run 2. The head manhole of Run 2 then gets named with the next number, and then the numbering proceeds down Run 2 (splitting out for any branches off Run 2) until it arrives back at Run 1.
The numbering then continues down Run 1 to the next branch, and so until until the outfall is reached.

To use the facility, select the **Drainage->Renumber Manhole Network...** menu option.

[Screenshot: drainage-renumber-menu.png - Manhole Renumbering Tool Menu](images/drainage-renumber-menu.png)

The following window is shown, with choices for which network to work on and how it should renumber the manholes:

[Screenshot: drainage-renumber-preferences-dlg.png - Manhole renumbering dialog](images/drainage-renumber-preferences-dlg.png)

### Look For Manholes

## On Network

Select to renumber either the **Storm** or **Foul** networks. The *Prefix* items will change to **S** or **F** accordingly.

## Numbers Prefixed

If this option selected, the renumbering will be limited to only the manholes which are currently prefixed with the specified letter(s) followed by a number (such as S2 or F12).

For example, you can limit the renumbering to only those manholes which are currently prefixed with the letter S followed by a number, i.e. S1, S2 ,S13, S26 etc. It will ignore other manholes which have names such as 'Headwall' or 'Storm Drain'.

## In Phase

If this option is selected, it will limit the renumbering to only those manholes in the given drainage phase.

*Note*: The drainage phase can be specified on the [manhole properties](manhole-properties.htm) window.

### Renumber To

## New Prefix

This will be the new prefix used to rename those manholes that match the filters set above. By default it is 'S' for storm networks and 'F' for foul, but can be set to anything you want.

## Starting Number

This is the starting number used when renumbering the system. By default this is set to 1, eg S1, S2, S3 etc. for a storm network. It can be changed to any number.

For example, you may wish to renumber each phase with different number sets. Eg, phase 1 starting at S101, Phase 2 starting at S201 etc.

* [Manhole Properties](manhole-properties.htm)

With the Roundabout Edit Tool you can pick a roundabout design feature from the plan view to quickly edit a specific element of the roundabout.

To access the roundabout editing tool, first click the Roundabout Tools button [Icon: Roundabout Tools button](images/roundabout-toolbar-button.png) and then the Roundabout Edit Tool [Icon: Edit Roundabout button](images/tool-buttons/roundabout-edit.png) button.

When the tool is active, a small white circle is drawn next to the roundabout design elements that can be modified in the plan view, as shown below:

[Screenshot: roundabout-edit-tool-ex1.png - Roundabout Editable Points](images/roundabout-edit-tool-ex1.png)

All of these elements can also be edited from the [Roundabout Junction Edit](roundabout-junction-edit.htm) window, which also includes additional options such as entry and exit junction fillet style.

Below is a close-up of a single roundabout arm with added labels:

[Screenshot: roundabout-edit-tool-ex2.png - Roundabout Edit Tool Labelled Example](images/roundabout-edit-tool-ex2.png)

Each of the labelled points edits a different property. Clicking on the point will bring up a window where you can see and edit the associated property.

### Junction Fillet Radius

**A:** Exit fillet radius.

**B:** Entry fillet radius.

[Screenshot: roundabout-edit-junction-radius.png - Roundabout Arm Junction Fillet Radius](images/roundabout-edit-junction-radius.png)

### Junction Entry and Exit Lane Widths

**C:** Exit lane width

**D:** Entry lane width

[Screenshot: roundabout-edit-junction-width.png - Roundabout Arm Junction Fillet Radius](images/roundabout-edit-junction-width.png)

### Splitter Island Exit Lane Style

**E:** Exit style

The default "Parallel" style will generate a splitter island exit line that is parallel to the channelline, offset by the exit lane width. You can swap to the "Tangential" style, which will then generate in the same was as the entry side. More information on the splitter island generation can be found in the splitter island section of the [roundabout junction edit help page](roundabout-junction-edit.htm).

[Screenshot: roundabout-edit-exit-island-style.png - Roundabout Arm Junction Fillet Radius](images/roundabout-edit-exit-island-style.png)

### Splitter Island Corner Fillets

**F:** Exit corner fillet radius.

**G:** Entry corner fillet radius.

**H:** Rear corner fillet radius.

A radius of -1m for any of these fillets will result in an automatic radius fillet being applied.

[Screenshot: roundabout-edit-splitter-island-radius.png - Roundabout Arm Junction Fillet Radius](images/roundabout-edit-splitter-island-radius.png)

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Greenfield Runoff');
show\_index();
//-->

Calculate the greenfield runoff rate of a proposed site based on the soil type and hydrological region. This provides an estimation of Qbar calculated using the IH124 method. The Qbar values represents the peak flow rate of rainfall over an area of land for a 1 year return period storm.

### Greenfield Runoff

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) drainage auto-sizing-levelling and simulation toolbar,
click the [Icon: Greenfield Runoff button](images/tool-buttons/greenfield-runoff.png) **Greenfield runoff** button.

After clicking on the **Greenfield runoff** button you will see the following window appear:

[Screenshot: greenfield-runoff-dlg.png - Greenfield Runoff Window](images/greenfield-runoff-dlg.png)

#### Site Details

If you know the plan surface area of your site, you can type in the value in the **Total Area** box. If you don't know the area, you can calculate it by clicking the **Pick...** button and digitise a site boundary line. After you have drawn the site boundary line, right click and choose **Finish** to return back to the Quick Storage Estimate window.

#### Hydrological region and soil type

These values are determined based on the site coordinates, clicking a point on the UK map will update these values based on the new location. Or you can manually specify them by clicking in the boxes and changing the values.

The **Hydrological** region index is from FSSR 14 and is in the range [1 to 13]. The **Soil Type** is the WRAP map of SOIL type from the Wallingford Procedure and is in the range [1 to 5]. The Standard Annual Average Rainfall (SAAR) is the average annual rainfall in mm, usually obtained from Flood Studies Report.

#### Greenfield Runoff Rate

This value is automatically calculated based on the site details and hydrological region and soil type values.

## Resetting Site Location

If you manually click a new location on plan, the **Reset Position to Site Extent** button will appear at the bottom. If clicked, this will reset the position on the map to the current site position.

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Quick Storage Estimate');
show\_index();
//-->

Calculate the storage estimate of a proposed site by running a SWMM drainage simulation on a representation of the site. The site is simulated as a single large catchment area that flows at a restricted rate to an outfall. The volume of water that would have flooded the site is the volume of estimated volume of storage required.

### Quick Storage Estimate

On the [Icon: Drainage Tools button](images/levelling-sizing-tools-button.png) drainage auto-sizing-levelling and simulation toolbar,
click the [Icon: Quick Storage Calculation button](images/tool-buttons/quick-storage-calc.png) **Quick Storage Estimate...** button.

After clicking on the **Quick Storage Estimate...** button you will see the following window appear:

[Screenshot: quick-storage-estimate-dlg.png - Quick Storage Estimate Window](images/quick-storage-estimate-dlg.png)

#### Storm Type

The rainfall data for the storm simulations can be specified using either FSR data (Flood Studies report, M5-60 Ratio R) or FEH99/FEH13 data (Flood Estimation Handbook).

#### Site Details

If you know the plan surface are of your site, you can type in the value in the **Total Area** box. If you don't know the area, you can calculate it by clicking the **Pick...** button and digitise a site boundary line. After you have drawn the site boundary line, right click and choose **Finish** to return back to the Quick Storage Estimate window.

#### Storage Estimate

The Discharge Rate specifies the rate that water flows to an outfall. You can manually input this or use the value generated from the greenfield runoff calculation. This value can be calculated by going to the [Greenfield Runoff](drainage-greenfield-runoff.htm) window.

If the Discharge Rate is lower than the **Minimum Discharge Rate**, the **Minimum Discharge Rate** value will be used in preference to calculate the storage estimate instead.

The **% Impermeable** (PIMP) value is a percentage of how much of the **Total Area** will be impermeable, like roads, house roofs and driveways.

The **Climate Change %** is the projected increase in rainfall intensity and **Urban Creep %** is the projected increase in impermeable area over the lifetime of the site.

Click the **Calculate...** button to use the values entered above to see a window showing the estimated storage requirements for your site.

//<!--
show\_header('Plot Boundary Properties');
show\_index();
//-->

The plot boundary properties window allows you to set the display properties for [plot boundary](plot-boundary.htm) annotations.

The Plot Boundary Properties window is accessed by first clicking on the [plot boundary tools](plot-boundary.htm) button [Icon: Plot Boundary Tools button](images/plot-boundary-tools-button.png)
and then clicking on the Properties button [Icon: Plot Boundary Properties button](images/tool-buttons/centreline-properties.png).

  
  
[Screenshot: plot-boundary-properties-dlg.png - Plot Boundary Properties window](images/plot-boundary-properties-dlg.png)

### Gravel Board and Retaining Wall Height Requirements

A plot boundary line can either show no boundary line annotation, a gravel board annotation, or retaining wall annotation.

The annotation shown is based on the *maximum* level difference between two sides of a plot boundary line.

The line will have no annotation if there is no level difference or the level difference doesn't meet the below criteria.

The line will annotate as a gravel board if the level difference is greater than the value for **Gravel Board over**.

The line will annotate as a retaining wall if the level difference is greater than the value for **Retaining Wall over**.

### Annotation

The gravel board/retaining wall annotation is comprised of a prefix, the level difference, and a suffix.

Using the default settings, a level difference of 450mm will use the retaining wall annotation settings as it is over the default 300mm level difference. Below are some examples of different retaining wall prefix/suffix/level difference combinations:

[Screenshot: plot-boundary-annotation-ex1.png - Plot Boundary Annotation Example](images/plot-boundary-annotation-ex1.png)
Prefix: "RTW"  
Level Difference: **on**  
Suffix: ""  
(Default Annotation Settings)  
[Screenshot: plot-boundary-annotation-ex2.png - Plot Boundary Annotation Example](images/plot-boundary-annotation-ex2.png)
Prefix: ""  
Level Difference: **on**

# Intuitive Civil Engineering Design Software Designed By Engineers For Engineers

## Road Networks Made Easy

Site3D is capable of handling both complex road networks with varying width roads as well as major highways, link roads and bypasses. Site3D's automatic junctioning makes linking roads a breeze.

[Screenshot: laptop-plan\_road\_network.png - Complex Road Networks](images/laptop-plan\_road\_network.png)

[Screenshot: laptop-isopachyte.png - Isopachyte Colouring](images/laptop-isopachyte.png)

## Volume Calculation and Isopachyte bands

One click formation surface generation with instant cut and fill volume calculation and automatic isopachyte depth banding at the touch of a button.

## BIM

Easily integrate Site3D into your BIM collaboration system by simply exporting the design in the BIM IFC file format. The IFC file loads straight into your BIM collaboration software of choice (e.g. Revit / Navisworks), including layer naming which follows the BS1192:2007 guidelines, and solid objects with their design attribute information.

[Screenshot: laptop-bim.png - BIM Model](images/laptop-bim.png)

[Screenshot: laptop-3d-junction.png - 3D Junction](images/laptop-3d-junction.png)

## 3D Junctions

Junctions automatically calculate the optimal vertical kerb-return design to tie the roads together both horizontally and vertically. Whenever design changes are made, the intelligent 3D junctions automatically update to maintain the specified engineering criteria.

## 3D Roundabouts

Create fully 3D roundabouts in seconds that use the DMRB guidelines. Vehicle driving deflection is calculated automatically so that it is always up-to-date after any design change. The automatic model recalculation means you can simply drag junctions around in seconds and observe the effects of the changes to allow for optimal roundabout design.

[Screenshot: laptop-3d-roundabout.png - 3D Roundabout](images/laptop-3d-roundabout.png)

[Screenshot: laptop-drainage.png - Drainage](images/laptop-drainage.png)

## Drainage

Create drainage networks by placing manholes along the desired runs, then let Site3D auto-size and level the network according the surface cover levels, drainable areas and rainfall profile. Drainage is automatically displayed and annotated on long-sections and instantly updated to show changes. Drainage simulations are run directly in Site3D, or export and import with your favourite drainage package.

## Earthworks

Create varying interface grades from the edge of your designs to a ground model of your choice. Stepped grades can be easily added for utility strips and other linear design features along the side of roads like swales.

[Screenshot: laptop-interface\_3d.png - Earthworks](images/laptop-interface\_3d.png)

[Screenshot: laptop-pond\_3d.png - Ponds](images/laptop-pond\_3d.png)

## Ponds

Ponds can be easily designed in Site3D. Dynamic cut and fill volumes, as well as water storage volumes, update as you define the pond shape and profile, allowing you to tailor the design to the job specification in minutes.

## One-click 2D to 3D Converter

Site3D reads every level text and automatically and intelligently finds the appropriate insertion point. Site3D also assigns levels to the 2D feature lines to form an accurate surface that has surface features well defined in the converted 3D surface.

[Screenshot: laptop-2d\_to\_3d.png - 2D to 3D Converter](images/laptop-2d\_to\_3d.png)

[Screenshot: laptop-setting\_out.png - Setting-out](images/laptop-setting\_out.png)

## Dynamic Setting Out

Site3D automatically updates the annotated coordinate and setting-out tables whenever the design changes in any way. This means the setting-out information is always up-to-date.

[Download and try for yourself](download.htm)

[Request an unrestricted trial](trial-form.htm)

[Pricing and buy now](buy.htm)

[View case studies](case-studies.htm)

Site3D - Road and Site Design

//<!--
show\_header('Vortex Flow Control Properties');
show\_index();
//-->

This option shows the properties of a vortex flow control and allows you to modify them.

On the **Manhole Properties** window, click the **Add...** flow control button and choose a vortex control like Hydro-Brake® Optimum, QBrake or StormBrake™, or select a vortex flow control from the flow control list and click the **Edit...** button to show the following vortex properties window.

[Screenshot: vortex-flow-control-dlg.png - Vortex Flow Control window](images/vortex-flow-control-dlg.png)

### Invert Offset

Vertical offset of the flow controls invert level above the manholes invert level.

### Vortex Type

Choosing one of the vortex flow control types from this drop down list will swap the current vortex control to the newly selected type. In doing so, the performance curve and results information will automatically update.

### Design Head

Enter the depth of water (head) above the invert of the flow control that you wish to optimise this vortex control for.

### Design Flow

Enter the maximum discharge rate that is likely to flow through this vortex flow control.

  

## Hydro-Brake® Optimum specific values

The following two options are only enabled when a Hydro-Brake® Optimum is selected:

### Sump Available

Tick this option if there is room for a sump below the Hydro-Brake® Optimum flow control.

### Objective

Choose the method to optimise the hydraulic characteristic curve (response curve)

* **Minimise storage requirements**: Passes forwards as much flow as possible early in a storm event, in order to minimise upstream storage.
* **Minimise blockage risk**: Makes the inlet and outlet openings as large as possible.
* **Future proof**: Adds an adjustable inlet, set to its mid-point for maximum flexibility for adjusting design flows post installation.
* **Linear discharge profile**: Generates a discharge rate as close as possible to linear, providing similar hydraulic performance as an orifice place, but with internal clearances approximately 2-4 times larger.

  

## Results

After the vortex flow control has automatically recalculated itself, the following results will be updated:

* **Reference**: Unique identifier/model number that the manufacturer might refer to when constructing this flow control.
* **Body Width**: Physical Width/Span of flow control device. If 0.0, then this value is not provided by the manufacturers calculations.
* **Body Depth**: Physical Depth/Thickness away from the manhole side wall of flow control device. If 0.0, then this value is not provided by the manufacturers calculations.
* **Min Outlet Diameter**: Minimum outlet/pipe diameter that can be used with the current vortex design.
* **Flush Flow Head**: Head of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control transforms from standard flow to vortex flow.
* **Flush Flow**: Flow rate of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control transforms from standard flow to vortex flow.
* **Kickback Head**: Head of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control produces a steady vortex.
* **Kickback Flow**: Flow rate of fluid at the point where the vortex flow control produces a steady vortex.
* **Min Manhole Diameter**: Minimum manhole diameter that can be used with this flow control design.
* **Min Sump**: Minimum required sump beneath this flow control for installation and usage. Please check with manufacturer for final install sump requirements.

### Performance Curve Preview

This window shows the depth to flow performance curve of the designed flow control. You can right click over this preview and choose **Copy Points to Clipboard** if you would like to export the performance curve depth/flow points to a spreadsheet, CSV or another system.
